title: Canary telemetry contract and validation for LLM variant rollouts
preview:
Summary
--------
This document defines a minimal, testable telemetry contract and a step-by-step validation runbook for canarying LLM model variants within Redwood’s serving platform. It provides precise metrics to collect, dashboard and alert templates, acceptance thresholds for automated promotion, and operator procedures for manual validation and rollback. The goal is to standardize canary rollouts so product and platform teams can safely verify model quality, latency and cost signals before broad promotion.

Scope
-----
- Applies to model variant rollouts across Hosted, Dedicated and Private deployments.
- Focuses on platform-observable signals (latency, error surface, token cost) and model-output quality signals surfaced via evaluation webhooks / retriever metrics.
- Does NOT replace domain-specific human-eval gating for regulated customers.

Key principles
--------------
- Observable: every canary must emit the same set of telemetry as baseline traffic.
- Testable: acceptance criteria should be automatable and expressed as PromQL/SQL where possible.
- Incremental: rollouts progress through small traffic ramps with hard safety gates.
- Reproducible: synthetic tests used in the canary must be runnable in CI.

Telemetry contract (required fields)
----------------------------------
All variants participating in a canary must export the following signals at request granularity and aggregated windows (1m, 5m, 1h):

1) platform.latency.p95_ms (ms) — server-side end-to-end latency p95 (including queueing).
2) platform.latency.p99_ms (ms) — server-side p99 latency.
3) platform.token_cost.per_request (float) — estimated token cost per request normalized to baseline model units.
4) platform.requests.error_rate (ratio) — fraction of requests that returned non-OK HTTP or LLM internal error codes.
5) platform.kv_cache.miss_rate (ratio) — fraction of KV cache misses for requests that include a cacheable prefix.
6) model.quality.delta_ranked_score (float) — delta of model quality score vs baseline on the canary prompt set (higher is better).
7) model.output.nudges (int) — counted triggered quality guardrail nudges (e.g., hallucination detectors, toxicity filters).
8) user.visible.regression_flags (int) — number of consumer-reported regressions annotated via support-webhook within the window.

Optional but recommended:
- platform.cpu_gpu_utilization (percent)
- platform.batch_size.avg
- model.kv_cache.evictions_per_minute

Metric tags/labels required
--------------------------
- model_name, model_variant, deployment_region, customer_tier, canary_group_id, route_id, rollout_wave

Dashboard & panels (minimum set)
-------------------------------
Create a conjoined dashboard with these panels:
- Top row: Total requests (1m), Canary traffic % (system-sourced), Baseline traffic %
- Latency heatmap: p50/p95/p99 split by variant and route_id (time-series + percentile bands)
- Cost panel: token_cost per request vs baseline with rolling 1h trend and projection of monthly spend delta
- Error surface: error_rate by HTTP status and LLM internal code; stack by route_id
- Quality delta: model.quality.delta_ranked_score for canary prompts with 90% CI error bars
- Guardrail events: model.output.nudges and user.visible.regression_flags as spikes/annotations
- KV cache metrics: miss_rate and evictions

Example PromQL queries
----------------------
- Canary traffic percent (1m):
  sum(rate(requests_total{rollout_wave=~"canary-.*",model_variant=~"canary.*"}[1m])) / sum(rate(requests_total[1m])) * 100

- p95 latency (canary, 5m):
  histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{model_variant="canary-v1"}[5m])) by (le)) * 1000

- error rate (5m):
  sum(rate(requests_failed_total{model_variant="canary-v1"}[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total{model_variant="canary-v1"}[5m]))

Alerting rules (suggested thresholds)
------------------------------------
- Alert: Canary: latency regression (P95)
  - Condition: p95_canary > p95_baseline * 1.30 for 5m
  - Severity: P1 (page)
  - Runbook: follow "Canary validation runbook -> Immediate rollback" steps below

- Alert: Canary: error spike
  - Condition: error_rate_canary > max(0.01, error_rate_baseline * 2) for 3m
  - Severity: P1 (page)

- Alert: Canary: quality regression (automated gate)
  - Condition: model.quality.delta_ranked_score < -0.03 over 1h (relative to baseline)
  - Severity: P0 (auto-fail - stop promotion)

- Alert: Canary: cost blowout
  - Condition: token_cost_per_request_canary > token_cost_per_request_baseline * 1.50 for 30m
  - Severity: P2 (notify)

Canary validation runbook (operator steps)
-----------------------------------------
Preconditions: baseline_health = green, canary traffic source verified (synthetic + live split), monitoring panels open.

1) Kickoff
   - Owner: release engineer. Create rollout_wave id: canary-YYYYMMDD-<short> and tag the deployment.
   - Note the baseline model_variant and traffic percentage to divert.

2) Synthetic smoke (0% live traffic)
   - Run the synthetic canary suite (CI job) against canary variant; expected: all synthetic asserts pass; no regression score delta > -2%
   - Command: curl -X POST https://ci.redwood/runner/run -d '{"suite":"canary-smoke","model_variant":"canary-v1"}'

3) Small live ramp (1-2%) — duration 15m
   - Observe latency p95/p99, error rate, guardrail nudges.
   - If alerts fire or model.quality.delta_ranked_score < -0.02, abort and rollback.

4) Medium ramp (5-10%) — duration 30m
   - Run fault-injection synthetic queries (long-form, edge-case prompts) from lab traffic group.
   - Validate no user.visible.regression_flags and no sustained latency deltas > 20%.

5) Full validation window (1–4 hours)
   - If all automations pass, schedule manual sampling session: product engineer reviews 50 random canary responses across different routes (template below).
   - If manual review passes and all gates green, programmatically promote to next wave or full traffic.

Acceptance criteria (automatable)
--------------------------------
- p95_canary <= p95_baseline * 1.15 over 1h
- p99_canary <= p99_baseline * 1.25 over 1h
- error_rate_canary <= max(0.01, error_rate_baseline * 1.5)
- model.quality.delta_ranked_score >= -0.02 over canary prompts (1h window)
- token_cost_per_request_canary <= token_cost_per_request_baseline * 1.25
- no unacknowledged P1/P0 alerts for the canary group

Manual sampling template (50 responses)
-------------------------------------
For each sampled response capture: request_id, route_id, prompt_snippet, baseline_response_summary, canary_response_summary, quality_rating(0-5), notes.

Rollback & mitigation
---------------------
Immediate rollback condition (auto-fail): any P0 alert or manual identification of a safety regression (e.g., hallucination causing customer-facing incorrect instruction).

Rollback steps:
1) Trigger routing rollback: set rollout_wave traffic to baseline using the orchestration CLI: redwoodctl rollout set --wave <wave-id> --variant baseline --percent 100
2) Trigger infra-scale-down for canary replicas: redwoodctl service scale --service <svc> --variant canary-v1 --replicas 0
3) Annotate the rollout and create incident ticket with labels: canary-failure, model-variant, <wave-id>
4) Start triage: run automated diff on canary vs baseline outputs on the canary prompt set and attach to ticket

Postmortem & retrospective triggers
-----------------------------------
Open a lightweight retrospective if any of the following occur:
- rollback executed
- any P1/P0 alert fired during canary
- user.visible.regression_flags > 5 within 24h of rollout

Runbook owner & contacts
------------------------
- Runbook owner: Aisha Patel (Platform SRE lead)
- Primary on-call: Platform shift rotation (pager duty)
- Product reviewer: Marco Ruiz

CI / test harness contract
-------------------------
- Canary synthetic suite must live in repo: infra/canary-tests/ and expose a /canary-health endpoint that returns JSON with fields: "pass_count", "failures", "score_delta".
- CI job name: canary-validate (pipeline tag: canary.validate).

Change coordination checklist (pre-rollout)
-----------------------------------------
- [ ] Confirm telemetry tags present for new variant
- [ ] Dashboards instantiated with model_variant filter
- [ ] Canary group id created and synthetic suite attached
- [ ] Stakeholders notified (platform, product, customer-facing eng)

Operator tips and anti-patterns
------------------------------
- Do not rely solely on overall error_rate; inspect error taxonomy (LLM runtime vs HTTP vs downstream integration).
- Watch for transient cold-start spikes — ensure warmup sequences are included in the synthetic suite.
- If token_cost delta is high but quality improves, escalate to product-owner tradeoff discussion rather than immediate rollback.

Appendix: Example alert rule (Grafana/Prometheus alertmanager style)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
groups:
- name: canary.rules
  rules:
  - alert: CanaryLatencyRegression
    expr: (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{model_variant=~"canary.*"}[5m])) by (le)) > on() histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{model_variant=~"baseline.*"}[5m])) by (le)) * 1.30)
    for: 5m
    labels:
      severity: page
    annotations:
      summary: "Canary p95 latency regression detected for {{ $labels.model_variant }}"

Appendix: Example manual review checklist
----------------------------------------
- 50 responses reviewed across routes: pass threshold 90% (quality_rating >= 3)
- No safety-flagged outputs (guardrail nudges) in sampled set
- At least 10 coverage items from the edge-case prompt list

Revision history
----------------
- 2025-11-12: created by Aisha Patel (initial draft)
- 2026-01-20: added automated gate thresholds and PromQL examples (reviewers: Marco Ruiz)
- 2026-02-03: finalized dashboard panel layout and rollback CLI snippets (published)

Related documents
-----------------
- Canary orchestration DSL guide (internal)
- Model variant fallback misfire investigation runbook
- Synthetic canary test generator repo README

Contact & feedback
------------------
For questions or suggested improvements, ping @aisha on Slack or file a doc-edit PR against the confluence source.
text:
Summary
--------
This document defines a minimal, testable telemetry contract and a step-by-step validation runbook for canarying LLM model variants within Redwood’s serving platform. It provides precise metrics to collect, dashboard and alert templates, acceptance thresholds for automated promotion, and operator procedures for manual validation and rollback. The goal is to standardize canary rollouts so product and platform teams can safely verify model quality, latency and cost signals before broad promotion.

Scope
-----
- Applies to model variant rollouts across Hosted, Dedicated and Private deployments.
- Focuses on platform-observable signals (latency, error surface, token cost) and model-output quality signals surfaced via evaluation webhooks / retriever metrics.
- Does NOT replace domain-specific human-eval gating for regulated customers.

Key principles
--------------
- Observable: every canary must emit the same set of telemetry as baseline traffic.
- Testable: acceptance criteria should be automatable and expressed as PromQL/SQL where possible.
- Incremental: rollouts progress through small traffic ramps with hard safety gates.
- Reproducible: synthetic tests used in the canary must be runnable in CI.

Telemetry contract (required fields)
----------------------------------
All variants participating in a canary must export the following signals at request granularity and aggregated windows (1m, 5m, 1h):

1) platform.latency.p95_ms (ms) — server-side end-to-end latency p95 (including queueing).
2) platform.latency.p99_ms (ms) — server-side p99 latency.
3) platform.token_cost.per_request (float) — estimated token cost per request normalized to baseline model units.
4) platform.requests.error_rate (ratio) — fraction of requests that returned non-OK HTTP or LLM internal error codes.
5) platform.kv_cache.miss_rate (ratio) — fraction of KV cache misses for requests that include a cacheable prefix.
6) model.quality.delta_ranked_score (float) — delta of model quality score vs baseline on the canary prompt set (higher is better).
7) model.output.nudges (int) — counted triggered quality guardrail nudges (e.g., hallucination detectors, toxicity filters).
8) user.visible.regression_flags (int) — number of consumer-reported regressions annotated via support-webhook within the window.

Optional but recommended:
- platform.cpu_gpu_utilization (percent)
- platform.batch_size.avg
- model.kv_cache.evictions_per_minute

Metric tags/labels required
--------------------------
- model_name, model_variant, deployment_region, customer_tier, canary_group_id, route_id, rollout_wave

Dashboard & panels (minimum set)
-------------------------------
Create a conjoined dashboard with these panels:
- Top row: Total requests (1m), Canary traffic % (system-sourced), Baseline traffic %
- Latency heatmap: p50/p95/p99 split by variant and route_id (time-series + percentile bands)
- Cost panel: token_cost per request vs baseline with rolling 1h trend and projection of monthly spend delta
- Error surface: error_rate by HTTP status and LLM internal code; stack by route_id
- Quality delta: model.quality.delta_ranked_score for canary prompts with 90% CI error bars
- Guardrail events: model.output.nudges and user.visible.regression_flags as spikes/annotations
- KV cache metrics: miss_rate and evictions

Example PromQL queries
----------------------
- Canary traffic percent (1m):
  sum(rate(requests_total{rollout_wave=~"canary-.*",model_variant=~"canary.*"}[1m])) / sum(rate(requests_total[1m])) * 100

- p95 latency (canary, 5m):
  histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{model_variant="canary-v1"}[5m])) by (le)) * 1000

- error rate (5m):
  sum(rate(requests_failed_total{model_variant="canary-v1"}[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total{model_variant="canary-v1"}[5m]))

Alerting rules (suggested thresholds)
------------------------------------
- Alert: Canary: latency regression (P95)
  - Condition: p95_canary > p95_baseline * 1.30 for 5m
  - Severity: P1 (page)
  - Runbook: follow "Canary validation runbook -> Immediate rollback" steps below

- Alert: Canary: error spike
  - Condition: error_rate_canary > max(0.01, error_rate_baseline * 2) for 3m
  - Severity: P1 (page)

- Alert: Canary: quality regression (automated gate)
  - Condition: model.quality.delta_ranked_score < -0.03 over 1h (relative to baseline)
  - Severity: P0 (auto-fail - stop promotion)

- Alert: Canary: cost blowout
  - Condition: token_cost_per_request_canary > token_cost_per_request_baseline * 1.50 for 30m
  - Severity: P2 (notify)

Canary validation runbook (operator steps)
-----------------------------------------
Preconditions: baseline_health = green, canary traffic source verified (synthetic + live split), monitoring panels open.

1) Kickoff
   - Owner: release engineer. Create rollout_wave id: canary-YYYYMMDD-<short> and tag the deployment.
   - Note the baseline model_variant and traffic percentage to divert.

2) Synthetic smoke (0% live traffic)
   - Run the synthetic canary suite (CI job) against canary variant; expected: all synthetic asserts pass; no regression score delta > -2%
   - Command: curl -X POST https://ci.redwood/runner/run -d '{"suite":"canary-smoke","model_variant":"canary-v1"}'

3) Small live ramp (1-2%) — duration 15m
   - Observe latency p95/p99, error rate, guardrail nudges.
   - If alerts fire or model.quality.delta_ranked_score < -0.02, abort and rollback.

4) Medium ramp (5-10%) — duration 30m
   - Run fault-injection synthetic queries (long-form, edge-case prompts) from lab traffic group.
   - Validate no user.visible.regression_flags and no sustained latency deltas > 20%.

5) Full validation window (1–4 hours)
   - If all automations pass, schedule manual sampling session: product engineer reviews 50 random canary responses across different routes (template below).
   - If manual review passes and all gates green, programmatically promote to next wave or full traffic.

Acceptance criteria (automatable)
--------------------------------
- p95_canary <= p95_baseline * 1.15 over 1h
- p99_canary <= p99_baseline * 1.25 over 1h
- error_rate_canary <= max(0.01, error_rate_baseline * 1.5)
- model.quality.delta_ranked_score >= -0.02 over canary prompts (1h window)
- token_cost_per_request_canary <= token_cost_per_request_baseline * 1.25
- no unacknowledged P1/P0 alerts for the canary group

Manual sampling template (50 responses)
-------------------------------------
For each sampled response capture: request_id, route_id, prompt_snippet, baseline_response_summary, canary_response_summary, quality_rating(0-5), notes.

Rollback & mitigation
---------------------
Immediate rollback condition (auto-fail): any P0 alert or manual identification of a safety regression (e.g., hallucination causing customer-facing incorrect instruction).

Rollback steps:
1) Trigger routing rollback: set rollout_wave traffic to baseline using the orchestration CLI: redwoodctl rollout set --wave <wave-id> --variant baseline --percent 100
2) Trigger infra-scale-down for canary replicas: redwoodctl service scale --service <svc> --variant canary-v1 --replicas 0
3) Annotate the rollout and create incident ticket with labels: canary-failure, model-variant, <wave-id>
4) Start triage: run automated diff on canary vs baseline outputs on the canary prompt set and attach to ticket

Postmortem & retrospective triggers
-----------------------------------
Open a lightweight retrospective if any of the following occur:
- rollback executed
- any P1/P0 alert fired during canary
- user.visible.regression_flags > 5 within 24h of rollout

Runbook owner & contacts
------------------------
- Runbook owner: Aisha Patel (Platform SRE lead)
- Primary on-call: Platform shift rotation (pager duty)
- Product reviewer: Marco Ruiz

CI / test harness contract
-------------------------
- Canary synthetic suite must live in repo: infra/canary-tests/ and expose a /canary-health endpoint that returns JSON with fields: "pass_count", "failures", "score_delta".
- CI job name: canary-validate (pipeline tag: canary.validate).

Change coordination checklist (pre-rollout)
-----------------------------------------
- [ ] Confirm telemetry tags present for new variant
- [ ] Dashboards instantiated with model_variant filter
- [ ] Canary group id created and synthetic suite attached
- [ ] Stakeholders notified (platform, product, customer-facing eng)

Operator tips and anti-patterns
------------------------------
- Do not rely solely on overall error_rate; inspect error taxonomy (LLM runtime vs HTTP vs downstream integration).
- Watch for transient cold-start spikes — ensure warmup sequences are included in the synthetic suite.
- If token_cost delta is high but quality improves, escalate to product-owner tradeoff discussion rather than immediate rollback.

Appendix: Example alert rule (Grafana/Prometheus alertmanager style)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
groups:
- name: canary.rules
  rules:
  - alert: CanaryLatencyRegression
    expr: (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{model_variant=~"canary.*"}[5m])) by (le)) > on() histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{model_variant=~"baseline.*"}[5m])) by (le)) * 1.30)
    for: 5m
    labels:
      severity: page
    annotations:
      summary: "Canary p95 latency regression detected for {{ $labels.model_variant }}"

Appendix: Example manual review checklist
----------------------------------------
- 50 responses reviewed across routes: pass threshold 90% (quality_rating >= 3)
- No safety-flagged outputs (guardrail nudges) in sampled set
- At least 10 coverage items from the edge-case prompt list

Revision history
----------------
- 2025-11-12: created by Aisha Patel (initial draft)
- 2026-01-20: added automated gate thresholds and PromQL examples (reviewers: Marco Ruiz)
- 2026-02-03: finalized dashboard panel layout and rollback CLI snippets (published)

Related documents
-----------------
- Canary orchestration DSL guide (internal)
- Model variant fallback misfire investigation runbook
- Synthetic canary test generator repo README

Contact & feedback
------------------
For questions or suggested improvements, ping @aisha on Slack or file a doc-edit PR against the confluence source.

title: Interservice Dependency Directory and Chaos-Preparation Playbook
preview:
Summary:

This document defines the authoritative interservice dependency directory for platform services, the classification system we use to prioritize mitigation actions, and an operational playbook to prepare for and respond to cascading failures. It is intended for platform engineers, oncall, SREs, and service owners.

Goals:
- Provide a single source of truth for upstream/downstream relationships and ownership.
- Standardize detection, alerting, and automated containment actions for dependency-driven incidents.
- Document proactive chaos/DR experiments and continuous validation steps to reduce blast radius.
- Clarify runbook steps for fault isolation, traffic shepherding, and recovery.

Scope:
- Applies to all Redwood platform services in the hosted/dedicated/private runtime stacks (control plane services, routing, caching, model-proxy, prefix-cache, billing, telemetry ingestion).
- Excludes ephemeral developer sandboxes and third-party SaaS endpoints (those should be captured in a separate vendor-dependency registry).

Key Definitions:
- Dependent service: a service that issues requests to another (upstream) service.
- Upstream service: the provider of an API or data consumed by dependents.
- Hard dependency: application correctness degrades or fails if the upstream is unavailable (e.g., auth, KV-store).
- Soft dependency: performance or observability degrades, but core flow usually remains (e.g., analytics ingestion).
- Fallback-capable: the dependent can route to an alternate service/model variant or use cached responses.

Directory Format (canonical fields):
- service_name: short identifier used in routing, logs, and dashboards.
- primary_owner: team and primary oncall alias.
- secondary_owner: escalation contact.
- dependency_type: hard | soft | optional | cache | datastore.
- typical_qps: sample steady qps (for traffic shaping heuristics).
- failure_modes: brief list (timeouts, increased error-rate, slow downstream KV reads).
- fallback_strategy: description or reference to fallback endpoint.

Example (table as text):
| service_name | primary_owner | dependency_type | typical_qps | fallback_strategy |
| model-proxy | infer-platform-team | hard | 12k | route to best-effort-quantized-model subset |
| prefix-cache | runtime-team | cache | 25k | return cached prefix or 503 tagged TTL |

Discovery & Inventory Process:
1. Automated tracing ingestion: run weekly job to scan sampling traces (X-Ray/Jaeger) and extract service-to-service edges with cardinality and p50/p99 latencies.
2. Config map scanning: parse Kubernetes service manifests, Env var references, and feature flags to identify candidate static dependencies.
3. OpenTelemetry dependency export: instrumented services must emit otel.resource.service.name and otel.peer.service attributes.
4. Manual verification: owners validate discovered edges quarterly; disputes escalate to platform architecture for arbitration.

Dependency Classification Rules:
- Hard if >90% of successful flows include the upstream within the request path, or if correctness depends on its output (auth, routing, billing).
- Cache if the upstream is used for read-mostly prefix/KV lookups and responses are safe to use slightly staled.
- Optional if the downstream can be functionally disabled without breaking the user narrative.

SLIs, Dashboards, and Alerting (recommended patterns):
- Per-edge SLI: error_rate (5xx) and latency_p99 for the upstream call edge. Create dashboards showing both absolute and normalized (errors/qps).
- Topology panel: adjacency matrix listing top 50 edges sorted by aggregated QPS (Prometheus + Cortex-derived metrics or Graph DB).
- Health summary: <service>.incoming-dependency-errors: alert when dependency error rate > 1% and impact > 5% of overall service errors (P1).

Suggested PromQL examples (copy/paste):
- Edge error rate (5xx over last 5m):
  sum(rate(http_client_requests_total{status=~\"5..\", upstream=\"${UPSTREAM}\"}[5m]))
  / sum(rate(http_client_requests_total{upstream=\"${UPSTREAM}\"}[5m]))

- Upstream latency p99 (5m):
  histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(http_client_request_duration_seconds_bucket{upstream=~\"${UPSTREAM}\"}[5m])) by (le))

Alert severity matrix (example):
- P0: Hard dependency outage generating >20% global error budget consumption for a user-facing product. Immediate paging to owners + platform SRE.
- P1: Sustained error rate >1% translating to user-visible errors. Page owners, execute isolation plan if no recovery in 10 minutes.
- P2: Latency degradation >3x p50 baseline without increased errors. Notify owners, monitor for 30 minutes.

Containment & Isolation Runbook (step-by-step):
1. Detect & Triage (oncall):
   - Confirm the adjacency: check dependency-directory for the upstream service and owners.
   - Verify alerts: correlate SLO dashboards, traces, and recent deploys.
2. Immediate Mitigation (platform SRE):
   - If upstream is a hard dependency with no fallback, initiate traffic-shepherding: shift traffic away from affected tenants using dynamic routing (see Routing CLI example).
   - If fallback exists: update routing policy to prefer fallback model variant; set a short TTL on caches to accelerate serving from fallback.
   - If high qps to upstream is saturating it, engage autoscaling (increase pool + evict noncritical workloads) and/or apply fair-queue throttling for soft tenants.
3. Containment commands (examples):
   - Disable feature flag for noncritical flows: rhubarb featurectl disable feature-x --namespace=prod
   - Apply routing change (gateway): curl -X POST https://platform-gw/api/v1/routing -d '{"route":"/v1/infer","variant":"fallback"}' 
   - Increase runtime worker count: kubectl scale deploy model-proxy --replicas=24 -n runtime-prod
4. Short-term stabilization: monitor error rates for 5–10 minutes. If containment succeeds, transition to mitigation and RCA prep.

Automations & Safeties to Implement (roadmap items):
- Auto-fallback policy engine: policy driven by SLI breach + dependency classification that can automatically swap to fallback variants for a bounded window.
- Graceful degradation feature flags: per-customer throttles and a fairness quota system enforced at the API gateway.
- Circuit-breaker library: centralized library declaratively configurable per edge; integrates with runtime telemetry for adaptive timeouts.

Chaos and Validation Plan:
- Quarterly chaos experiments targeting different dependency classes:
  - Cache eviction drill: purge prefix-cache in a canary zone and measure downstream p95 increase; validate fallbacks.
  - Upstream latency injection: add 200–500ms delay to a soft upstream to verify graceful degradation.
  - Hard dependency failover: simulate an auth service outage and validate routing/fallback automation.
- Success criteria: no customer-facing error growth >1% for critical tenants, and automated rollback triggers executed correctly.

Playbook for Post-Incident and Retrospective Capture:
- Immediately after stabilization: create an incident summary and tag with dependency edges involved. Include exact routing changes and automation triggers invoked.
- RCA template fields: timeline (UTC), decisions made, who executed commands, impact (tenants affected), what failed (root cause), contributing factors, and proposed mitigations with owners.
- Schedule a follow-up GH issue for any automation work and a 30-day verification task to confirm mitigations.

Operational Ownership & Routines:
- Owners must review indicated edges monthly and certify correctness in the dependency directory.
- Platform SRE maintains the auto-fallback policy engine and circuit-breaker templates.
- Change control: any permanent routing/fallback policy change requires a design note and a canary rollout (10% -> 50% -> 100%).

Appendix: Example circuit-breaker config (YAML text):
apiVersion: platform.redwood/v1
kind: CircuitBreakerPolicy
metadata:
  name: model-proxy-edge-policy
spec:
  upstream: prefix-cache
  failureThreshold: 0.02 # 2%
  samplingWindow: 120s
  cooldown: 60s
  fallback: model-proxy-fallback

Related pages and references:
- See service-call-graphs-and-cascading-failure-mitigation-playbook for graph-based mitigation patterns.
- Observability onboarding playbook for SLI standardization and dashboard templates.

Contacts and escalation aliases:
- Platform SRE: #platform-oncall (slack) and platform-oncall@redwood.ai
- Runtime team primary: runtime-team@redwood.ai
- Architecture: infra-arch@redwood.ai

Change log:
- 2024-09-12: Created initial dependency schema and discovery process (Priya N.).
- 2025-06-08: Added auto-fallback policy roadmap and PromQL examples (Liam C.).
- 2026-02-03: Incorporated circuit-breaker YAML example and revised alert severities (Mariana S.).

Next review due: 2026-08-01.

title: Safe Apply + Rollback (Optimization Configs) — Internal Beta Retrospective
preview:
## Overview
This page captures what we learned from the **internal beta** of **Optimization Config versioning + Safe Apply + one-click Rollback**.

**Beta window:** 2026-01-06 → 2026-01-24

**Primary goals of the beta**
- Validate that config changes are **traceable, reversible, and safe to apply** under real operational conditions.
- Confirm **rollback reliability** under partial rollout, retries, and concurrent activity.
- Stress test **gating signals** (5xx + p95/p99 latency; eval/quality gates for quant profiles where applicable).
- Collect **Console usability feedback** from on-call (SRE + Platform + Runtime).

**Beta scope**
- Hosted and Dedicated internal tenants only (3 internal workloads; 6 models across 2 regions).
- Features enabled behind feature flags:
  - `opt_config_versioning`
  - `opt_safe_apply_controller`
  - `opt_console_rollback`
  - `opt_audit_events`

**Participants / involved teams**
- Platform Control Plane: Ethan Park, Benji Okafor, Michael Nguyen
- Console & Tooling: Chloe Martin, Jada Williams, Felix Schneider
- Serving Runtime: Noah Patel, Omar El-Sayed
- SRE: Sean Gallagher, Aisha Bello
- Applied ML / Evals (quant gates): Jonas Weber, Arjun Desai
- Security (audit/RBAC review): Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei

---

## What we shipped / exercised in beta
1) **Versioned Optimization Configs**
- Immutable versions with metadata (author, created_at, change reason, scope).
- Diff view between versions (Console + API).

2) **Safe Apply workflow**
- Validate: schema + semantic checks.
- Progressive apply (canary percentage) by scope.
- Gates:
  - SLO gate: 5xx error rate and p95/p99 latency
  - Saturation signals (GPU utilization / queue depth) used for “pause recommended” but not hard-fail in beta

3) **Rollback workflow**
- Console “Rollback” action with confirmation.
- API rollback endpoint.
- Runtime idempotent applier + checkpointing.

4) **Audit logging + RBAC**
- Emitted audit events: create/validate/apply/pause/resume/promote/rollback.
- Role tested: “optimization change operator” (internal variant).

---

## Success metrics (beta results)
### Operational outcomes
- **Rollbacks executed:** 7 (4 planned drills, 3 real mitigations)
- **Median time from detection → rollback initiated:** 4m 10s
- **Median time from rollback initiated → workers converged:** 2m 35s (p95: 5m 20s)
- **Change failure events (beta):** 2 (both gated before full rollout)

### Safety / gating effectiveness
- 2/2 real regressions were caught by gates before 100% rollout:
  - Regression A: p95 latency +18% during canary (cache toggle + batching adjustment)
  - Regression B: elevated 5xx (mis-scoped apply hit wrong shard set; see “What didn’t work”)

### Usability signals (qualitative)
- On-call operators were able to complete rollback without paging an engineer in 5 of 7 cases.
- Biggest friction: understanding **blast radius** and **“what does rollback mean operationally”** (convergence, cache warm-up, and partial rollout states).

---

## What worked well
### 1) Runtime idempotency and convergence were strong
- The runtime applier handled retries safely; no observed “flip-flop” between versions once a rollback was committed.
- Checkpointing reduced stuck states; workers recovered correctly after intentional failure injection (killing canary controller mid-rollout).

### 2) Console flow was fast for the core path
- Operators liked the **version timeline** and “Active version” badge.
- “Rollback to last known good” as a pre-filled target reduced decision time.

### 3) Audit logging was actionable for incident review
- Events had enough context for correlating dashboard anomalies with config operations.
- Request IDs in the audit payload were specifically called out as useful by SRE.

### 4) Validation errors were (mostly) understandable
- Semantic validation messages for batching were cited as “actionable” (e.g., max batch tokens exceeding model memory guardrails).

---

## What didn’t work / pain points
### 1) Blast radius and scope visibility were not clear enough
Operators repeatedly asked:
- “Is this rollback for tenant X only, or also the shared pool?”
- “Is canary 10% of traffic, 10% of workers, or 10% of shards?”

Observed failure mode:
- One apply was created with intended scope `tenant=A, region=us-east` but was effectively applied to an unexpected shard group due to a scope-to-shard mapping mismatch in the controller. Gates caught the 5xx increase early, but it was still an operator surprise.

**Takeaway:** The system must present **scope → concrete targets** (regions, clusters, shard sets) in a way that is not ambiguous.

### 2) Gate feedback wasn’t “operator-ready”
- Gate status displayed as pass/fail without enough “why” detail.
- Operators wanted links to the exact dashboards/queries used for gate evaluation.
- False positive risk: p99 latency gate triggered during a known noisy window (deploy + unrelated neighbor workload). Rollout was paused manually.

**Takeaway:** Gates need explainability, and we need a standard “gate override policy” with logging.

### 3) Rollback semantics under partial rollout were confusing
- In 2 cases, a rollback target was selected, but some workers were still in “Applying (old)” vs “Applying (new)” states, and the UI didn’t clearly indicate expected convergence time.
- Operators didn’t know whether to wait, retry, or escalate.

**Takeaway:** We need an explicit “convergence view” and clearer idempotent semantics in the UI copy.

### 4) Cooldown / rate limiting was missing for high-churn situations
- During a drill, an operator applied 3 changes in quick succession. This created noise in gate evaluation and made it harder to interpret trends.

**Takeaway:** Add guardrails: cooldowns, warnings, and recommended minimum observation windows.

### 5) RBAC edge case caused a real delay
- One operator could view history but not click “Rollback” due to policy propagation lag / role mismatch.

**Takeaway:** RBAC must fail “loudly” in the UI with specific remediation guidance (who to contact, what role needed).

---

## On-call feedback (verbatim themes)
- “I can find the rollback button quickly, but I’m not 100% sure what it will hit.”
- “Show me the exact blast radius before I type-to-confirm.”
- “Gate says fail — I need to know which metric, over what window, and what the threshold was.”
- “After rollback, I need a checklist of what ‘good’ looks like (latency + error + throughput + saturation) and where to look.”

---

## Incidents / drills captured in beta
### Drill 1: Latency regression from batching heuristic
- Canary at 10% triggered p95 gate (+18% over baseline for 10 minutes)
- Controller paused; operator rolled back to prior version
- Recovery: p95 back to baseline within 6 minutes

### Drill 2: Wrong-region apply simulation
- Intentional mis-scope scenario to test safeguards
- UI did not clearly warn about region mismatch vs last known active
- Gates caught 5xx within canary window; rollback successful

### Real mitigation 1: Cache toggle causing queue instability
- Symptoms: queue depth rose; p99 latency spiked
- Gate did not hard-fail (queue depth not a hard gate), but operator initiated rollback based on dashboard
- Outcome: recovered; identified need to promote saturation from “informational” to a configurable gate

---

## Prioritized follow-ups
Below are follow-ups prioritized for **Limited GA (2026-02 → 2026-03)** readiness.

### P0 (must-have before Limited GA)
1) **Blast radius summary (scope → concrete targets) in Console + API**
- Owner: Jada Williams (Console), Benji Okafor (API)
- Target: 2026-02-14
- Notes: Include region/cluster/shard set and estimated % traffic/workers impacted.

2) **Gate explainability**
- Owner: Allison Grant (Observability), Aisha Bello (SRE)
- Target: 2026-02-18
- Notes: UI should show metric name, threshold, window, current value, and link to the dashboard panel/query.

3) **Rollback convergence UI + clear status model**
- Owner: Felix Schneider (Console), Noah Patel (Runtime)
- Target: 2026-02-21
- Notes: Distinguish states: queued/applying/converged/failed; show expected convergence time and last worker heartbeat.

4) **RBAC failure UX + supportable error messages**
- Owner: Jordan Lee (Policy/RBAC), Hannah Rivera (Console)
- Target: 2026-02-12
- Notes: Provide explicit “missing permission: optimization_config.rollback” and the role that grants it.

### P1 (should-have for GA quality)
5) **Cooldown + recommended observation windows**
- Owner: Chloe Martin
- Target: 2026-03-01
- Notes: Add warning on successive applies; optionally enforce cooldown by scope.

6) **Saturation gate policy**
- Owner: Sean Gallagher
- Target: 2026-03-05
- Notes: Define default saturation thresholds and when they hard-fail vs pause-recommended.

7) **Post-rollback verification checklist embedded in Console**
- Owner: Aisha Bello, Kenji Nakamura
- Target: 2026-03-08
- Notes: Checklist: 5xx, p95/p99, throughput, GPU util, queue depth; include links.

### P2 (nice-to-have / follow-up)
8) **Customer-visible change history format alignment**
- Owner: Paula Mendes
- Target: 2026-03-20
- Notes: Ensure fields and retention align with PM-903.

9) **Quantization quality gate UX and noise handling**
- Owner: Jonas Weber, Arjun Desai
- Target: 2026-03-25
- Notes: Improve messaging for flaky eval signals; add “insufficient data” state.

---

## Decisions captured
- We will treat **blast radius transparency** and **gate explainability** as P0 requirements (not polish).
- For Limited GA, **automatic rollback remains opt-in** and limited to internal/selected accounts; broader automation requires additional guardrails and customer comms.
- We will publish a single canonical definition of canary semantics: **% of workers (shard-based)** for the initial release, and ensure UI copy is consistent.

---

## Open questions
- Do we need an explicit “emergency rollback” path during Sev events that bypasses some confirmations (while still audit-logging)?
- What is the default baseline window for latency gates (e.g., trailing 30m vs same time-of-day)?
- For Private deployments, how do we present blast radius when the operator’s cluster topology is customer-specific?

---

## Next steps
- Platform + Console to review P0 implementation plan in the next release train sync (owner: Connor O'Brien).
- SRE to schedule a second gameday focused on partial rollout + concurrent apply/rollback (owner: Sean Gallagher; target week of 2026-02-17).
- Security to re-validate audit events and SIEM export fields after gate explainability and RBAC UX changes (owner: Kevin Osei).

---

## Appendix: “Known-good” operator workflow (beta)
1) Confirm current **Active version** and scope.
2) Run **Validate** on the candidate version; resolve semantic warnings.
3) Apply with canary (default 10%); watch gate panel + rollout health dashboard.
4) If gates fail or operator suspects regression, initiate rollback to last known good.
5) Wait for convergence; verify post-rollback checklist.
6) Document outcome in change reason + incident link (if applicable).

title: Alert Context Enrichment and Priority Scorer Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook describes the priority scoring algorithm and enrichment pipeline used by platform on-call operators to triage, classify, and act on cross-service degradation alerts. It covers the data inputs to the scorer, the threshold matrix for severity buckets, recommended operator actions for each bucket, dashboard queries, and automated remediation policies.

Intended audience:
- Platform operators
- Observability engineers
- SREs on-call for inference-router, batcher, and cache services
- Product owners responsible for SLOs

Goals and scope:
- Make alert severity predictable and repeatable across regions and tenants.
- Surface business impact (cost, affected customers, token loss) alongside technical signals.
- Provide deterministic operator steps for each priority to reduce cognitive load.
- Apply only to alerts that originate in the platform telemetry pipeline: inference-router, kv-prefix-cache, batcher, model-variant-router, and platform-sidecar.

Key concepts:
- Priority score: numeric 0-100 computed per alert. Higher == greater urgency.
- Impact signals: token-rate-loss, success-rate-drop, latency-tail-95 delta, tenant-count-affected, estimated-dollar-loss-per-hour.
- Confidence signal: corroboration across telemetry sources (traces, logs, metrics).
- Priority buckets: P1 (>=85), P2 (65-84), P3 (40-64), P4 (<40).

Priority scorer inputs (enrichment pipeline):
1) Raw alert payload: alert_name, service, region, metric, observed_value, threshold, query_id.
2) Correlated traces: sampling of trace IDs tagged by alert query.
3) Recent incident history: incidents for same service in last 72h.
4) Tenant impact lookup: mapping of active tenants and their SLA tier (free/standard/premium).
5) Cost model estimator: converts token loss or extra compute to USD/hr.
6) User-facing feature map: list of product features tied to the impacted route (ex: streaming-chat, embeddings).

Scoring heuristic (human readable):
- Base = normalize(metric_delta) * 40
- Tenancy multiplier = min(1 + 0.15 * premium_tenants_impacted, 1.6) * 25
- Cost signal = sigmoid(estimated_usd_loss_hour / 100) * 15
- Confidence bonus = (corroborated_sources / 3) * 10
- Recurrence penalty = +10 if incident_count_last_72h >= 2
- Final = clamp(Base + Tenancy + Cost + Confidence + Recurrence, 0, 100)

Severity buckets and immediate operator actions:
- P1 (>=85) — Immediate paging, run emergency playbook:
  1) Page on-call (pager duty): platform-core-P1
  2) Attach top 5 traces to incident, run \"model-swap-preflight\" if model-serving variant is failing
  3) Trigger auto-fallback to compatible model-variant if configured (see automatic-fallback policy table below)
  4) If latency tail-95 > SLO by >300ms, enable fast-path routing (route small requests to regional-cache)
  5) Create incident in incident tracker, tag with tenants impacted and estimated cost.
- P2 (65-84) — Operator attention during shift, potential canary rollback:
  1) Create a channel and add service owner, SRE and product owner
  2) Run diagnostic queries (examples below) and collect full stack traces
  3) If token loss estimate > $200/hr or >50 premium tenants, escalate to on-call lead
  4) Prepare canary rollback or model variant swap, but do not execute without approval
- P3 (40-64) — Investigate during normal rota, file RCA if unresolved after 24h:
  1) Validate signal (ensure not synthetic or scheduled maintenance)
  2) Run correlation notebook and check KV cache hit-rate, prefix-cache pressure, and scheduling queue depth
  3) If verified, open a maintenance ticket for dev-owner to address
- P4 (<40) — Informational, no immediate action required:
  1) Attach to weekly dashboard for trend analysis
  2) Auto-close alert after 4h if no escalation

Example diagnostic queries (Grafana / PromQL):
- Latency tail-95 delta for inference-router (5m):
  sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{route=~\"/v1/generate|/v1/chat\"}[5m])) by (le)
- Token throughput loss (per cluster):
  sum(rate(tokens_produced_total[1m])) by (cluster)
- KV prefix cache miss storm indicator:
  increase(kv_prefix_cache_evictions_total[5m]) > 100

Automatic remediation policies (safe-by-default):
- model-fallback-policy (table):
  | condition | action | safety | owner |
  |-----------|--------|--------|-------|
  | model-ooms >= 3 in 10m | swap to quantized-variant | automated | eng-serving-runtime |
  | tail-latency > 2x SLO and confirmed cache-miss | enable regional-cache-prefetch | automated, rate-limited | eng-platform |
  | token-loss > 100k tokens/min and premium-tenants>10 | throttle non-premium traffic 20% | automated with cooldown 10m | eng-platform |

Dashboard and alert templates to inspect (links):
- Platform impact dashboard: grafana/platform/impact-summary
- Tenant exposure heatmap: grafana/platform/tenant-exposure
- Cost drift dashboard: grafana/platform/cost-drift
- On-call context snapshot (generated per alert): /dashboards/alert-context/{{alert_id}}

Alert payload example (JSON forwarded to scorer):
{
  \"alert_id\": \"a1b2c3\",
  \"service\": \"inference-router\",
  \"region\": \"us-west-2\",
  \"metric\": \"request_duration_ms_p95\",
  \"observed\": \"1250\",
  \"threshold\": \"700\",
  \"timestamp\": \"2026-02-03T02:14:00Z\"
}

Operator checklist for P1 (step-by-step):
1) Acknowledge the page in PagerDuty and set the incident priority to SEV1.
2) Grab the auto-generated context URL and verify impacted tenants and estimated cost.
3) Attach top-5 traces (click \"collect traces\") to the incident.
4) If model-ooms or GPU errors appear in logs, run: 
   $ rwdctl model swap --from current --to fallback-quantized --region us-west-2 --dry-run && rwdctl model swap --from current --to fallback-quantized --region us-west-2 --confirm
5) If high tail-latency with cache misses: enable cache-prefetch for the affected prefix:
   $ rwdctl cache prefetch --prefix /v1/chat --region us-west-2 --rate-limit 500
6) Notify CS if >50 premium-tenants impacted and create a customer comms stub.
7) Keep incident channel open and post updates every 15 minutes until mitigated.

Post-incident duties and retrospective triggers:
- Create an RCA within 48 hours if incident classified P1 or P2.
- Run the priority-scorer sensitivity test: replay the alert through historical telemetry for last 90 days and confirm bucket assignment.
- If false-positive or noisy P3/P4, add suppression rule to the alert-router with expiration.

Operational metrics for the scorer itself (SLOs for observability):
- False escalation rate <= 5% (monthly)
- Mean enrichment latency <= 8s (95th <= 12s) from alert ingestion to score published
- Corroboration coverage >= 70% for P1 alerts

Maintenance and tuning playbook:
1) Monthly: review tenant weightings and cost model coefficients with product and finance.
2) Quarterly: run a sensitivity analysis and publish suggested coefficient edits.
3) After every on-call rotation: operator should flag misclassifications in the shared spreadsheet: /team-wiki/alert-classification-feedback

Ownership and escalation contacts:
- Primary owner: eng-platform-observability (on-call alias)
- Secondary owner: eng-serving-runtime (pager alias)
- On-call lead: Priya Nair (pager)
- Incident commander rotation: see /team-schedule/oncall-rotation

Open questions and known limitations:
- Current cost estimator is calibrated for CPU/GPU price points from 2025; large hardware price changes need coefficient updates.
- Cross-region tenant deduplication can inflate tenant counts when traffic bursts replicate across regions; there's an open ticket ENG-PLATFORM-847 to address it.

Appendix: quick tuning knobs (operators only):
- config/env PRIORITY_BASE_SCALE (default 40)
- config/env TENANCY_MULTIPLIER_CAP (default 1.6)
- config/feature auto_fallback_enabled (true/false), cooldowns are enforced at API-gateway layer

Related docs and templates:
- See: /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/platform-operator-snapshot-and-rollback-patterns-2026 for snapshot patterns
- Alert template: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/incident-signal-playbook-dashboard-library-2026

Revision history:
- 2026-01-12: Initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2026-01-25: Incorporated feedback from Diego and Samir; added example rwdctl commands
- 2026-02-03: Finalized scoring coefficients and published as canonical playbook
text:
Summary:
This playbook describes the priority scoring algorithm and enrichment pipeline used by platform on-call operators to triage, classify, and act on cross-service degradation alerts. It covers the data inputs to the scorer, the threshold matrix for severity buckets, recommended operator actions for each bucket, dashboard queries, and automated remediation policies.

Intended audience:
- Platform operators
- Observability engineers
- SREs on-call for inference-router, batcher, and cache services
- Product owners responsible for SLOs

Goals and scope:
- Make alert severity predictable and repeatable across regions and tenants.
- Surface business impact (cost, affected customers, token loss) alongside technical signals.
- Provide deterministic operator steps for each priority to reduce cognitive load.
- Apply only to alerts that originate in the platform telemetry pipeline: inference-router, kv-prefix-cache, batcher, model-variant-router, and platform-sidecar.

Key concepts:
- Priority score: numeric 0-100 computed per alert. Higher == greater urgency.
- Impact signals: token-rate-loss, success-rate-drop, latency-tail-95 delta, tenant-count-affected, estimated-dollar-loss-per-hour.
- Confidence signal: corroboration across telemetry sources (traces, logs, metrics).
- Priority buckets: P1 (>=85), P2 (65-84), P3 (40-64), P4 (<40).

Priority scorer inputs (enrichment pipeline):
1) Raw alert payload: alert_name, service, region, metric, observed_value, threshold, query_id.
2) Correlated traces: sampling of trace IDs tagged by alert query.
3) Recent incident history: incidents for same service in last 72h.
4) Tenant impact lookup: mapping of active tenants and their SLA tier (free/standard/premium).
5) Cost model estimator: converts token loss or extra compute to USD/hr.
6) User-facing feature map: list of product features tied to the impacted route (ex: streaming-chat, embeddings).

Scoring heuristic (human readable):
- Base = normalize(metric_delta) * 40
- Tenancy multiplier = min(1 + 0.15 * premium_tenants_impacted, 1.6) * 25
- Cost signal = sigmoid(estimated_usd_loss_hour / 100) * 15
- Confidence bonus = (corroborated_sources / 3) * 10
- Recurrence penalty = +10 if incident_count_last_72h >= 2
- Final = clamp(Base + Tenancy + Cost + Confidence + Recurrence, 0, 100)

Severity buckets and immediate operator actions:
- P1 (>=85) — Immediate paging, run emergency playbook:
  1) Page on-call (pager duty): platform-core-P1
  2) Attach top 5 traces to incident, run \"model-swap-preflight\" if model-serving variant is failing
  3) Trigger auto-fallback to compatible model-variant if configured (see automatic-fallback policy table below)
  4) If latency tail-95 > SLO by >300ms, enable fast-path routing (route small requests to regional-cache)
  5) Create incident in incident tracker, tag with tenants impacted and estimated cost.
- P2 (65-84) — Operator attention during shift, potential canary rollback:
  1) Create a channel and add service owner, SRE and product owner
  2) Run diagnostic queries (examples below) and collect full stack traces
  3) If token loss estimate > $200/hr or >50 premium tenants, escalate to on-call lead
  4) Prepare canary rollback or model variant swap, but do not execute without approval
- P3 (40-64) — Investigate during normal rota, file RCA if unresolved after 24h:
  1) Validate signal (ensure not synthetic or scheduled maintenance)
  2) Run correlation notebook and check KV cache hit-rate, prefix-cache pressure, and scheduling queue depth
  3) If verified, open a maintenance ticket for dev-owner to address
- P4 (<40) — Informational, no immediate action required:
  1) Attach to weekly dashboard for trend analysis
  2) Auto-close alert after 4h if no escalation

Example diagnostic queries (Grafana / PromQL):
- Latency tail-95 delta for inference-router (5m):
  sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{route=~\"/v1/generate|/v1/chat\"}[5m])) by (le)
- Token throughput loss (per cluster):
  sum(rate(tokens_produced_total[1m])) by (cluster)
- KV prefix cache miss storm indicator:
  increase(kv_prefix_cache_evictions_total[5m]) > 100

Automatic remediation policies (safe-by-default):
- model-fallback-policy (table):
  | condition | action | safety | owner |
  |-----------|--------|--------|-------|
  | model-ooms >= 3 in 10m | swap to quantized-variant | automated | eng-serving-runtime |
  | tail-latency > 2x SLO and confirmed cache-miss | enable regional-cache-prefetch | automated, rate-limited | eng-platform |
  | token-loss > 100k tokens/min and premium-tenants>10 | throttle non-premium traffic 20% | automated with cooldown 10m | eng-platform |

Dashboard and alert templates to inspect (links):
- Platform impact dashboard: grafana/platform/impact-summary
- Tenant exposure heatmap: grafana/platform/tenant-exposure
- Cost drift dashboard: grafana/platform/cost-drift
- On-call context snapshot (generated per alert): /dashboards/alert-context/{{alert_id}}

Alert payload example (JSON forwarded to scorer):
{
  \"alert_id\": \"a1b2c3\",
  \"service\": \"inference-router\",
  \"region\": \"us-west-2\",
  \"metric\": \"request_duration_ms_p95\",
  \"observed\": \"1250\",
  \"threshold\": \"700\",
  \"timestamp\": \"2026-02-03T02:14:00Z\"
}

Operator checklist for P1 (step-by-step):
1) Acknowledge the page in PagerDuty and set the incident priority to SEV1.
2) Grab the auto-generated context URL and verify impacted tenants and estimated cost.
3) Attach top-5 traces (click \"collect traces\") to the incident.
4) If model-ooms or GPU errors appear in logs, run: 
   $ rwdctl model swap --from current --to fallback-quantized --region us-west-2 --dry-run && rwdctl model swap --from current --to fallback-quantized --region us-west-2 --confirm
5) If high tail-latency with cache misses: enable cache-prefetch for the affected prefix:
   $ rwdctl cache prefetch --prefix /v1/chat --region us-west-2 --rate-limit 500
6) Notify CS if >50 premium-tenants impacted and create a customer comms stub.
7) Keep incident channel open and post updates every 15 minutes until mitigated.

Post-incident duties and retrospective triggers:
- Create an RCA within 48 hours if incident classified P1 or P2.
- Run the priority-scorer sensitivity test: replay the alert through historical telemetry for last 90 days and confirm bucket assignment.
- If false-positive or noisy P3/P4, add suppression rule to the alert-router with expiration.

Operational metrics for the scorer itself (SLOs for observability):
- False escalation rate <= 5% (monthly)
- Mean enrichment latency <= 8s (95th <= 12s) from alert ingestion to score published
- Corroboration coverage >= 70% for P1 alerts

Maintenance and tuning playbook:
1) Monthly: review tenant weightings and cost model coefficients with product and finance.
2) Quarterly: run a sensitivity analysis and publish suggested coefficient edits.
3) After every on-call rotation: operator should flag misclassifications in the shared spreadsheet: /team-wiki/alert-classification-feedback

Ownership and escalation contacts:
- Primary owner: eng-platform-observability (on-call alias)
- Secondary owner: eng-serving-runtime (pager alias)
- On-call lead: Priya Nair (pager)
- Incident commander rotation: see /team-schedule/oncall-rotation

Open questions and known limitations:
- Current cost estimator is calibrated for CPU/GPU price points from 2025; large hardware price changes need coefficient updates.
- Cross-region tenant deduplication can inflate tenant counts when traffic bursts replicate across regions; there's an open ticket ENG-PLATFORM-847 to address it.

Appendix: quick tuning knobs (operators only):
- config/env PRIORITY_BASE_SCALE (default 40)
- config/env TENANCY_MULTIPLIER_CAP (default 1.6)
- config/feature auto_fallback_enabled (true/false), cooldowns are enforced at API-gateway layer

Related docs and templates:
- See: /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/platform-operator-snapshot-and-rollback-patterns-2026 for snapshot patterns
- Alert template: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/incident-signal-playbook-dashboard-library-2026

Revision history:
- 2026-01-12: Initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2026-01-25: Incorporated feedback from Diego and Samir; added example rwdctl commands
- 2026-02-03: Finalized scoring coefficients and published as canonical playbook

title: Operator Sandbox: Synthetic Scenarios & Verification Catalog
preview:
Overview
--------
This page is a curated catalog of synthetic, reproducible platform scenarios for the operator sandbox environment (sandbox.redwood.internal). The goal is to provide a single, searchable library of tests that operators and SREs can run to validate runtime behavior, fallback logic, and observability plumbing before changes reach staging or production.

Short intro: each entry is a self-contained scenario with intent, preconditions, steps to execute, expected signals, verification checks, and teardown instructions. Scenarios are designed to be idempotent and safe to run in the operator sandbox; some are safe to run in regional staging with approval.

Goals
-----
- Provide reproducible test cases for common and high-risk operational behaviors (eviction, batching regression, fallback misfire, cold-cache storms).
- Surface gaps in dashboards, alerting, and tracing by driving deterministic failure modes.
- Create a lightweight verification workflow to gate merges that change routing, batching logic, kernel selection, or cache sharding.
- Maintain scenario metadata and example observability queries that map to SLOs.

Audience
--------
- Platform operators, oncall SREs, runtime engineers, release engineers, applied ML infra teams.

Scope and Safety
----------------
- Runs are permitted only in operator sandbox unless a scenario explicitly marks regional staging as allowed.
- All scenarios include a rate/impact cap. Do not exceed those caps without senior operator approval.
- Scenarios that touch customer tenants or production data are explicitly forbidden.

Catalog: high-level scenario index
--------------------------------
(Use the scenario ID to reference in runbooks and PR checks)

| Scenario ID | Title | Primary intent | Safe in staging? | Estimated duration |
| SCN-001 | KV-prefix-cold-cache-spike | Validate prefix/KV cache miss storm handling and warmup behavior | no | 12m |
| SCN-002 | Batch-size-regression-inject | Confirm batcher caps and slow-path fallback when batch sizes exceed threshold | yes (staging) | 8m |
| SCN-003 | Model-variant-fallback-loop | Verify model variant fallback policy does not enter retry loops under transient errors | yes (staging) | 10m |
| SCN-004 | Tenant-throttle-and-credit-surge | Simulate tenant sudden delta in token usage and validate soft-quota enforcement | no | 15m |
| SCN-005 | Kernel-switch-latency-impact | Force JIT kernel switch on live small-batch workload and capture tail latency regressions | yes (staging) | 20m |
| SCN-006 | Graceful-degrade-region-split | Simulate partial region partition and validate zero-trust fallback routing | no | 25m |

Detailed scenario example: SCN-001 (KV-prefix-cold-cache-spike)
----------------------------------------------------------------
Summary: Drive a controlled burst of requests that hit a previously-evicted KV prefix to validate warmup throughput, operator backpressure signals, and cache warmup dashboards.

Preconditions
1) Sandbox cluster has small test cache pool named sandbox-kv-01.
2) Test model "tiny-verify-v1" pinned for the tenant used by the scenario (tenant-id: sandbox-verify-001).
3) Operator must create an ephemeral prefix set using the helper: 
   rctl sandbox create-prefix --pool sandbox-kv-01 --size 128 --output prefix-list.txt

Execution steps (operator)
1) Evict target prefixes to ensure cold cache:
   - CLI: rctl sandbox evict-prefix --pool sandbox-kv-01 --file prefix-list.txt
2) Fire synthetic load with rate limits (cap: 500 concurrent tokens/s total, 50 requests/s):
   - Test harness script (bash):
     ./sandbox-loadgen --tenant sandbox-verify-001 --model tiny-verify-v1 --prefix-file prefix-list.txt --rate 50 --concurrency 8 --duration 600s
3) Monitor dashboards (see Observability section) and watch for backpressure signals.
4) Stop load after 10 minutes and run verification queries.

Expected signals and tolerances
- Cache hit ratio for target prefix should rise from ~0% to >60% within 5 minutes.
- Per-request tail latency (p95) should not exceed 600ms for more than 2 minutes during warmup.
- Batch rejection rate should remain <1% during the run.
- Operator backpressure metric sandbox.kvpool.backpressure_events should not exceed 3 events for the pool.

Verification checks (automated where possible)
1) Cache warmup: run query against metrics store: "sum(rate(kv_cache_hits{pool='sandbox-kv-01',prefix=~'scn1-.*'}[1m])) / sum(rate(kv_cache_requests{pool='sandbox-kv-01',prefix=~'scn1-.*'}[1m]))" and assert > 0.6 at minute 5.
2) Latency: p95 tail from APM: "trace_latency_p95{service='runtime-proxy',route='generate'}" - assert <= 600ms except for allowed window.
3) Batch rejection: alert metric "batcher.rejection_rate{pool='sandbox-kv-01'}" - assert < 0.01.
4) Backpressure events: check logs and metric sandbox.kvpool.backpressure_events <= 3.

Observability & dashboards
-------------------------
Required dashboards (links are internal):
- Sandbox KV Warmup (dashboards/platform/sandbox-kv-warmup)
- Operator Backpressure Summary (dashboards/platform/operator-backpressure-summary)
- Batch Rejections & Queue Depth (dashboards/platform/batcher-health)
Recommended dashboard panels and example PromQL snippets:
- Cache hit ratio (per-prefix):
  promql: |
    sum(rate(kv_cache_hits{pool="sandbox-kv-01"}[1m])) / sum(rate(kv_cache_requests{pool="sandbox-kv-01"}[1m]))
- Tail latency (p95):
  promql: |
    histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(runtime_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route="generate"}[1m])) by (le))
- Operator backpressure events (counter):
  promql: |
    increase(sandbox_kvpool_backpressure_events_total{pool="sandbox-kv-01"}[10m])

Alerting & gating rules (for sandbox verification only)
-------------------------------------------------------
These are soft gates used by verification CI runs. They do not auto-incident in production.
- ALRT-SCN-001-LATENCY: p95(generate) > 1000ms for 5m -> mark scenario failed.
- ALRT-SCN-001-BATCH-REJ: batcher.rejection_rate > 0.02 for 3m -> fail.
- ALRT-SCN-001-BP: increase(sandbox_kvpool_backpressure_events_total[10m]) > 5 -> fail.

Runbook excerpt: operator actions on failure
------------------------------------------
1) Immediately stop the load generator: ./sandbox-loadgen --stop --run-id <run-id>
2) If p95 remains > 1000ms after 2 minutes, scale sandbox-kv-01 pool by +1 instance: rctl pool scale --pool sandbox-kv-01 --delta 1
3) If backpressure persists, enable temporary routing to fallback-variant: rctl routing set-policy --tenant sandbox-verify-001 --policy fallback-variant
4) Capture a sampling trace set: curl -s "http://apm.internal/trace/sample?service=runtime-proxy&tag=scn=SCN-001" -o scn001-traces.json

Post-run checklist and reporting
--------------------------------
1) Attach run metadata to the scenario ticket (SCN-001-<YYYYMMDD>-<operator>): run-id, start/end timestamps, run artifacts (traces, metric screenshots).
2) Run automated verification script: ./sandbox-verify --run-id <run-id> --scenario SCN-001 --output verification.json
3) If verification fails, open a platform-ops ticket with severity "verification-fail" and tag the owning teams.
4) Add a short blameless note to the scenario log explaining the root cause if understood.

Adding new scenarios: process and metadata
-----------------------------------------
1) Create a new markdown file under the operator-sandbox repository at docs/scenarios/SCN-XXX-<short-name>.md using the template below.
2) PR must include: intent, preconditions, exact execution commands, monitoring panels to check, exact verification queries, owner, and safety caps.
3) Required metadata fields (front-matter):
   - scenario_id: SCN-
   - title:
   - owner_team:
   - safe_in_staging: yes/no
   - estimated_duration_minutes:
   - rate_cap_description:

Naming convention
-----------------
- SCN-<NNN>-<short-keyword> (e.g., SCN-042-evict-warmup). Keep NNN zero-padded 3 digits. Use short-keyword for readability.

Scenario metadata template (example)
----------------------------------
---
scenario_id: SCN-XXX
title: Short descriptive title
owner_team: eng-platform
safe_in_staging: no
estimated_duration_minutes: 10
rate_cap_description: "max 100 reqs/s, max 500 tokens/s"
---

Ownership and cadence
---------------------
- Maintainers: eng-platform/operators (primary), eng-serving-runtime (secondary).
- Scenario review cadence: quarterly review of top 20 scenarios; new scenarios require at least two approvers (one from ops, one from runtime).
- Scenario retirement: mark with status "deprecated" and add migration notes if replaced.

Appendix: CLI snippets and example payloads
------------------------------------------
Evicting prefixes (example):
- CLI: rctl sandbox evict-prefix --pool sandbox-kv-01 --file prefix-list.txt
Load generator (example invocation):
- ./sandbox-loadgen --tenant sandbox-verify-001 --model tiny-verify-v1 --prefix-file prefix-list.txt --rate 50 --concurrency 8 --duration 600s
APM sampling (curl):
- curl -s "http://apm.internal/trace/sample?service=runtime-proxy&tag=scn=SCN-001" -o scn001-traces.json

JSON event example (metadata attached to run):
{
  "scenario_id": "SCN-001",
  "run_id": "scn001-20260202-01",
  "operator": "maya.chen",
  "start_ts": "2026-02-02T15:04:05Z",
  "end_ts": "2026-02-02T15:14:05Z",
  "notes": "Warmup behavior within expected bounds"
}

Contacts and escalation
----------------------
- Primary owner: eng-platform/operators (oncall roster)
- Secondary: eng-serving-runtime oncall
- For CI gating failures escalate to release-engineering lead.

Change history
--------------
- 2025-03-12: Initial catalog and SCN-001..SCN-003 entries (Maya Chen).
- 2025-09-18: Added SCN-004 tenant throttle scenario and gating rules (Rafael Ortiz).
- 2026-02-02: Added kernel-switch example and clarified verification PromQLs (Sabrina Li).

Related runbooks and docs
------------------------
- Emergency model swap and token surge mitigation (runbook)
- Cache prefetch prioritization for LLM workloads (how-to)
- Operator sandbox provisioning guide (internal link)

title: Rolling Linux Kernel Patch Safety for GPU Fleet Ops
preview:
Overview:

Purpose: This runbook describes safe operational procedures, observability checks, and automated safeguards for performing rolling Linux kernel patches on Redwood GPU node pools. It focuses on minimizing P99/P99.9 latency blasts for inference traffic, preserving KV cache health, and maintaining SLOs during a multi-region rolling kernel update.

Scope: Applies to all GPU fleets that serve production inference (hosted + dedicated nodes) and any Kubernetes or VM groups running the Redwood serving runtime. Excluded: isolated on-prem clusters with dedicated change processes (see eng-private-deployments).

When to use this playbook:
- Security or stability kernel patches that must be applied within a maintenance window.
- Kernel backports that address scheduler, memory reclamation, or driver compatibility impacting inference performance.
- When automated node replacement is used (termination + re-enroll) instead of in-place kernel upgrade.

Owner and escalation:
- Primary owner: Fleet Reliability (eng-sre)
- Secondary: Serving Runtime Ops (eng-serving-runtime)
- Pager: on-call fleet-sre rotation
- Escalation matrix: Tier 1 operator -> Fleet Reliability lead -> Platform Eng manager -> CTO on severe customer impact

SLOs and guardrails (pre-update):
- Target P50 token latency delta during maintenance: < +15% of baseline
- Target P99 token latency delta: < +30% of baseline
- Allowed error budget consumption window: no more than 2% of monthly budget per region for scheduled maintenance
- KV cache hit-rate degradation allowed: <= 8% drop sustained for >10 minutes

Preflight checklist (automated + manual steps):
1) Canary validation (automated):
   - Spin up a 3-node canary pool in a non-customer region that mirrors production traffic profile using synthetic load generator (read from /ops/canary/job-playbooks).
   - Run the following validation suite for 45 minutes: kernel boot, driver module load, runtime healthcheck, prefix cache warmup, 1k streaming generation sessions.
   - Pass criteria: no OOMs, no KV-cache corruption, <10% P99 delta vs baseline.

2) Metrics and dashboard baseline snapshot:
   - Capture a 2-hour baseline for the targeted node group: token-latency.{p50,p95,p99}, request-rate, gpunode.memory.available, gpubandwidth.util, kvcache.hit_rate, serving-runtime.errors.
   - Save metrics snapshot ID to the maintenance ticket.

3) Alert and rollback hooks:
   - Ensure the following alert rules are active and have on-call notifications:
     * gpu-node-reboot-rate > 0.5/min (5m window) -> paging
     * serving-runtime.request-error-rate > 0.2% (2m) -> alert (pager if sustained 10m)
     * token-latency.p99-increase > 40% (5m) -> auto-throttle traffic + page on-call
   - Ensure the automated rollback playbook is registered in orchestration (see Automation section).

4) Communication:
   - Open maintenance ticket with target node groups, planned rollout cadence, and observable dashboard links.
   - Notify downstream teams: Customer Success for impacted tenants, Platform Observability for dashboard watch, and Support.

Rolling upgrade procedure (operator steps):
1) Drain strategy and prewarm:
   - Mark node as unschedulable for new inference sessions: kubectl label node <node> redwood-maintenance=true && kubectl cordon <node>
   - Wait for in-flight sessions to drain using grace period 90s (adjust per tenant SLA). Monitor streaming.session_count.{node}.
   - If using live-migrate for KV cache, trigger KV-shard handoff to target node per operator workflows.

2) Apply kernel patch (two supported flows):
   A) Immutable replacement (recommended):
      - Trigger autoscaler to provision replacement nodes with patched image.
      - Ensure new nodes pass health checks and are added to pool.
      - Evict old node once KV cache synced or prefix cache checkpointed.
   B) In-place upgrade (only for non-critical pools):
      - Run remote upgrade: apt/yum package install -> update-grub -> reboot.
      - Monitor dmesg and journalctl for driver errors during boot.

3) Post-boot validations (automated checks, operator watches):
   - Verify kernel version: uname -r -> matches patched version.
   - GPU driver present and in the right mode: nvidia-smi -> check GPU memory, GPU persistence mode.
   - Serving runtime health: curl -f http://localhost:9090/healthz -> status=ok
   - KV cache consistency: run cache-checker tool (ops/bin/cache-checker --node <node>) -> exit code 0
   - Token latency smoke test: run a short synthetic workload (100 requests) and compare to baseline snapshot.

4) Admission back to pool:
   - If all checks pass, uncordon node: kubectl uncordon <node> and remove redwood-maintenance label.
   - Wait 10 minutes of steady-state observation before proceeding to next node.

Automated safety gates and orchestration:
- Orchestration system (fleet-orch) must support the following gates before advancing to the next node:
  1) No P99 spike > 40% across the region for 10m.
  2) Global KV cache hit-rate decline < 8% sustained.
  3) Failed node fraction < 1 in 100 nodes.
- Implementation pattern (pseudocode):

  # Pseudocode for orchestration gate (shell-style)
  for node in nodes_to_patch:
    apply_patch(node)
    start_time=$(date +%s)
    sleep 120
    if metrics.p99_increase(region) > 40:
      rollback_node(node)
      notify_oncall("p99 spike: rollback triggered for node ${node}")
      abort_rolling_update()
    fi
    if metrics.kvcache_drop(region) > 8 and elapsed > 600:
      rollback_node(node)
      notify_oncall("kv cache degradation: rollback triggered for node ${node}")
      abort_rolling_update()
    fi
    wait_until_steady(600)
  done

Dashboards and essential views to monitor:
- Fleet health overview (fleet/ui/fleet-health): node readiness, reboot rate, replace rate
- Latency burn-down: token-latency.{p50,p95,p99} by region and node-pool
- KV cache topology: kvcache.hit_rate by shard and region; prefix-cache.write_lag
- Driver & kernel errors: syslog kernel.err and dmesg.gpu.error counts
- Canary test panel: synthetic success rate, P99 latency, resource pressure graphs

Alert tuning guidance (examples):
- Set token-latency.p99-increase alert threshold at +40% for operator paging, +60% for critical SLA escalation.
- Use composite alerts to reduce noise: only page if latency spike AND error-rate > 0.05%.

Rollback strategies (fast vs safe):
- Fast rollback: revert traffic routing to previous model variants and mark node group draining. Used for severe customer-impact incidents.
- Safe rollback: re-provision nodes with previous image, wait for KV cache rehydration, then re-introduce. Preferred for cache-sensitive regressions.

Post-incident retrospective checklist:
1) Capture traces and flamegraphs for any latency spike windows.
2) Correlate kernel boot timestamps with latency/error telemetry.
3) Re-run canary with narrower workload slices to reproduce.
4) Update playbook with observed deviations (e.g., driver regression, scheduler preemption).

Automation snippets and CLI examples:
- Health check curl:
  curl -fsS http://localhost:9090/healthz || exit 1

- Minimal orchestration command to check kernel on a node (bash):
  ssh ops@${NODE} "uname -r && nvidia-smi --query-gpu=driver_version --format=csv"

- Grafana snapshot export (example):
  grafana-cli snapshot export --dashboard-id=1234 --org-id=1 --api-key=${GRAFANA_KEY} > /tmp/patch-dashboard.json

Operational limits and timing recommendations:
- Recommended patch cadence: monthly security windows for non-critical pools; quarterly for latency-critical pools unless security urgency requires otherwise.
- Max concurrent patching per region: floor(total_nodes/50) or 10 nodes, whichever is smaller, unless canary shows lower risk.
- Maintenance window: prefer 02:00–07:00 local region time for lowest traffic (metric-backed).

Known gotchas and mitigation notes:
- Kernel/driver combos: some older kernels regress GPU page-fault handling; test both mainline and vendor drivers.
- KV cache cold-start penalty: when replacing multiple nodes, coordinate staggered prefix checkpoint restores to avoid N*cache-rebuild storms.
- Live-migration edge-case: in-place driver reloads may leave stale device nodes; prefer immutable replacement flow.

Appendix: quick reference table

| Item | Command / Metric | Threshold / Expected |
|------|------------------|----------------------|
| Health endpoint | curl http://localhost:9090/healthz | status=ok |
| Kernel check | uname -r | <patched-version-string> |
| GPU driver | nvidia-smi | driver_version matches image |
| Latency panel | token-latency.p99 | < +40% allowed for gate |
| KV cache | kvcache.hit_rate | decline < 8% |
| Max concurrent nodes | config | floor(total_nodes/50) or 10 |

References and related docs:
- eng-platform/runbooks/gpu-pool-live-shrink-and-maintenance-runbook-2026
- eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/prefix-cache-shard-operator-playbook
- security-and-compliance/change-management/kernel-patch-approval-flow

Contact and feedback:
- To propose improvements, open a PR against this page or ping #fleet-ops on Slack.
- Post-mortem owner for kernel patch incidents: Fleet Reliability lead (fleet-reliability@redwood.com).

Document history:
- 2025-08-14: initial draft by Aisha Patel
- 2025-10-02: added automated gate pseudocode and threshold tuning by Marco Chen
- 2026-02-02: expanded rollback strategies and added KV cache mitigation guidance by Lina Rodriguez

title: Project Orion — Weekly status update (2026-01-30)
preview:
# Summary
We reduced the main integration risk by landing a low-overhead decision-event emission path and validating its performance impact.

# Status
- Overall: On Track
- RAG: Green

# Progress this week
- Runtime router: implemented asynchronous decision event emission behind a gate (orion_decision_events_enabled).
- Perf: measured overhead at p95 < 0.3ms for chat workloads with 1% sampling.
- Control plane: implemented a basic policy evaluation endpoint returning canonical fallback_reason values.

# Risks / blockers
- Console policy editor still missing validation rules for incompatible model variants.

# Next week
- Add compatibility validation in console (block save if variant is not compatible).
- Start end-to-end integration test: console -> control plane -> router -> decision event viewer.

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Connor O'Brien (runtime), Alex Martinez (control plane), Hiro Tanaka (perf), Maya Srinivasan (console)
