- [ ] Panels render for all regions with no missing series
- [ ] Streaming dashboards include TTFT + disconnect rate panels
- [ ] Alert annotations include links to: component dashboard + status page update runbook
- [ ] Anti-flap and hysteresis rules tested against a known incident replay window
- [ ] Support/CS can access the read-only “Statuspage Evidence” view (no sensitive drilldowns)

## FAQ
**Q: Why show both burn-rate and raw error/latency?**
A: Burn-rate is the best early indicator for SLO impact; raw signals help explain scope and select the right public component.

**Q: What about Dedicated / Private incidents?**
A: Dashboards should only power customer-visible updates for components that are publicly represented. Dedicated control-plane signals are aggregated and must not reveal customer-specific capacity or tenant-level details. For Private/on-prem, follow the comms guidance and do not use public components unless explicitly applicable.

**Q: What if the dashboards disagree with customer reports?**
A: Follow the escalation playbook (Support/CS → SRE evidence review). Do not “force green” or “force red” without documented evidence and a clear scope statement.

## Appendix: Minimum component set (current)
The exact component IDs and display names are defined in the status page component taxonomy and registry. Dashboards must exist for each component/region combination that appears publicly.

Key categories:
- Hosted API (chat/completions; non-streaming)
- Streaming
- Embeddings
- Reranking
- Console
- Dedicated control-plane (signals only)

For questions or change requests, file a ticket under the Status Page Transparency Upgrades epic and tag Observability + SRE.

title: cross-stack-resilience-architectural-patterns-and-training-path
preview:
Overview:
This page defines a cross-stack failure-mode taxonomy, prescriptive architectural patterns to contain or degrade safely, and a concrete operator training pathway. It is intended for platform engineers, oncall operators, SREs, and platform product owners responsible for inference availability and cost-sensitive SLOs.

Purpose:
- Establish a common language for multi-layer failures (network, infra, runtime, model, data) so responders can triage quickly.
- Provide tested architectural mitigations and dashboard/alert specs that reduce mean time to repair (MTTR) and customer impact.
- Outline a progressive training program and gamedays to raise operator readiness.

Scope:
- Applies to Redwood-hosted runtime stacks (serving runtime, model runtime-proxy, KV cache, batcher, traffic router).
- Also covers interactions with Dedicated and Private control planes (where behavior differs).
- Does NOT cover end-user application logic beyond ingress/egress contracts.

Failure-mode taxonomy (high level):
1) Latency storm (queue buildup + batching amplification)
   - Symptoms: rising request queue length, median batch size jumps, p95/p99 token latency increases while throughput stalls.
   - Common root causes: misconfigured batching policy, downstream KV cache slowness, GPU thermal/reduced frequency.
   - Example impact: customer A sees 4x p99, adaptive routing kicks but fallback capacity exhausted.

2) Token-error cascade (model or runtime leading to 5xx tokenization errors)
   - Symptoms: spike in 5xx errors at model-runtime, failed tokenization logs, increased client retries.
   - Root causes: model artifact corruption, kernel ABI mismatch after runtime update, quantization rollout regressions.

3) Partial KV cache corruption / shard misroute
   - Symptoms: high cold-cache ratio for a subset of prefixes, increased upstream model compute, tenant-specific cost spikes.
   - Root causes: shard rebalance bug, replication lag, operator-initiated vacuum without backfill.

4) Regional egress/network partition
   - Symptoms: cross-region replication lag, request success but metadata failures (audit logging drops), increased tail-latency for region fallbacks.

5) Capacity depletion due to noisy tenant or runaway job
   - Symptoms: sudden surge of tokens from single tenant, eviction churn, preemptible pool exhaustion.

Architectural patterns (prescriptive):
- Multi-tier fallback: always configure three compatible model variants (primary, cheap-quantized fallback, ultracheap routing) and declare strict compatibility matrix in the model-catalog.
- Prefix-aware KV caching: implement per-tenant/KV-prefix rate-limits, and a fast-path for critical prefixes (SLA-flagged) with higher cache retention.
- Adaptive batching with locality hints: batcher should prefer requests from the same region and tenant when possible; degrade to single-request fast-path when p95 exceeds threshold.
- Graceful shedding: soft-throttling tokens per request (max_tokens cap override) for tenants exceeding burst credits; perform credit checks before enqueuing long generations.
- Observability contract: every request must carry trace_id, tenant_id, model_variant, and cost_estimate tags. Logging must emit structured fields for these.

Detection & triage (playbook):
1) Automated detectors (what to watch):
   - Queue-length anomaly: alert when global enqueue > 120s of sustained growth and per-pool queue > 50% capacity for 10m.
   - KV cold-hit-rate: alert when any shard cold-hit% increases > 30% vs baseline for 5m.
   - Model-error surge: alert p10->p90 error rate multiplier > 10x within 2m.

2) Initial triage steps (operator):
   - Grab the incident context bundle (link in alert).
   - Check flow: ingress -> router -> batcher -> runtime -> GPU pool -> model-runtime. Confirm which hop shows amplification.
   - Correlate with deploy events (CI/CD), kernel updates, scheduled maintenance, or region failover.

3) Rapid mitigations (ordered):
   - Activate read-only degradation: set max_tokens=128 globally for affected pool.
   - Force fallback to quantized variant: redwoodctl model swap --service <pool> --variant <fallback> (see Appendix).
   - Isolate noisy tenant: apply temporary per-tenant throttle via OPA policy.

Operator runbook excerpts (commands & checks):
- Inspect batching health (promql):
  rate(batcher_enqueue_total[1m]) by (pool)
  hist_quantile(0.95, batcher_processing_latency_seconds_bucket{pool="runtime-ggpu"})
- Swap model variant (safe path):
  # dry-run to validate compatibility
  redwoodctl model-variant check --model-id m-redwood/llama-x --variant v2-q8
  # perform swap with rolling windows
  redwoodctl model-variant swap --pool runtime-ggpu-prod --from v2.1 --to v2-q8 --canary 5% --timeout 60s
- Isolate tenant via OPA gate:
  opa apply policy restrict-tenant --tenant-id tenant-123 --max-qps 5
- KV cache backfill preview:
  kvtool shard-stats --shard 17 --since 60m
  kvtool backfill --shard 17 --mode progressive --max-ops-per-sec 200

Dashboards & alert spec (dashboard pages and critical panels):
- Dashboards to create/maintain:
  1) Cross-Stack Resilience Overview (single pane of glass)
     - Panels: global enqueue, per-pool p50/p95/p99 latency, global batch size distribution, global model-error-rate, KV cold-hit ratio.
  2) Tenant Impact & Cost View
     - Panels: top-10 tenants by token-rate, anomaly flag, projected token-cost burn vs commitment.
  3) Model Variant Health
     - Panels: per-variant error-rate, average response length, quantization-failure events.

- Alert rules (examples):
  - P0: model-error-surge
    - Trigger: model_runtime_5xx_rate increase > 10x sustained for 2m AND p99 latency > 1s
    - Action: page oncall SRE, runbook link, auto-failover to quantized fallback (if configured).
  - P1: queue-amplification
    - Trigger: pool_enqueue_len > 0.5 * pool_capacity for 10m AND batcher_p95 > 300ms
    - Action: notify platform-oncall, create incident, consider soft-throttles.
  - P2: kv-hotspot
    - Trigger: kv_shard_cold_hit_rate delta > +35% over baseline for 5m
    - Action: create ticket to cache-team, run shard rehydrate flow if safe.

SLOs and thresholds (recommendations):
- Overview SLOs (global):
  - Token-p95 latency <= 250ms (SLO objective)
  - Token-p99 latency <= 800ms
  - System-level 5xx error rate <= 0.1% per 30d window
  - Percent of requests served by primary variant >= 98% under normal load

- Alert thresholds should bias toward avoiding noisy paging: use multi-window rules (e.g., 1m spike ignored unless 5m sustained).

Training & operator skillmap (progressive path):
- Levels and expected competencies:
  - Level 1 (platform responder): understands dashboards, can perform variant swaps, isolate tenants, run cached backfills. (1-week onboarding lab)
  - Level 2 (platform engineer): can author OPA throttle rules, adjust batching knobs, and interpret kernel/runtime traces. (2-week lab + shadowing)
  - Level 3 (platform incident lead): capable of multi-region failover orchestration, design temporary routing policies, and run postmortems. (mentor-led gameday)

- Gameday program (quarterly):
  - Scenario A: KV-shard-corruption (operator must rehydrate and restore SLO within 45m).
  - Scenario B: Noisy-tenant token surge (operator must isolate and maintain 90% of other tenants' SLOs).
  - Scenario C: Quantization rollout misfire (operator must rollback and validate canary path).

Roadmap (12–24 months):
- Q2 2026: implement per-tenant fast-path cache with SLA flags and dedicated retention policy.
- Q4 2026: automated model-variant canary promotion based on live quality and cost signals.
- H1 2027: operator training portal with scenario replay and synthetic incident generator.

Ownership & escalation matrix (example):
- Serving runtime (batcher, runtime-proxy): owner team = eng-serving-runtime, primary oncall = runtime-oncall@redwood.com.
- KV cache (prefix-store): owner team = eng-platform-cache, primary oncall = cache-oncall@redwood.com.
- Model catalog & rollout: owner team = eng-developer-experience, primary oncall = modelops-oncall@redwood.com.

Appendix: diagnostic snippets and links
- Prometheus examples (copy into Alerting rules):
  - queue_alert: rate(batcher_enqueue_total[5m]) > 100
  - p99_latency: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(runtime_response_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)) > 0.8

- Safe rollback checklist:
  1) Pause automated rollouts for affected model-id.
  2) Promote last-known-good variant as canary at 10%.
  3) Observe model_error_rate and p99 for 10m.
  4) If stable, roll forward remaining 90% in 10% steps every 5m.

References and related playbooks:
- See also: Observability-driven traffic shaping and fallbacks playbook, Emergency model swap and token surge mitigation runbook (links above).

Document maintenance:
- Owners: platform-architecture (primary), eng-serving-runtime (secondary).
- Review cadence: quarterly or after any P0 incident; update labels and thresholds based on retrospective outcomes.

Last reviewed: 2026-01-18 by Diego Morales, Priya Raman.

title: Operator-focused telemetry partitioning and lightweight agent playbook
preview:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes a low-risk, operator-focused approach to partitioning runtime telemetry for cost attribution and operator workflows. It prescribes a lightweight in-namespace agent pattern, concrete dashboard and alert definitions, and an operational rollout plan. The intent is to make cost signals actionable for operators while keeping cardinality and storage growth under control.

Goals
-----
- Provide operators with per-tenant and per-model cost telemetry that explains high-cost events within 5 minutes of occurrence.
- Keep high-cardinality labels out of hot Prometheus collections; use a partitioned timeseries + event-log pattern.
- Ensure safe rollout with feature flag, manual operator guardrails, and gradual backfill.

Scope
-----
- Applies to ingested runtime metrics (token counts, model selection, KV cache hits, batch sizes) and derived cost estimates.
- Does NOT change billing pipelines or accounting ledgers; cost outputs are advisory and tagged as "estimated".
- Targets hosted and Dedicated clusters initially; Private on-prem adoption follows after 2 quarters.

Owner and responsibilities
-------------------------
- Platform Observability: primary owner for dashboards and PromQL/monitoring query templates.
- Platform Operators: responsibility for agent rollout, runbook execution, and incident mitigations.
- Runtime Team: owns the telemetry schema and cardinality budget.
- Cost Insights team (cross-functional): validates cost model and sampling heuristics.

Data model and partitioning strategy
----------------------------------
Summary: emit low-cardinality base metrics to the central Prometheus (or Cortex) and stream detailed per-tenant events to a partitioned event store (ClickHouse/BigQuery). Use a deterministic shard key (region:model_family:customer_bucket) to keep partition counts bounded.

Key labels retained in central metrics (low cardinality):
- region
- model_family (e.g., gptx-1, redwood-rlf)
- environment (prod/staging)
- tenant_bucket (small integer bucket derived from tenant_id, see bucketing rules)

High-cardinality labels streamed as events:
- tenant_id (raw)
- api_key_id
- model_variant_id
- request_id
- input_token_count, output_token_count

Bucketing rules (operator-facing):
1) Hash tenant_id into 256 buckets: bucket = fnv32(tenant_id) % 256.
2) For high-spike tenants (>= 95th percentile cost in a week) create an exception and route to a dedicated partition.
3) Limit partition count per region to 512.

Metrics, dashboards and example queries
-------------------------------------
Essential metrics exposed in central Prometheus (name conventions):
- runtime_request_rate_total{region,model_family,tenant_bucket} (counter)
- runtime_token_output_total{region,model_family,tenant_bucket} (counter)
- prefix_kv_cache_hit_ratio{region,model_family} (gauge)
- runtime_p95_latency_ms{region,model_family} (histogram/summary)
- runtime_batch_size_avg{region,model_family} (gauge)

Example PromQL panels (for an operator dashboard):
- Global token burn rate (tokens per minute): rate(runtime_token_output_total[2m])
- Top model families by token rate (last 5m): topk(10, sum by (model_family) (rate(runtime_token_output_total[5m])))
- Tenant-bucket cost estimate (5m window): sum by (tenant_bucket) (rate(runtime_token_output_total[5m]) * on(model_family) group_left(cost_per_token_cents) cost_per_token_cents)

Note: cost_per_token_cents is maintained as a separate time series with model_family label and updated via a config pipeline.

Detailed event store queries (examples for ClickHouse / BigQuery for root-cause):
- Query: find spikes where tenant_id emitted > 1M tokens in 15 minutes.
  SELECT tenant_id, SUM(output_token_count) as tokens_15m
  FROM runtime_events
  WHERE ingest_time >= TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 15 MINUTE)
  GROUP BY tenant_id
  HAVING tokens_15m > 1000000
  ORDER BY tokens_15m DESC

- Query: model_variant churn during spike: SELECT model_variant_id, COUNT(*) FROM runtime_events WHERE tenant_id = 'T-123' AND ingest_time BETWEEN X AND Y GROUP BY model_variant_id ORDER BY 2 DESC

Alerting strategy and concrete rules
----------------------------------
We recommend two alert tiers: operator-actionable and escalation. All alerts include a "cost impact" estimate field using the central token-rate metric multiplied by cost_per_token.

Recommended alert definitions (examples):
1) tenant-bucket-token-spike
- Trigger: tenant_bucket token rate > 10x baseline (baseline = rolling median of last 6 hours) for 5 minutes.
- Severity: warning
- Operator action: check event store for tenant_id; if spike is legitimate, tag as "expected-burst" for 24 hours; otherwise follow mitigation.

2) steady-high-cost-tenant
- Trigger: tenant_bucket 24h cost estimate > $2,000 and growth rate > 30% versus previous 24h.
- Severity: ticket
- Operator action: open cost investigation ticket with Cost Insights and notify solutions engineering.

3) cardinality-budget-exceeded
- Trigger: distinct label count for tenant_bucket or model_variant exceeds configured threshold for region.
- Severity: critical
- Operator action: throttle ingestion for detailed events to 10% sampling and notify Runtime Team.

Runbook: incident triage workflow (operator-facing)
------------------------------------------------
1) Detect: alert fires with link to operator dashboard and top-10 suspect buckets.
2) Quick triage (5 minutes): check cost estimate, top models, recent deploys, and customer-initiated bulk uploads.
3) If spike is unexplained, escalate to mitigation: temporarily reduce detailed-event sampling to 1% for affected tenant_bucket and enable token-rate circuit-breaker for tenant_bucket (see controls below).
4) If spike is due to deploy or test traffic, tag the incident and update customer-facing runway.
5) After stabilization, schedule postmortem and re-run backfill for sampled windows to reconstruct full detail (backfill plan below).

Mitigation controls (operator knobs)
----------------------------------
- sampling_rate_override{tenant_bucket}: reduces detailed-event stream percentage.
- token_rate_soft_limit{tenant_bucket}: when exceeded, responses switch to reduced batch-size policy and operator message is injected for customer-facing routes.
- dedicated-tenant-route: route tenant to a lower-cost model variant until human review.

Agent pattern and deployment guide
--------------------------------
Rationale: a small, single-purpose agent running in each runtime namespace collects request metadata and forwards events to the event store. It avoids embedding high-card labels in central metrics and provides resilient buffering.

Agent responsibilities:
- Read minimal request context from request router (request_id, model_variant_id, tenant_id hash)
- Compute derived fields (tenant_bucket, estimated_cost)
- Emit central low-card metrics via PushGateway or metrics bridge
- Batch and forward detailed events to ClickHouse/GCS with backpressure and retry logic

Lightweight agent example (conceptual YAML for k8s DaemonSet):
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: telemetry-agent
  namespace: runtime-ops
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: telemetry-agent
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: telemetry-agent
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: agent
        image: ghcr.io/redwood/telemetry-agent:stable-2026-01
        env:
          - name: EVENT_STORE_ENDPOINT
            value: https://clickhouse.internal:8443
          - name: BUCKET_COUNT
            value: "256"
        resources:
          limits:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "256Mi"

Operational safety notes:
- Agent must support a dry-run mode that only logs events (no forwarding) for 48 hours during rollout.
- Agents must honor global operator feature-flag (platform.telemetry.partitioning.enable) toggles.

Backfill and sampling plan
-------------------------
- During rollout, store sampled events at 10% by default for 7 days. After 2 weeks, increase to 25% for tenants identified as high variance.
- Backfill strategy: for an incident window where sampling was active, reconstruct full counts by scaling sampled totals by 1/sampling_rate and validating against central token counters. Backfill requires coordination with Cost Insights to avoid double-counting.

Rollout checklist (safe gate steps)
----------------------------------
1) Merge telemetry schema changes behind feature flag.
2) Deploy agent in dry-run mode to a canary region and collect logs for 48 hours.
3) Enable forwarding for 5% sampling in canary region for 72 hours.
4) Validate dashboards and verify cardinality metrics remain within targets.
5) Increase to 10% sampling account-wide and enable tenant_bucket partitioning.
6) After 30 days, raise sampling for selected tenants and enable automated backfill pipelines.

SLOs and operational thresholds
------------------------------
- Freshness: cost estimate panels must reflect new tokens within 5 minutes (95th percentile).
- Accuracy: estimated cost error vs backfilled ground truth <= 6% for 95% of tenant-week pairs.
- Cardinality: distinct tenant_bucket*model_family series should not exceed 50k per region.

Observability of the observability system
---------------------------------------
Key health signals to surface about the system itself:
- agent_forwarder_retry_rate
- event_store_ingest_lag_seconds (95th)
- partition_storage_growth_bytes
- sampling_rate_applied_by_tenant_count

Retrospective and post-rollout review items
------------------------------------------
- Run a 6-week retrospective after account-wide rollout. Focus questions:
  * Did operators reduce time-to-meaningful-action for cost incidents?
  * Were there unexpected cardinality bursts or tagging mismatches?
  * Was the cost estimate model adequate for large model-variant changes?
- Collect anonymized operator feedback via a short survey and incorporate into Q3 roadmap.

Roadmap and follow-ups (next 12 months)
--------------------------------------
- Q1: Add automatic anomaly scoring for tenant spikes using a lightweight ML model in the event pipeline.
- Q2: Replace manual bucketing with adaptive buckets (merge/split) based on tenant behavior.
- Q3: Extend agent to on-prem packages and provide an optional local event-backfill utility for air-gapped customers.
- Q4: Integrate cost telemetry with account management UI and automated usage caps.

Appendix: quick operator commands and queries
-------------------------------------------
- Force sampling override for bucket 42 (CLI):
  redwood-platformctl telemetry sampling set --bucket 42 --rate 0.01 --reason "emergency-throttle"

- Inspect recent high-cost tenants (promql): topk(20, sum by (tenant_bucket) (rate(runtime_token_output_total[15m])))

- Example incident template to open a ticket:
  Summary: Unexplained token spike for tenant_bucket 42 on 2026-01-14
  Impact: Estimated $3,200 in 15 minutes
  Immediate action taken: sampling rate reduced to 1%, token_rate_soft_limit applied
  Owner: oncall-platform-operators

Contact and escalation
----------------------
- Platform Observability oncall: #platform-observability (Slack)
- Runtime escalation: runtime-leads@redwood.ai
- Cost Insights: cost-insights@redwood.ai

This playbook should be reviewed quarterly and updated with lessons from postmortems and operator feedback.
text:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes a low-risk, operator-focused approach to partitioning runtime telemetry for cost attribution and operator workflows. It prescribes a lightweight in-namespace agent pattern, concrete dashboard and alert definitions, and an operational rollout plan. The intent is to make cost signals actionable for operators while keeping cardinality and storage growth under control.

Goals
-----
- Provide operators with per-tenant and per-model cost telemetry that explains high-cost events within 5 minutes of occurrence.
- Keep high-cardinality labels out of hot Prometheus collections; use a partitioned timeseries + event-log pattern.
- Ensure safe rollout with feature flag, manual operator guardrails, and gradual backfill.

Scope
-----
- Applies to ingested runtime metrics (token counts, model selection, KV cache hits, batch sizes) and derived cost estimates.
- Does NOT change billing pipelines or accounting ledgers; cost outputs are advisory and tagged as "estimated".
- Targets hosted and Dedicated clusters initially; Private on-prem adoption follows after 2 quarters.

Owner and responsibilities
-------------------------
- Platform Observability: primary owner for dashboards and PromQL/monitoring query templates.
- Platform Operators: responsibility for agent rollout, runbook execution, and incident mitigations.
- Runtime Team: owns the telemetry schema and cardinality budget.
- Cost Insights team (cross-functional): validates cost model and sampling heuristics.

Data model and partitioning strategy
----------------------------------
Summary: emit low-cardinality base metrics to the central Prometheus (or Cortex) and stream detailed per-tenant events to a partitioned event store (ClickHouse/BigQuery). Use a deterministic shard key (region:model_family:customer_bucket) to keep partition counts bounded.

Key labels retained in central metrics (low cardinality):
- region
- model_family (e.g., gptx-1, redwood-rlf)
- environment (prod/staging)
- tenant_bucket (small integer bucket derived from tenant_id, see bucketing rules)

High-cardinality labels streamed as events:
- tenant_id (raw)
- api_key_id
- model_variant_id
- request_id
- input_token_count, output_token_count

Bucketing rules (operator-facing):
1) Hash tenant_id into 256 buckets: bucket = fnv32(tenant_id) % 256.
2) For high-spike tenants (>= 95th percentile cost in a week) create an exception and route to a dedicated partition.
3) Limit partition count per region to 512.

Metrics, dashboards and example queries
-------------------------------------
Essential metrics exposed in central Prometheus (name conventions):
- runtime_request_rate_total{region,model_family,tenant_bucket} (counter)
- runtime_token_output_total{region,model_family,tenant_bucket} (counter)
- prefix_kv_cache_hit_ratio{region,model_family} (gauge)
- runtime_p95_latency_ms{region,model_family} (histogram/summary)
- runtime_batch_size_avg{region,model_family} (gauge)

Example PromQL panels (for an operator dashboard):
- Global token burn rate (tokens per minute): rate(runtime_token_output_total[2m])
- Top model families by token rate (last 5m): topk(10, sum by (model_family) (rate(runtime_token_output_total[5m])))
- Tenant-bucket cost estimate (5m window): sum by (tenant_bucket) (rate(runtime_token_output_total[5m]) * on(model_family) group_left(cost_per_token_cents) cost_per_token_cents)

Note: cost_per_token_cents is maintained as a separate time series with model_family label and updated via a config pipeline.

Detailed event store queries (examples for ClickHouse / BigQuery for root-cause):
- Query: find spikes where tenant_id emitted > 1M tokens in 15 minutes.
  SELECT tenant_id, SUM(output_token_count) as tokens_15m
  FROM runtime_events
  WHERE ingest_time >= TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 15 MINUTE)
  GROUP BY tenant_id
  HAVING tokens_15m > 1000000
  ORDER BY tokens_15m DESC

- Query: model_variant churn during spike: SELECT model_variant_id, COUNT(*) FROM runtime_events WHERE tenant_id = 'T-123' AND ingest_time BETWEEN X AND Y GROUP BY model_variant_id ORDER BY 2 DESC

Alerting strategy and concrete rules
----------------------------------
We recommend two alert tiers: operator-actionable and escalation. All alerts include a "cost impact" estimate field using the central token-rate metric multiplied by cost_per_token.

Recommended alert definitions (examples):
1) tenant-bucket-token-spike
- Trigger: tenant_bucket token rate > 10x baseline (baseline = rolling median of last 6 hours) for 5 minutes.
- Severity: warning
- Operator action: check event store for tenant_id; if spike is legitimate, tag as "expected-burst" for 24 hours; otherwise follow mitigation.

2) steady-high-cost-tenant
- Trigger: tenant_bucket 24h cost estimate > $2,000 and growth rate > 30% versus previous 24h.
- Severity: ticket
- Operator action: open cost investigation ticket with Cost Insights and notify solutions engineering.

3) cardinality-budget-exceeded
- Trigger: distinct label count for tenant_bucket or model_variant exceeds configured threshold for region.
- Severity: critical
- Operator action: throttle ingestion for detailed events to 10% sampling and notify Runtime Team.

Runbook: incident triage workflow (operator-facing)
------------------------------------------------
1) Detect: alert fires with link to operator dashboard and top-10 suspect buckets.
2) Quick triage (5 minutes): check cost estimate, top models, recent deploys, and customer-initiated bulk uploads.
3) If spike is unexplained, escalate to mitigation: temporarily reduce detailed-event sampling to 1% for affected tenant_bucket and enable token-rate circuit-breaker for tenant_bucket (see controls below).
4) If spike is due to deploy or test traffic, tag the incident and update customer-facing runway.
5) After stabilization, schedule postmortem and re-run backfill for sampled windows to reconstruct full detail (backfill plan below).

Mitigation controls (operator knobs)
----------------------------------
- sampling_rate_override{tenant_bucket}: reduces detailed-event stream percentage.
- token_rate_soft_limit{tenant_bucket}: when exceeded, responses switch to reduced batch-size policy and operator message is injected for customer-facing routes.
- dedicated-tenant-route: route tenant to a lower-cost model variant until human review.

Agent pattern and deployment guide
--------------------------------
Rationale: a small, single-purpose agent running in each runtime namespace collects request metadata and forwards events to the event store. It avoids embedding high-card labels in central metrics and provides resilient buffering.

Agent responsibilities:
- Read minimal request context from request router (request_id, model_variant_id, tenant_id hash)
- Compute derived fields (tenant_bucket, estimated_cost)
- Emit central low-card metrics via PushGateway or metrics bridge
- Batch and forward detailed events to ClickHouse/GCS with backpressure and retry logic

Lightweight agent example (conceptual YAML for k8s DaemonSet):
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: telemetry-agent
  namespace: runtime-ops
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: telemetry-agent
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: telemetry-agent
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: agent
        image: ghcr.io/redwood/telemetry-agent:stable-2026-01
        env:
          - name: EVENT_STORE_ENDPOINT
            value: https://clickhouse.internal:8443
          - name: BUCKET_COUNT
            value: "256"
        resources:
          limits:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "256Mi"

Operational safety notes:
- Agent must support a dry-run mode that only logs events (no forwarding) for 48 hours during rollout.
- Agents must honor global operator feature-flag (platform.telemetry.partitioning.enable) toggles.

Backfill and sampling plan
-------------------------
- During rollout, store sampled events at 10% by default for 7 days. After 2 weeks, increase to 25% for tenants identified as high variance.
- Backfill strategy: for an incident window where sampling was active, reconstruct full counts by scaling sampled totals by 1/sampling_rate and validating against central token counters. Backfill requires coordination with Cost Insights to avoid double-counting.

Rollout checklist (safe gate steps)
----------------------------------
1) Merge telemetry schema changes behind feature flag.
2) Deploy agent in dry-run mode to a canary region and collect logs for 48 hours.
3) Enable forwarding for 5% sampling in canary region for 72 hours.
4) Validate dashboards and verify cardinality metrics remain within targets.
5) Increase to 10% sampling account-wide and enable tenant_bucket partitioning.
6) After 30 days, raise sampling for selected tenants and enable automated backfill pipelines.

SLOs and operational thresholds
------------------------------
- Freshness: cost estimate panels must reflect new tokens within 5 minutes (95th percentile).
- Accuracy: estimated cost error vs backfilled ground truth <= 6% for 95% of tenant-week pairs.
- Cardinality: distinct tenant_bucket*model_family series should not exceed 50k per region.

Observability of the observability system
---------------------------------------
Key health signals to surface about the system itself:
- agent_forwarder_retry_rate
- event_store_ingest_lag_seconds (95th)
- partition_storage_growth_bytes
- sampling_rate_applied_by_tenant_count

Retrospective and post-rollout review items
------------------------------------------
- Run a 6-week retrospective after account-wide rollout. Focus questions:
  * Did operators reduce time-to-meaningful-action for cost incidents?
  * Were there unexpected cardinality bursts or tagging mismatches?
  * Was the cost estimate model adequate for large model-variant changes?
- Collect anonymized operator feedback via a short survey and incorporate into Q3 roadmap.

Roadmap and follow-ups (next 12 months)
--------------------------------------
- Q1: Add automatic anomaly scoring for tenant spikes using a lightweight ML model in the event pipeline.
- Q2: Replace manual bucketing with adaptive buckets (merge/split) based on tenant behavior.
- Q3: Extend agent to on-prem packages and provide an optional local event-backfill utility for air-gapped customers.
- Q4: Integrate cost telemetry with account management UI and automated usage caps.

Appendix: quick operator commands and queries
-------------------------------------------
- Force sampling override for bucket 42 (CLI):
  redwood-platformctl telemetry sampling set --bucket 42 --rate 0.01 --reason "emergency-throttle"

- Inspect recent high-cost tenants (promql): topk(20, sum by (tenant_bucket) (rate(runtime_token_output_total[15m])))

- Example incident template to open a ticket:
  Summary: Unexplained token spike for tenant_bucket 42 on 2026-01-14
  Impact: Estimated $3,200 in 15 minutes
  Immediate action taken: sampling rate reduced to 1%, token_rate_soft_limit applied
  Owner: oncall-platform-operators

Contact and escalation
----------------------
- Platform Observability oncall: #platform-observability (Slack)
- Runtime escalation: runtime-leads@redwood.ai
- Cost Insights: cost-insights@redwood.ai

This playbook should be reviewed quarterly and updated with lessons from postmortems and operator feedback.

title: Project Orion — Weekly status update (2026-01-16)
preview:
# Summary
Orion is progressing, but we have an emerging integration risk with the runtime router path for fallback decisions.

# Status
- Overall: At Risk
- RAG: Red

# Progress this week
- Policy engine skeleton merged behind a feature flag (orion_policy_engine_enabled).
- Defined fallback precedence rules for model variants (pinning > compatibility > cost).

# Risks / blockers
- Runtime router currently applies fallback in a different code path than the control plane expects; we may need a refactor or a shim layer.
- Timeline risk: if refactor is required, console beta slips by 1–2 weeks.

# Mitigations
- Schedule a 90-minute design review with runtime + platform owners.
- Implement a temporary adapter that maps router decisions into Orion policy evaluation events.

# Next week
- Prototype adapter in staging and validate event schema.

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (platform), Connor OBrien (runtime routing), Maya Srinivasan (console)

Project Orion is the cross-team effort to unify rollout controls (canary, A/B, and automatic fallback) into a single policy surface in the Redwood Console and routing layer.

# Status

- Eng: Alex Martinez (platform), Maya Srinivasan (console)
