| field | description | example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| name | scenario id | kv-prefix-saturation-202511 |
| duration | planned wallclock | 00:20:00 |
| traffic_profile | request generator name | prefix-heavy-replay-v2 |
| resource_stress | list of stressors | gpu-stress:2, fio-iops:1000 |
| observability_tag | trace tag prefix | sim-run/kv-202511 |
| operator_checklist | runbook ids to exercise | RB-011, RB-023 |

Deployment and sandboxing steps (operator checklist):

1) Create sandbox namespace: platform-sim-<run-id>.
2) Apply scenario manifest with simctl apply -f <manifest.yaml>.
3) Verify observability pipeline has registered sim-run/<run-id> traces.
4) Wait for warmup period (default 2 minutes) then advance to injection phase with simctl inject --phase=1.
5) Monitor dashboards; if auto-safeguard trips, document at what timestamp it activated.
6) Run mitigation steps from the linked runbook.
7) Tear down with simctl teardown --force and collect artifacts.

Dashboards and alerting required for meaningful runs:

- Simulation overview dashboard (templated): shows active runs, phase, run-id, last-heartbeat.
- Multi-signal correlation dashboard: KV cache hit-rate, batch queue depth, per-tenant token latency, GPU utilization, model-swap rates.
- Alerts to validate: 
  - sim:primary-detection (SLO: fire when cache hit-rate drops > 15% and queue depth > threshold for 60s)
  - sim:cost-burn (SLO: fire when cost-per-token > 2x baseline for 5m)
  - sim:auto-safeguard (non-actionable alert used to validate automation trigger)

Example alert rule (pseudo-YAML):

rule: sim:primary-detection
expr: (kv_hit_rate{env="sim"} < 0.65 and batch_queue_depth > 200)
for: 1m
labels:
  severity: critical
annotations:
  runbook: RB-011
  description: "Primary detection for simulated KV cache saturation ({{ $labels.run_id }})"

Operator training curriculum (4-week rotation template):

Week 0: Prep and onboarding
- Read this playbook and run the dry-run tutorial.
- Complete the simulator quickstart (local mode).

Week 1: Detection and Triage drills
- Exercises: KV saturation, batching spike.
- Goals: surface triage steps, dashboard readouts, and signal correlation.

Week 2: Mitigation choreography
- Exercises: model fallback rollback, cache-resize.
- Goals: complete manual mitigation and practice automation handovers.

Week 3: Automation and Postmortem
- Exercises: enable/disable auto-safeguards, run retrospective runbook.
- Goals: confirm run-archive completeness, artifact retrieval, and postmortem write-up.

Assessment rubric (pass/fail and scoring):

- Detection time (40%): <120s = full points, 120–300s = partial, >300s = fail.
- Correct mitigation (40%): all required actions performed in sequence without missed steps.
- Documentation quality (20%): clear artifacts, run-archive links, and lessons.

Runbook integration checklist (for runbook owners):

- Link runbook IDs used in scenario manifest to runbook pages.
- Ensure runbook has an operator checklist with explicit CLI/console commands and expected telemetry.
- Add a short preface \"simulation-tested\" with the last test date.

Post-run retrospective template (to be completed within 48h):

1) Run summary: short description of scenario and run id.
2) Timeline: injection time, detection time, mitigation start, mitigation end.
3) Gaps found: dashboards that missed signals, flapping automations, noise.
4) Action items: owner, ETA, risk, and acceptance criteria.

Retention and artifacts:

- Artifacts stored in s3://platform-sim-archive/<year>/<run-id> including: run-manifest.json, traces (jaeger), raw logs (gz), benchmark CSVs.
- Retention policy: 365 days by default; critical-run artifacts marked for 3-year retention.

Safety and guardrails:

- Sandbox network egress disabled by default.
- All synthetic traffic is tagged and rate-limited; traffic generators include kill-switch endpoints.
- Only operators in group platform-sim-admins can run destructive scenarios.

Oncall play path (how to escalate during live production discoveries triggered by simulation):

1) If a simulation accidentally impacts prod (unlikely but possible): immediately run simctl teardown --force and notify SRE incident channel.
2) Follow the Production Impact Emergency Checklist (linked RB-900).

Implementation roadmap (next 12 months):

- Q1 2025: ship simctl v0.1, create three canonical scenario manifests, build the simulation overview dashboard.
- Q2 2025: integrate sim-run tags into trace sampler, hook run-archive retention policy.
- Q3 2025: operator training pilot across two oncall rotations. Measure detection fidelity.
- Q4 2025: automate basic mitigations and gate auto-safeguard playbook behind runbook approval.

Metrics to track for program health:

- simulation_runs_per_month
- detection_time_p50_p95
- percent_runs_with_complete_artifacts
- operator_pass_rate_after-training

Appendices:

A) Minimal sim manifest example:

---
name: kv-prefix-saturation-example
duration: 00:15:00
traffic_profile: prefix-heavy-replay-v2
resource_stress: [gpu-stress:1]
observability_tag: sim-run/kv-example
operator_checklist: [RB-011]
---

B) Common CLI commands:

- simctl apply -f <manifest.yaml>    # deploys and schedules run
- simctl inject --run <run-id> --phase=1   # advance to injection
- simctl status --run <run-id>   # show run state and phases
- simctl teardown --run <run-id> --force   # immediate cleanup

C) Contacts and escalation paths:

- Platform Operations Pager: #platform-ops (Slack)
- Serving Runtime lead: Diego Morales (diego@redwood.ai)
- SRE oncall: see rota page /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotations

Revision history:

- 2025-02-14: Draft created by Ava Chen.
- 2025-05-08: Added GPU interference scenario and sample alert rule. (Reviewer: Priya Nair)
- 2025-11-09: Polished operator curriculum and retention policy. (Reviewer: Liam O'Sullivan)
text:
Summary:

This playbook establishes a reproducible simulation framework and an operator training curriculum for proactive detection, mitigation, and escalation during multi-signal platform degradations (cache churn, batch spikes, GPU contention, and model fallback churn). The goal is to give operators a deterministic, hands-on safety net for exercising runbooks before incidents occur and to validate dashboard alerting/automations end-to-end.

Why this exists:

- Many incidents are caused by subtle multi-signal interactions that are hard to reproduce in production.
- Operators need repeatable, time-boxed exercises so oncall fatigue and cognitive load can be reduced.
- Verifying dashboards, runbooks, and automation together prevents false confidence in isolated tests.

Scope and non-goals:

- In scope: platform-level degradations that affect inference latency and token-cost SLOs for multi-tenant workloads. Includes KV prefix cache saturation, batching amplification, model-variant fallback storms, and GPU interference scenarios.
- Out of scope: application-layer bugs inside customer code, and multi-region network partitions (handled in a separate DR runbook).

Owners and stakeholders:

- Primary owner: Platform Operations (eng-platform).
- Secondary owners: Serving Runtime, SRE, Developer Experience.
- Stakeholders: Customer Success (for critical tenant scenarios), Product (for SLO changes).

SLOs and success criteria for exercises:

1) Exercise completion criteria: runbook executed within target RTO and operator checklist marked complete.
2) Detection fidelity: dashboards and alerts must surface the primary root-cause signal within 120s of injection 90% of runs.
3) Mitigation effectiveness: automated fallback or manual operator action returns 75th percentile latency within 2x baseline within 15 minutes.

Simulation design overview:

- Architecture: lightweight simulation harness runs in a sandboxed k8s namespace. Each scenario composes workload drivers (request generators), resource signals (GPU stress pods, fio-style disk I/O), and state mutators (cache eviction script, model-swap controller).
- Orchestration: a central experiment controller (small CLI) deploys scenario manifests, advances phases, and records timestamps for key events. The harness replays recorded sequences with deterministic timings to ensure reproducibility.
- Observability hooks: every run attaches a trace id prefixed with sim-run/<run-id> and emits a run manifest to the run-archive S3 bucket for postmortem analysis.

Key scenarios (templates):

1) KV Prefix Cache Saturation
- Trigger: burst of synthetic prefix-heavy traffic that exceeds cache capacity by 3x for 10 minutes.
- Expected signals: KV cache hit-rate drop, token-per-request increase, CPU throttle on cache sharders.
- Operator actions: throttle offending callers, clear high-entropy prefixes, trigger cache-resizing or manual shard rebalance.

2) Batching Amplification Spike
- Trigger: sudden concentration of large-sequence requests from a tenant causing batching queue length growth and tail latency amplification.
- Expected signals: queue length > 95th percentile, token latency tail rises, autoscaler lag.
- Operator actions: apply soft-quotas, enable tenant burst-credit, temporarily pin to smaller model-variant.

3) Model-Variant Fallback Storm
- Trigger: upstream model registry rollout misconfig leads to many requests falling back to slower CPU-quantized variants.
- Expected signals: model-swap events, cross-service error-rate changes, cost-per-token increase.
- Operator actions: rollback rollout, enact emergency model-swap runbook, throttle non-critical routes.

4) GPU Interference and Preemption Mix
- Trigger: scheduled maintenance + spot preemption simulation to force resource balancing events across tenant pools.
- Expected signals: GPU queue stalls, eviction events, tenant throughput loss.
- Operator actions: activate burst-pools, migrate critical tenants to reserved capacity, escalate to capacity-ops.

Runner blueprint (manifest fields):

| field | description | example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| name | scenario id | kv-prefix-saturation-202511 |
| duration | planned wallclock | 00:20:00 |
| traffic_profile | request generator name | prefix-heavy-replay-v2 |
| resource_stress | list of stressors | gpu-stress:2, fio-iops:1000 |
| observability_tag | trace tag prefix | sim-run/kv-202511 |
| operator_checklist | runbook ids to exercise | RB-011, RB-023 |

Deployment and sandboxing steps (operator checklist):

1) Create sandbox namespace: platform-sim-<run-id>.
2) Apply scenario manifest with simctl apply -f <manifest.yaml>.
3) Verify observability pipeline has registered sim-run/<run-id> traces.
4) Wait for warmup period (default 2 minutes) then advance to injection phase with simctl inject --phase=1.
5) Monitor dashboards; if auto-safeguard trips, document at what timestamp it activated.
6) Run mitigation steps from the linked runbook.
7) Tear down with simctl teardown --force and collect artifacts.

Dashboards and alerting required for meaningful runs:

- Simulation overview dashboard (templated): shows active runs, phase, run-id, last-heartbeat.
- Multi-signal correlation dashboard: KV cache hit-rate, batch queue depth, per-tenant token latency, GPU utilization, model-swap rates.
- Alerts to validate: 
  - sim:primary-detection (SLO: fire when cache hit-rate drops > 15% and queue depth > threshold for 60s)
  - sim:cost-burn (SLO: fire when cost-per-token > 2x baseline for 5m)
  - sim:auto-safeguard (non-actionable alert used to validate automation trigger)

Example alert rule (pseudo-YAML):

rule: sim:primary-detection
expr: (kv_hit_rate{env="sim"} < 0.65 and batch_queue_depth > 200)
for: 1m
labels:
  severity: critical
annotations:
  runbook: RB-011
  description: "Primary detection for simulated KV cache saturation ({{ $labels.run_id }})"

Operator training curriculum (4-week rotation template):

Week 0: Prep and onboarding
- Read this playbook and run the dry-run tutorial.
- Complete the simulator quickstart (local mode).

Week 1: Detection and Triage drills
- Exercises: KV saturation, batching spike.
- Goals: surface triage steps, dashboard readouts, and signal correlation.

Week 2: Mitigation choreography
- Exercises: model fallback rollback, cache-resize.
- Goals: complete manual mitigation and practice automation handovers.

Week 3: Automation and Postmortem
- Exercises: enable/disable auto-safeguards, run retrospective runbook.
- Goals: confirm run-archive completeness, artifact retrieval, and postmortem write-up.

Assessment rubric (pass/fail and scoring):

- Detection time (40%): <120s = full points, 120–300s = partial, >300s = fail.
- Correct mitigation (40%): all required actions performed in sequence without missed steps.
- Documentation quality (20%): clear artifacts, run-archive links, and lessons.

Runbook integration checklist (for runbook owners):

- Link runbook IDs used in scenario manifest to runbook pages.
- Ensure runbook has an operator checklist with explicit CLI/console commands and expected telemetry.
- Add a short preface \"simulation-tested\" with the last test date.

Post-run retrospective template (to be completed within 48h):

1) Run summary: short description of scenario and run id.
2) Timeline: injection time, detection time, mitigation start, mitigation end.
3) Gaps found: dashboards that missed signals, flapping automations, noise.
4) Action items: owner, ETA, risk, and acceptance criteria.

Retention and artifacts:

- Artifacts stored in s3://platform-sim-archive/<year>/<run-id> including: run-manifest.json, traces (jaeger), raw logs (gz), benchmark CSVs.
- Retention policy: 365 days by default; critical-run artifacts marked for 3-year retention.

Safety and guardrails:

- Sandbox network egress disabled by default.
- All synthetic traffic is tagged and rate-limited; traffic generators include kill-switch endpoints.
- Only operators in group platform-sim-admins can run destructive scenarios.

Oncall play path (how to escalate during live production discoveries triggered by simulation):

1) If a simulation accidentally impacts prod (unlikely but possible): immediately run simctl teardown --force and notify SRE incident channel.
2) Follow the Production Impact Emergency Checklist (linked RB-900).

Implementation roadmap (next 12 months):

- Q1 2025: ship simctl v0.1, create three canonical scenario manifests, build the simulation overview dashboard.
- Q2 2025: integrate sim-run tags into trace sampler, hook run-archive retention policy.
- Q3 2025: operator training pilot across two oncall rotations. Measure detection fidelity.
- Q4 2025: automate basic mitigations and gate auto-safeguard playbook behind runbook approval.

Metrics to track for program health:

- simulation_runs_per_month
- detection_time_p50_p95
- percent_runs_with_complete_artifacts
- operator_pass_rate_after-training

Appendices:

A) Minimal sim manifest example:

---
name: kv-prefix-saturation-example
duration: 00:15:00
traffic_profile: prefix-heavy-replay-v2
resource_stress: [gpu-stress:1]
observability_tag: sim-run/kv-example
operator_checklist: [RB-011]
---

B) Common CLI commands:

- simctl apply -f <manifest.yaml>    # deploys and schedules run
- simctl inject --run <run-id> --phase=1   # advance to injection
- simctl status --run <run-id>   # show run state and phases
- simctl teardown --run <run-id> --force   # immediate cleanup

C) Contacts and escalation paths:

- Platform Operations Pager: #platform-ops (Slack)
- Serving Runtime lead: Diego Morales (diego@redwood.ai)
- SRE oncall: see rota page /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotations

Revision history:

- 2025-02-14: Draft created by Ava Chen.
- 2025-05-08: Added GPU interference scenario and sample alert rule. (Reviewer: Priya Nair)
- 2025-11-09: Polished operator curriculum and retention policy. (Reviewer: Liam O'Sullivan)

title: Model Registry Changelog Service (MRC): System Overview
preview:
## Summary
The **Model Registry Changelog Service** (service name: **mrc-changelog**) generates and serves **customer-visible** and **internal-only** model release notes from **model metadata change events** and **model version promotion events**.

This service is the canonical source for:
- A **chronological change history** for each served model and variant.
- **Release note artifacts** consumed by:
  - Redwood Console (Release Notes feed, per-model history)
  - Redwood Docs build (public release notes pages + feeds)
  - Support/internal tools (audit-driven investigations)

The service is explicitly designed to:
- Enforce **public vs internal field redaction**.
- Produce consistent categorization and language per the **Customer Release Notes taxonomy/style guide**.
- Provide **idempotent** generation (same events → same outputs) with traceability to schema hashes and eval evidence.

---

## Ownership and on-call
- **Primary owner:** Eng Platform (Console & Tooling)
- **Operational partners:** Release Engineering, Security & Compliance
- **Escalations:**
  - Customer-visible redaction issue → Security Eng reviewer + Console backend EM
  - Docs publishing regressions → DevEx/Docs build owner

**Pager rotation:** `eng-platform-oncall`

---

## Goals / Non-goals
### Goals
1. Generate a **normalized, templated changelog entry** per qualifying event.
2. Maintain an **append-only public feed** and an **append-only internal feed**.
3. Expose APIs for Console and Docs to query:
   - Latest release notes
   - Notes for a model/version
   - Change history with filters (deployment mode, region, severity)
4. Preserve auditability:
   - Who/what/when/where for changes
   - Link to source event IDs and schema versions

### Non-goals (current)
- Customer notification subscriptions (email/webhook) beyond internal webhook plumbing.
- Rewriting all historical release notes (limited backfill only).
- Serving runtime configuration; this service is “read-only” from the perspective of rollout policy.

---

## High-level architecture
### Components
- **Event ingress:** subscribes to Control Plane event stream for model registry changes.
- **Normalizer:** validates and normalizes event payloads against **Model Metadata Schema v1**.
- **Redaction + classification:** splits event details into **public** and **internal** views, assigns taxonomy category + severity.
- **Renderer:** produces human-readable release note text and machine-readable structured payloads.
- **Storage layer:** persists entries, indexes, and processing checkpoints.
- **Serving API:** internal APIs for Console/Docs/Support.

### Data flow (text diagram)
1) Control Plane emits event → 2) Event bus → 3) mrc-changelog consumes → 4) Validate + normalize → 5) Redact + classify → 6) Persist → 7) Publish artifacts → 8) Console/Docs fetch

---

## Inputs
### Event sources
Events are emitted by the Control Plane and Model Registry workflows.

Primary event types:
- `model_metadata.changed`
- `model_version.promoted`
- `model_version.deprecated`
- `model_version.rollback_initiated` (internal-only notes; used to annotate timelines)

### Required event invariants
- Events must include:
  - Stable `event_id` (UUID)
  - `occurred_at` timestamp
  - `actor` (user/service principal)
  - `model_key` (canonical registry key)
  - `model_version` (semantic version string)
  - `deployment_availability` snapshot (Hosted/Dedicated/Private + regions if applicable)
  - `public_change_summary` and/or a structured diff that can be rendered

If a required field is missing, the event is quarantined (see **Failure modes**).

---

## Event schema (canonical)
The changelog service uses a canonical internal envelope, regardless of event transport.

### Envelope (canonical)
```json
{
  "event_id": "0f5b2f1b-89b1-4a90-9b48-6da6c33d7d2a",
  "event_type": "model_version.promoted",
  "occurred_at": "2025-10-23T18:42:11Z",
  "producer": "control-plane",
  "actor": {
    "type": "user|service",
    "id": "u_12345",
    "display_name": "Connor O'Brien"
  },
  "model": {
    "model_key": "redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
    "variant": "base",
    "quantization_profile": "fp16",
    "version": "3.1.4"
  },
  "scope": {
    "deployment_modes": ["hosted", "dedicated"],
    "regions": ["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"],
    "tenant_visibility": "public|internal"
  },
  "metadata": {
    "schema_version": "model-metadata-v1",
    "schema_hash": "sha256:...",
    "tokenizer": "tiktoken-compat@2",
    "runtime": "redwood-runtime@0.19"
  },
  "change": {
    "category": "perf|quality|bugfix|security|breaking|deprecation",
    "severity": "low|medium|high|critical",
    "public_summary": "Improved median latency for typical chat workloads.",
    "internal_notes": "Kernel selection updated; see runbook link.",
    "links": [
      "https://confluence.redwood.inference/...",
      "https://github.com/redwood/..."
    ]
  },
  "evidence": {
    "eval_run_ids": ["eval_9f1..."],
    "baseline_version": "3.1.3",
    "guardrails": "no_regressions_detected"
  }
}
```

### Notes
- `tenant_visibility` controls whether an event can ever produce a public entry. Internal-only events still create internal timeline entries.
- `internal_notes` may contain sensitive implementation details; it is never returned from public endpoints.
- `evidence` fields are optional and subject to redaction rules (statistical claims must be conservative; see taxonomy/style guide).

---

## Outputs
### 1) Persisted changelog entries
For each qualifying event, the service writes a **Changelog Entry** record (public and/or internal view).

Key properties:
- Immutable entry ID
- Stable ordering (by `occurred_at`, tie-broken by `event_id`)
- Links back to:
  - source event (`event_id`)
  - model metadata (`model_key`, `version`, `schema_hash`)
  - evidence (`eval_run_ids`) when allowed

### 2) Release note artifacts (for docs builds)
The service publishes artifacts to object storage for deterministic docs builds.

Bucket: `redwood-changelog-artifacts-prod`
Path format:
- Public: `public/model/{model_key}/version/{version}/{entry_id}.json`
- Internal: `internal/model/{model_key}/version/{version}/{entry_id}.json`

Artifacts are **append-only**; updates require a new entry (e.g., “Correction” note), except for rare redaction hotfix workflows (see **Redaction hotfix**).

---

## APIs
All APIs are internal-to-Redwood services. Customer-facing access is via Console/Docs, not direct calls.

### AuthZ/RBAC
- Console service-to-service auth required.
- RBAC scopes:
  - `changelog.read_public`
  - `changelog.read_internal` (Support/Admin only)
  - `changelog.admin` (break-glass for redaction hotfix)

### REST endpoints (internal)
Base: `https://mrc-changelog.internal.redwood` (env-specific)

1) **List release notes (feed)**
- `GET /v1/release-notes`
- Query params:
  - `visibility=public|internal` (default: public)
  - `deployment_mode=hosted|dedicated|private`
  - `region=us-east-1` (optional)
  - `category=perf|quality|bugfix|security|breaking|deprecation`
  - `severity=low|medium|high|critical`
  - `cursor` / `limit`

2) **Per-model history**
- `GET /v1/models/{model_key}/history`
- Query params:
  - `visibility=public|internal`
  - `from` / `to` timestamps
  - `include_rollout_events=true|false`

3) **Release notes for a specific model version**
- `GET /v1/models/{model_key}/versions/{version}/release-notes`

4) **Artifact fetch helper (docs build)**
- `GET /v1/artifacts/manifest?visibility=public&since=2025-10-01T00:00:00Z`
- Returns a list of artifact URIs + checksums (the docs pipeline uses this to avoid scanning buckets).

### Response shape (conceptual)
Responses include both structured fields (category, severity, affected deployments) and rendered text blocks. The API guarantees:
- Public responses contain **no internal fields**.
- Internal responses include `internal_notes`, source identifiers, and richer links.

---

## Classification + templating
### Category mapping
Classification uses a rule-based mapper with explicit overrides.

Precedence rules (highest wins):
1. `security`
2. `breaking`
3. `deprecation`
4. `quality`
5. `perf`
6. `bugfix`

### Severity guidelines
- **critical:** security hotfix; breaking change with high blast radius; rollback likely
- **high:** observable quality regression risk; major perf changes; wide rollout
- **medium:** moderate perf/quality changes with guardrails
- **low:** minor wording/documentation, metadata-only changes without serving impact

### Rendering templates
Templates produce consistent language and avoid over-claiming:
- Performance notes must specify **direction + scope** (e.g., “median latency improved for typical chat prompts”) and avoid absolute universal claims.
- Quality notes require either:
  - “No statistically significant change detected in Redwood standard eval suite” (if supported), or
  - A conservative description (“Adjusted decoding defaults; some outputs may differ”).

---

## Storage and indexing
### Primary datastore
- **PostgreSQL** (service-owned)
- Tables (logical):
  - `events_ingested` (dedupe + processing state)
  - `changelog_entries_public`
  - `changelog_entries_internal`
  - `processing_checkpoints`
  - `quarantine_events`

### Idempotency
- Primary idempotency key: `event_id`
- Secondary safety: unique constraint on (`model_key`, `version`, `event_type`, `occurred_at`, `schema_hash`) to prevent producer bugs from duplicating entries.

### Retention
- Public entries: retained indefinitely (customer-facing history).
- Internal entries: retained per audit policy (see Security/Compliance doc); minimum **18 months** online, with cold storage beyond.
- Quarantine payloads: retained **30 days** unless escalated.

---

## Failure modes and mitigations
### 1) Event ingestion lag
**Symptoms:** Console feed stale; docs pages missing recent updates.

**Detection:**
- Lag metric: `mrc_changelog_event_consumer_lag_seconds` (warn > 120s, page > 600s)

**Mitigation:**
- Autoscale consumer workers.
- If backlog is caused by poison event, quarantine it (see below).

### 2) Poison / invalid event payload
**Symptoms:** Consumer retries; no progress; repeated failures.

**Mitigation:**
- Validate against canonical schema early.
- Move to `quarantine_events` after N attempts (default 5) with failure reason.
- Emit internal alert with:
  - event_id
  - producer
  - first_seen_at
  - validation error summary

### 3) Redaction regression (internal data leaked)
**Severity:** Critical.

**Preventative controls:**
- Public vs internal rendering paths are separate and unit-tested.
- Snapshot tests for known sensitive fields.
- CI check requires a “public preview” artifact for reviewer approval.

**Response:**
- Immediately disable public artifact publication via feature flag `mrc_public_publish_enabled`.
- Purge affected public artifacts and reissue corrected entries.
- File incident + security review; add regression tests.

### 4) Duplicate / out-of-order events
**Symptoms:** Multiple entries for the same promotion; confusing timeline.

**Mitigation:**
- Enforce idempotency with DB constraints.
- Deterministic ordering by (`occurred_at`, `event_id`).
- If producers emit out-of-order timestamps, the service preserves timestamps but flags internal warning `timestamp_anomaly=true`.

### 5) Partial outage of object storage
**Symptoms:** Console still works (DB reads), docs builds fail to fetch artifacts.

**Mitigation:**
- Docs pipeline can fall back to API manifest and API fetch if artifact store is degraded (rate-limited).
- Retry with exponential backoff; fail docs build with clear error message.

---

## Observability
### Key metrics
- Ingestion:
  - `events_received_total{event_type,producer}`
  - `events_quarantined_total{reason}`
  - `event_consumer_lag_seconds`
- Generation:
  - `entries_created_total{visibility,category,severity}`
  - `render_time_ms`
  - `redaction_denials_total{field}` (counts removed sensitive fields)
- Serving:
  - `api_requests_total{route,status,visibility}`
  - `api_latency_ms_p50/p95/p99`

### Logs
- Structured logs include `event_id`, `model_key`, `version`, `visibility`, and `entry_id`.
- Internal-only logs may include failure details; avoid logging raw internal_notes.

### Tracing
- Propagate trace context from Control Plane event producer where available.

---

## SLOs
- **API availability:** 99.9% monthly (internal consumers)
- **Freshness:** 95% of qualifying events produce a public entry within **5 minutes**
- **Correctness:** 0 tolerance for internal field leakage into public endpoints/artifacts

---

## Operational procedures
### Backfill procedure (limited)
Used to create entries for a small, curated set of recent model promotions.
1. Provide a list of `event_id`s or (`model_key`, `version`, time window) to Release Eng.
2. Run `mrc-changelog backfill --source=eventstore --since=... --until=...` in a controlled environment.
3. Validate public preview artifacts.
4. Publish artifacts and reindex.

### Redaction hotfix (break-glass)
Only for accidental leakage or policy violations.
- Requires approvals: Security Eng reviewer + Eng Platform on-call + Product lead.
- Steps:
  1) Disable public publication
  2) Identify affected artifact URIs
  3) Purge + regenerate entries (or append correction note when possible)
  4) Re-enable publication
  5) Incident write-up and test hardening

---

## Dependency map
- Upstream:
  - Control Plane event emitter (model metadata change + promotion events)
  - Model Metadata Schema v1 validation library
- Downstream:
  - Console backend (release notes feed + model history UI)
  - Docs build pipeline (public release notes pages)
  - Support/Admin audit tooling

---

## Open questions / follow-ups
1. Tenant-specific visibility: how to represent customer-specific or embargoed changes in a forward-compatible way without leaking existence.
2. Private deployments: standard packaging of release note artifacts alongside offline upgrade bundles (tracked in ENG-9829).
3. Quality claims: formalize allowed phrasing based on statistical significance thresholds and eval suite coverage.

---

## Appendix: Example public entry (rendered)
**Category:** Performance (Medium)

**Summary:** Improved median latency for typical chat workloads.

**Details:**
- Updated runtime kernel selection for this model variant.
- No API changes required.

**Availability:** Hosted (us-east-1, eu-west-1); Dedicated rollout follows account policy.

**Rollback:** Pin to prior version (3.1.3) or use automatic fallback policy if configured.

(Internal-only fields such as exact kernel names, internal incident links, and raw diff details are excluded.)
text:
## Summary
The **Model Registry Changelog Service** (service name: **mrc-changelog**) generates and serves **customer-visible** and **internal-only** model release notes from **model metadata change events** and **model version promotion events**.

This service is the canonical source for:
- A **chronological change history** for each served model and variant.
- **Release note artifacts** consumed by:
  - Redwood Console (Release Notes feed, per-model history)
  - Redwood Docs build (public release notes pages + feeds)
  - Support/internal tools (audit-driven investigations)

The service is explicitly designed to:
- Enforce **public vs internal field redaction**.
- Produce consistent categorization and language per the **Customer Release Notes taxonomy/style guide**.
- Provide **idempotent** generation (same events → same outputs) with traceability to schema hashes and eval evidence.

---

## Ownership and on-call
- **Primary owner:** Eng Platform (Console & Tooling)
- **Operational partners:** Release Engineering, Security & Compliance
- **Escalations:**
  - Customer-visible redaction issue → Security Eng reviewer + Console backend EM
  - Docs publishing regressions → DevEx/Docs build owner

**Pager rotation:** `eng-platform-oncall`

---

## Goals / Non-goals
### Goals
1. Generate a **normalized, templated changelog entry** per qualifying event.
2. Maintain an **append-only public feed** and an **append-only internal feed**.
3. Expose APIs for Console and Docs to query:
   - Latest release notes
   - Notes for a model/version
   - Change history with filters (deployment mode, region, severity)
4. Preserve auditability:
   - Who/what/when/where for changes
   - Link to source event IDs and schema versions

### Non-goals (current)
- Customer notification subscriptions (email/webhook) beyond internal webhook plumbing.
- Rewriting all historical release notes (limited backfill only).
- Serving runtime configuration; this service is “read-only” from the perspective of rollout policy.

---

## High-level architecture
### Components
- **Event ingress:** subscribes to Control Plane event stream for model registry changes.
- **Normalizer:** validates and normalizes event payloads against **Model Metadata Schema v1**.
- **Redaction + classification:** splits event details into **public** and **internal** views, assigns taxonomy category + severity.
- **Renderer:** produces human-readable release note text and machine-readable structured payloads.
- **Storage layer:** persists entries, indexes, and processing checkpoints.
- **Serving API:** internal APIs for Console/Docs/Support.

### Data flow (text diagram)
1) Control Plane emits event → 2) Event bus → 3) mrc-changelog consumes → 4) Validate + normalize → 5) Redact + classify → 6) Persist → 7) Publish artifacts → 8) Console/Docs fetch

---

## Inputs
### Event sources
Events are emitted by the Control Plane and Model Registry workflows.

Primary event types:
- `model_metadata.changed`
- `model_version.promoted`
- `model_version.deprecated`
- `model_version.rollback_initiated` (internal-only notes; used to annotate timelines)

### Required event invariants
- Events must include:
  - Stable `event_id` (UUID)
  - `occurred_at` timestamp
  - `actor` (user/service principal)
  - `model_key` (canonical registry key)
  - `model_version` (semantic version string)
  - `deployment_availability` snapshot (Hosted/Dedicated/Private + regions if applicable)
  - `public_change_summary` and/or a structured diff that can be rendered

If a required field is missing, the event is quarantined (see **Failure modes**).

---

## Event schema (canonical)
The changelog service uses a canonical internal envelope, regardless of event transport.

### Envelope (canonical)
```json
{
  "event_id": "0f5b2f1b-89b1-4a90-9b48-6da6c33d7d2a",
  "event_type": "model_version.promoted",
  "occurred_at": "2025-10-23T18:42:11Z",
  "producer": "control-plane",
  "actor": {
    "type": "user|service",
    "id": "u_12345",
    "display_name": "Connor O'Brien"
  },
  "model": {
    "model_key": "redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
    "variant": "base",
    "quantization_profile": "fp16",
    "version": "3.1.4"
  },
  "scope": {
    "deployment_modes": ["hosted", "dedicated"],
    "regions": ["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"],
    "tenant_visibility": "public|internal"
  },
  "metadata": {
    "schema_version": "model-metadata-v1",
    "schema_hash": "sha256:...",
    "tokenizer": "tiktoken-compat@2",
    "runtime": "redwood-runtime@0.19"
  },
  "change": {
    "category": "perf|quality|bugfix|security|breaking|deprecation",
    "severity": "low|medium|high|critical",
    "public_summary": "Improved median latency for typical chat workloads.",
    "internal_notes": "Kernel selection updated; see runbook link.",
    "links": [
      "https://confluence.redwood.inference/...",
      "https://github.com/redwood/..."
    ]
  },
  "evidence": {
    "eval_run_ids": ["eval_9f1..."],
    "baseline_version": "3.1.3",
    "guardrails": "no_regressions_detected"
  }
}
```

### Notes
- `tenant_visibility` controls whether an event can ever produce a public entry. Internal-only events still create internal timeline entries.
- `internal_notes` may contain sensitive implementation details; it is never returned from public endpoints.
- `evidence` fields are optional and subject to redaction rules (statistical claims must be conservative; see taxonomy/style guide).

---

## Outputs
### 1) Persisted changelog entries
For each qualifying event, the service writes a **Changelog Entry** record (public and/or internal view).

Key properties:
- Immutable entry ID
- Stable ordering (by `occurred_at`, tie-broken by `event_id`)
- Links back to:
  - source event (`event_id`)
  - model metadata (`model_key`, `version`, `schema_hash`)
  - evidence (`eval_run_ids`) when allowed

### 2) Release note artifacts (for docs builds)
The service publishes artifacts to object storage for deterministic docs builds.

Bucket: `redwood-changelog-artifacts-prod`
Path format:
- Public: `public/model/{model_key}/version/{version}/{entry_id}.json`
- Internal: `internal/model/{model_key}/version/{version}/{entry_id}.json`

Artifacts are **append-only**; updates require a new entry (e.g., “Correction” note), except for rare redaction hotfix workflows (see **Redaction hotfix**).

---

## APIs
All APIs are internal-to-Redwood services. Customer-facing access is via Console/Docs, not direct calls.

### AuthZ/RBAC
- Console service-to-service auth required.
- RBAC scopes:
  - `changelog.read_public`
  - `changelog.read_internal` (Support/Admin only)
  - `changelog.admin` (break-glass for redaction hotfix)

### REST endpoints (internal)
Base: `https://mrc-changelog.internal.redwood` (env-specific)

1) **List release notes (feed)**
- `GET /v1/release-notes`
- Query params:
  - `visibility=public|internal` (default: public)
  - `deployment_mode=hosted|dedicated|private`
  - `region=us-east-1` (optional)
  - `category=perf|quality|bugfix|security|breaking|deprecation`
  - `severity=low|medium|high|critical`
  - `cursor` / `limit`

2) **Per-model history**
- `GET /v1/models/{model_key}/history`
- Query params:
  - `visibility=public|internal`
  - `from` / `to` timestamps
  - `include_rollout_events=true|false`

3) **Release notes for a specific model version**
- `GET /v1/models/{model_key}/versions/{version}/release-notes`

4) **Artifact fetch helper (docs build)**
- `GET /v1/artifacts/manifest?visibility=public&since=2025-10-01T00:00:00Z`
- Returns a list of artifact URIs + checksums (the docs pipeline uses this to avoid scanning buckets).

### Response shape (conceptual)
Responses include both structured fields (category, severity, affected deployments) and rendered text blocks. The API guarantees:
- Public responses contain **no internal fields**.
- Internal responses include `internal_notes`, source identifiers, and richer links.

---

## Classification + templating
### Category mapping
Classification uses a rule-based mapper with explicit overrides.

Precedence rules (highest wins):
1. `security`
2. `breaking`
3. `deprecation`
4. `quality`
5. `perf`
6. `bugfix`

### Severity guidelines
- **critical:** security hotfix; breaking change with high blast radius; rollback likely
- **high:** observable quality regression risk; major perf changes; wide rollout
- **medium:** moderate perf/quality changes with guardrails
- **low:** minor wording/documentation, metadata-only changes without serving impact

### Rendering templates
Templates produce consistent language and avoid over-claiming:
- Performance notes must specify **direction + scope** (e.g., “median latency improved for typical chat prompts”) and avoid absolute universal claims.
- Quality notes require either:
  - “No statistically significant change detected in Redwood standard eval suite” (if supported), or
  - A conservative description (“Adjusted decoding defaults; some outputs may differ”).

---

## Storage and indexing
### Primary datastore
- **PostgreSQL** (service-owned)
- Tables (logical):
  - `events_ingested` (dedupe + processing state)
  - `changelog_entries_public`
  - `changelog_entries_internal`
  - `processing_checkpoints`
  - `quarantine_events`

### Idempotency
- Primary idempotency key: `event_id`
- Secondary safety: unique constraint on (`model_key`, `version`, `event_type`, `occurred_at`, `schema_hash`) to prevent producer bugs from duplicating entries.

### Retention
- Public entries: retained indefinitely (customer-facing history).
- Internal entries: retained per audit policy (see Security/Compliance doc); minimum **18 months** online, with cold storage beyond.
- Quarantine payloads: retained **30 days** unless escalated.

---

## Failure modes and mitigations
### 1) Event ingestion lag
**Symptoms:** Console feed stale; docs pages missing recent updates.

**Detection:**
- Lag metric: `mrc_changelog_event_consumer_lag_seconds` (warn > 120s, page > 600s)

**Mitigation:**
- Autoscale consumer workers.
- If backlog is caused by poison event, quarantine it (see below).

### 2) Poison / invalid event payload
**Symptoms:** Consumer retries; no progress; repeated failures.

**Mitigation:**
- Validate against canonical schema early.
- Move to `quarantine_events` after N attempts (default 5) with failure reason.
- Emit internal alert with:
  - event_id
  - producer
  - first_seen_at
  - validation error summary

### 3) Redaction regression (internal data leaked)
**Severity:** Critical.

**Preventative controls:**
- Public vs internal rendering paths are separate and unit-tested.
- Snapshot tests for known sensitive fields.
- CI check requires a “public preview” artifact for reviewer approval.

**Response:**
- Immediately disable public artifact publication via feature flag `mrc_public_publish_enabled`.
- Purge affected public artifacts and reissue corrected entries.
- File incident + security review; add regression tests.

### 4) Duplicate / out-of-order events
**Symptoms:** Multiple entries for the same promotion; confusing timeline.

**Mitigation:**
- Enforce idempotency with DB constraints.
- Deterministic ordering by (`occurred_at`, `event_id`).
- If producers emit out-of-order timestamps, the service preserves timestamps but flags internal warning `timestamp_anomaly=true`.

### 5) Partial outage of object storage
**Symptoms:** Console still works (DB reads), docs builds fail to fetch artifacts.

**Mitigation:**
- Docs pipeline can fall back to API manifest and API fetch if artifact store is degraded (rate-limited).
- Retry with exponential backoff; fail docs build with clear error message.

---

## Observability
### Key metrics
- Ingestion:
  - `events_received_total{event_type,producer}`
  - `events_quarantined_total{reason}`
  - `event_consumer_lag_seconds`
- Generation:
  - `entries_created_total{visibility,category,severity}`
  - `render_time_ms`
  - `redaction_denials_total{field}` (counts removed sensitive fields)
- Serving:
  - `api_requests_total{route,status,visibility}`
  - `api_latency_ms_p50/p95/p99`

### Logs
- Structured logs include `event_id`, `model_key`, `version`, `visibility`, and `entry_id`.
- Internal-only logs may include failure details; avoid logging raw internal_notes.

### Tracing
- Propagate trace context from Control Plane event producer where available.

---

## SLOs
- **API availability:** 99.9% monthly (internal consumers)
- **Freshness:** 95% of qualifying events produce a public entry within **5 minutes**
- **Correctness:** 0 tolerance for internal field leakage into public endpoints/artifacts

---

## Operational procedures
### Backfill procedure (limited)
Used to create entries for a small, curated set of recent model promotions.
1. Provide a list of `event_id`s or (`model_key`, `version`, time window) to Release Eng.
2. Run `mrc-changelog backfill --source=eventstore --since=... --until=...` in a controlled environment.
3. Validate public preview artifacts.
4. Publish artifacts and reindex.

### Redaction hotfix (break-glass)
Only for accidental leakage or policy violations.
- Requires approvals: Security Eng reviewer + Eng Platform on-call + Product lead.
- Steps:
  1) Disable public publication
  2) Identify affected artifact URIs
  3) Purge + regenerate entries (or append correction note when possible)
  4) Re-enable publication
  5) Incident write-up and test hardening

---

## Dependency map
- Upstream:
  - Control Plane event emitter (model metadata change + promotion events)
  - Model Metadata Schema v1 validation library
- Downstream:
  - Console backend (release notes feed + model history UI)
  - Docs build pipeline (public release notes pages)
  - Support/Admin audit tooling

---

## Open questions / follow-ups
1. Tenant-specific visibility: how to represent customer-specific or embargoed changes in a forward-compatible way without leaking existence.
2. Private deployments: standard packaging of release note artifacts alongside offline upgrade bundles (tracked in ENG-9829).
3. Quality claims: formalize allowed phrasing based on statistical significance thresholds and eval suite coverage.

---

## Appendix: Example public entry (rendered)
**Category:** Performance (Medium)

**Summary:** Improved median latency for typical chat workloads.

**Details:**
- Updated runtime kernel selection for this model variant.
- No API changes required.

**Availability:** Hosted (us-east-1, eu-west-1); Dedicated rollout follows account policy.

**Rollback:** Pin to prior version (3.1.3) or use automatic fallback policy if configured.

(Internal-only fields such as exact kernel names, internal incident links, and raw diff details are excluded.)

title: Fleet Readiness Service (FRS) — Control Plane Service Documentation
preview:
## Overview
Fleet Readiness Service (FRS) is the Redwood control-plane component responsible for:
- Recording and serving the canonical **node readiness state** during fleet bring-up (Dedicated + shared capacity).
- Persisting **validation results and artifacts** produced by the provisioning orchestrator and node validation agent.
- Exposing APIs to internal tools (Fleet UI, capacity ops tooling, SRE dashboards) to answer: “Is this node safe to join serving pools?” and “Why did it fail?”
- Emitting **telemetry** (metrics, logs, and traces) used for time-to-ready SLIs/SLOs and triage.

FRS is part of the Provision-to-Ready automation sprint and implements the readiness model defined in ADR-017.

---

## Service ownership and support
**Owner team:** Platform Control Plane (eng-platform)

**Primary on-call:** Platform Control Plane
- Escalation path: Platform on-call → Infra/Fleet on-call (for infra-side failures) → SRE (for SLO/alerting issues)

**Key partners:**
- Infra/Fleet: provisioning orchestrator, Terraform outputs, pool metadata
- SRE/Observability: SLIs/SLOs, dashboards/alerts, tracing standards
- Security/Compliance: image channel audit events, least-privilege access, artifact retention

---

## Responsibilities (and non-goals)
### Responsibilities
1) **Canonical state tracking**
- Maintain current readiness state per node.
- Enforce valid state transitions.
- Preserve transition timestamps for time-to-ready measurement.

2) **Validation result persistence**
- Store step-level validation outcomes (pass/fail/skip), durations, and pointers to logs/artifacts.
- Normalize failure reasons into operator-actionable categories.

3) **API for internal tooling**
- Provide read APIs for summarization (counts by state, top failures) and drill-down (node details, validation history).
- Provide controlled write APIs for state updates (only approved control-plane identities).

4) **Telemetry and audit events**
- Emit metrics and traces for:
  - time-to-ready
  - failure rates by step
  - quarantine counts
  - API health
- Emit audit events for high-impact changes (e.g., pool image channel/version updates) when applicable.

### Non-goals
- FRS does **not** perform provisioning actions (instance creation, kubelet bootstrap, CNI setup).
- FRS does **not** run validations; it only records results produced by external actors.
- FRS is **not** a generic artifact store; it stores metadata and pointers (object storage) rather than large logs inline.

---

## System context and data flow
### High-level flow (Provision → Validate → Ready)
1) **Infra allocates** GPU instances and bootstraps base OS + kubelet.
2) **Provisioning Orchestrator** runs orchestration steps (join cluster, apply configs, deploy validation agent, etc.).
3) **Node Validation Agent (DaemonSet)** executes pre/post-flight checks and produces validation results.
4) Orchestrator and/or validation agent **POST results to FRS**.
5) FRS updates node state to **Validating** and then **Ready** (or **Quarantined**) based on gating policy.
6) Control plane and fleet tooling consult FRS before adding a node to **serving pools**.

### Integration points
- Provisioning Orchestrator → FRS: state transitions, step telemetry, orchestration metadata
- Validation Agent → FRS: validation results + artifact pointers
- Fleet UI / Ops Tooling → FRS: node search, readiness summaries, failure reasons
- SLO Toolkit / Observability → FRS: event timestamps for SLI computation, exported metrics

---

## Readiness states and transitions
FRS implements the canonical state machine from ADR-017.

### States
- **Allocated**: Capacity exists and is assigned to a pool, but node has not started provisioning.
- **Provisioning**: Orchestrator is bringing the node to a baseline Kubernetes-joined configuration.
- **Validating**: Node is running required validation gates (hardware + config + runtime smoke tests).
- **Ready**: Node has passed required validations and is eligible to join serving pools.
- **Quarantined**: Node is blocked from serving due to repeated failures or quarantine-worthy signals; requires operator action.

### Authoritative signals
- **State writes:** Only control-plane service identities (provisioning orchestrator) may mutate readiness state.
- **Validation results:** Validation Agent writes results; FRS uses them to compute gating outcomes.
- **Heartbeat / liveness:** FRS uses control-plane writes + freshness windows (not agent heartbeats) as the authoritative readiness state. Heartbeats are treated as diagnostic signals, not state authority.

### Transition rules (summary)
- Allocated → Provisioning: orchestration started
- Provisioning → Validating: node joined cluster + baseline config applied + validation agent deployed
- Validating → Ready: all required checks passed
- Validating → Quarantined: quarantine-worthy check fails OR failure count exceeds threshold
- Any → Quarantined: manual operator action (restricted)

Invalid transitions are rejected (HTTP 409) with a reason.

---

## Data model
FRS stores (at minimum) the following entities.

### NodeReadiness (current state)
Fields:
- node_id (internal canonical ID)
- provider_instance_id (e.g., i-…)
- cluster_id
- pool_id (Dedicated pool or shared pool)
- region
- gpu_type (A100/H100/L40S/etc.)
- image_channel (candidate/stable)
- image_version (immutable build identifier)
- state (Allocated/Provisioning/Validating/Ready/Quarantined)
- state_reason (short operator-readable string)
- last_transition_at
- created_at
- updated_at

### ReadinessEvent (append-only transition log)
Fields:
- node_id
- from_state / to_state
- transition_at
- actor (service identity)
- request_id / trace_id
- metadata (small key/value tags; see cardinality constraints)

### ValidationRun and ValidationResult
ValidationRun fields:
- run_id
- node_id
- run_kind (preflight/postflight)
- started_at / completed_at
- overall_status (pass/fail)
- orchestrator_attempt (int)

ValidationResult fields:
- run_id
- check_name (e.g., gpu_health, driver_parity, kubelet_join, nccl_smoke, runtime_smoke, perf_canary)
- status (pass/fail/skip)
- duration_ms
- failure_category (see below)
- failure_code (stable string for dashboards)
- failure_summary (operator-readable)
- artifact_refs (pointers only)

### Failure categories (normalized)
- GPU_HARDWARE (ECC/Xid, unhealthy GPU)
- DRIVER_TOOLKIT (driver/CUDA/NCCL mismatch)
- K8S_JOIN (kubelet/CSR/CNI)
- NETWORK_FABRIC (bandwidth/latency/NCCL fabric)
- STORAGE (mounts/IOPS)
- RUNTIME_SMOKE (inference runtime failure)
- PERF_REGRESSION (perf canary below threshold)
- CONFIG_DRIFT (baseline config mismatch)
- UNKNOWN (fallback)

---

## Artifact storage
FRS stores pointers (URIs) to artifacts produced by orchestrator/agent.

### Artifact types
- validation logs (bundled)
- diagnostic snapshots (nvidia-smi, dmesg excerpts)
- NCCL test output
- runtime smoke test output
- perf canary results summary

### Storage policy
- Artifacts are stored in object storage (internal bucket). FRS stores:
  - uri
  - sha256
  - byte_size
  - created_at
  - retention_class

### Retention
- Default retention: 14 days for validation artifacts
- Extended retention (restricted): 30–90 days for incident-linked runs (ticket/incident ID required)

### Access control
- Artifact URIs are not world-readable; access requires internal auth + membership in approved groups.
- FRS returns URIs only to authorized identities; otherwise returns redacted pointers.

---

## API (internal)
All endpoints are internal-only behind the control-plane gateway.

### Authentication and authorization
- mTLS + service identity for machine-to-machine calls.
- User access via gateway-issued JWT with RBAC scopes.

Roles/scopes:
- frs.read: view readiness + validation details
- frs.write_state: mutate readiness state (restricted)
- frs.write_validation: submit validation runs/results
- frs.quarantine_override: manual quarantine/unquarantine (very restricted)

### Core endpoints
#### Get node readiness
GET /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}
Returns current readiness state and latest validation summary.

#### List nodes (filters)
GET /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes?pool_id=&region=&gpu_type=&state=&image_channel=
Supports pagination.

#### Readiness summary for UI
GET /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/summary?pool_id=&region=
Returns counts by state and top failure codes (no per-node identifiers in summary).

#### Record a state transition
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}/transition
Body (example):
{
  "to_state": "Validating",
  "reason": "kubelet joined; validation agent deployed",
  "actor": "provisioning-orchestrator",
  "request_id": "...",
  "metadata": {
    "pool_id": "ded-123",
    "region": "us-east-1",
    "gpu_type": "a100"
  }
}
Notes:
- Invalid transitions return 409 with expected_state.
- Metadata is subject to cardinality constraints (see Telemetry section).

#### Submit a validation run
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}/validation-runs
Creates a run_id.

#### Submit validation results
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/validation-runs/{run_id}/results
Accepts multiple results per run.

#### Quarantine/unquarantine (operator override)
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}/quarantine
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}/unquarantine
Restricted; emits audit log entries.

### Idempotency and retries
- Write endpoints support Idempotency-Key header.
- Orchestrator retries are expected; FRS de-dupes by (node_id, to_state, request_id) where possible.

---

## Readiness gating and serving pool admission
FRS is the source of truth for whether a node may join a serving pool.

Admission rule (current):
- Only nodes in **Ready** state may be added to serving pools.
- Nodes in **Validating** may remain schedulable for validation workloads only.
- Nodes in **Quarantined** are tainted and blocked from inference workloads.

If capacity is constrained, operators may temporarily bypass gating via feature flag, but this must be treated as an incident-level decision with explicit approval and rollback plan.

---

## Telemetry
FRS exports metrics and traces following observability standards (low-cardinality labels; no per-node IDs in metrics labels).

### Metrics (examples)
Service health:
- frs_http_requests_total{route,method,status}
- frs_http_request_duration_ms_bucket{route,method}
- frs_db_query_duration_ms_bucket{operation}

Readiness state:
- frs_nodes_in_state{state,region,pool_type,gpu_type}
- frs_state_transitions_total{from_state,to_state,region,pool_type,gpu_type}

Validation:
- frs_validation_checks_total{check_name,status,region,pool_type,gpu_type}
- frs_validation_check_duration_ms_bucket{check_name,region,pool_type,gpu_type}
- frs_failure_codes_total{failure_code,region,pool_type,gpu_type}

Time-to-ready:
- frs_time_to_ready_seconds_bucket{region,pool_type,gpu_type}

### Tracing
- Every write request attaches trace_id; FRS creates spans:
  - frs.transition.write
  - frs.validation_run.create
  - frs.validation_results.write
  - frs.summary.read
- Downstream calls (DB, object-store signer) are wrapped in child spans.

### Logs
- Structured JSON logs with fields: request_id, trace_id, actor, node_id (allowed in logs), pool_id, region, gpu_type, operation, outcome.
- Logs are the correct place for per-node identifiers (not metric labels).

### Cardinality constraints
- DO NOT include node_id, instance_id, or run_id as metric labels.
- Allowed labels are limited to: region, pool_type (dedicated/shared), gpu_type, image_channel, state, check_name, failure_code.

---

## Reliability: SLIs/SLOs (service-level)
FRS participates in the overall Provision-to-Ready SLOs and also maintains service health SLOs.

### Service SLO targets (initial)
- Availability: 99.9% monthly for read APIs; 99.5% for write APIs (writes depend on external actors)
- Latency:
  - p95 < 250ms for GET /summary
  - p95 < 400ms for GET /nodes (filtered)
  - p95 < 500ms for POST write endpoints (excluding client retries)

### Error budget policy
- If burn rate exceeds threshold, pause rollout expansion (additional regions/GPU types) until stabilized.

---

## Operational procedures
### Common operator workflows
1) Node stuck in Validating
- Check latest ValidationRun and failing checks.
- Confirm whether failure is flake vs deterministic.
- If deterministic and quarantine-worthy: quarantine node, reimage/retry per runbook.

2) High quarantine counts causing capacity shortfall
- Identify top failure codes via /summary and dashboards.
- If caused by a new image version/channel: stop promotion, consider rollback.
- If caused by NCCL/network flakes: tune timeouts/retries; consider lowering sensitivity temporarily with SRE approval.

3) Artifact access request
- Ensure user has approved group access.
- Provide dashboard links and the node details view; do not paste raw URIs in tickets unless necessary.

### Runbooks and references
- Provision-to-ready failures triage: confluence://eng-infra/runbooks/provision-to-ready-failures-triage-runbook
- SLOs and alerting: confluence://eng-sre/reliability-initiatives/provision-to-ready-slo-and-error-budget
- Dashboards: confluence://eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/provision-to-ready-dashboards

---

## Failure modes and mitigations
### DB unavailable / high latency
Impact: UI and orchestrator writes fail; readiness states may not progress.
Mitigations:
- Circuit-breaker on client side; retry with exponential backoff.
- Page Platform on-call if write error rate breaches threshold.

### Metric tag explosion
Impact: monitoring instability and alert noise.
Mitigations:
- Enforce label allowlist in code review.
- If detected in prod: hotfix to remove offending labels; backfill dashboards if needed.

### Incorrect state transitions
Impact: nodes admitted prematurely or blocked incorrectly.
Mitigations:
- Strict transition validation (409s on invalid transitions).
- Audit log for manual overrides.
- Post-deploy canary: ensure Ready requires required check set.

### Artifact access leakage
Impact: security incident.
Mitigations:
- Signer service with short-lived tokens.
- Redaction for unauthorized callers.
- Audit logs for artifact URI requests.

---

## Security and compliance
- RBAC enforced at gateway and service layer.
- Manual quarantine/unquarantine is an auditable action.
- Image channel/version changes are audited (where initiated through control-plane tooling).
- Least privilege: validation agent may only write validation results for its node_id; orchestrator may write transitions for nodes in its assigned pools.

---

## Configuration and deployment
- Deployed as a control-plane service in each major region.
- Backed by regional database (multi-AZ) with read replicas for summary endpoints.
- Feature flags:
  - frs.enable_quarantine (default: on)
  - frs.enforce_ready_gate_for_serving_pool (default: on in staged rollout regions)
  - frs.allow_manual_override (default: off; requires break-glass)

---

## Change management
- API changes must be backwards compatible for internal clients.
- New validation check names require:
  1) documented semantics and failure codes
  2) dashboard updates
  3) runbook updates if quarantine-worthy

---

## Appendix: Check naming conventions
Use snake_case and keep names stable across releases.
Examples:
- gpu_health
- driver_parity
- kubelet_join
- network_throughput
- storage_iops
- nccl_smoke
- runtime_smoke
- perf_canary

Failure codes should be stable strings suitable for dashboards, e.g.:
- GPU_XID_ERROR
- DRIVER_VERSION_MISMATCH
- CUDA_MINOR_MISMATCH
- KUBELET_CSR_TIMEOUT
- NCCL_ALLREDUCE_TIMEOUT
- PERF_P50_REGRESSION

---

## Appendix: Quick links (internal)
- FRS service repo: (internal) control-plane/redwood/fleet-readiness-service
- Grafana dashboards: see confluence://eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/provision-to-ready-dashboards
- Alerts: “Provision-to-Ready” alert group in on-call tool
text:
## Overview
Fleet Readiness Service (FRS) is the Redwood control-plane component responsible for:
- Recording and serving the canonical **node readiness state** during fleet bring-up (Dedicated + shared capacity).
- Persisting **validation results and artifacts** produced by the provisioning orchestrator and node validation agent.
- Exposing APIs to internal tools (Fleet UI, capacity ops tooling, SRE dashboards) to answer: “Is this node safe to join serving pools?” and “Why did it fail?”
- Emitting **telemetry** (metrics, logs, and traces) used for time-to-ready SLIs/SLOs and triage.

FRS is part of the Provision-to-Ready automation sprint and implements the readiness model defined in ADR-017.

---

## Service ownership and support
**Owner team:** Platform Control Plane (eng-platform)

**Primary on-call:** Platform Control Plane
- Escalation path: Platform on-call → Infra/Fleet on-call (for infra-side failures) → SRE (for SLO/alerting issues)

**Key partners:**
- Infra/Fleet: provisioning orchestrator, Terraform outputs, pool metadata
- SRE/Observability: SLIs/SLOs, dashboards/alerts, tracing standards
- Security/Compliance: image channel audit events, least-privilege access, artifact retention

---

## Responsibilities (and non-goals)
### Responsibilities
1) **Canonical state tracking**
- Maintain current readiness state per node.
- Enforce valid state transitions.
- Preserve transition timestamps for time-to-ready measurement.

2) **Validation result persistence**
- Store step-level validation outcomes (pass/fail/skip), durations, and pointers to logs/artifacts.
- Normalize failure reasons into operator-actionable categories.

3) **API for internal tooling**
- Provide read APIs for summarization (counts by state, top failures) and drill-down (node details, validation history).
- Provide controlled write APIs for state updates (only approved control-plane identities).

4) **Telemetry and audit events**
- Emit metrics and traces for:
  - time-to-ready
  - failure rates by step
  - quarantine counts
  - API health
- Emit audit events for high-impact changes (e.g., pool image channel/version updates) when applicable.

### Non-goals
- FRS does **not** perform provisioning actions (instance creation, kubelet bootstrap, CNI setup).
- FRS does **not** run validations; it only records results produced by external actors.
- FRS is **not** a generic artifact store; it stores metadata and pointers (object storage) rather than large logs inline.

---

## System context and data flow
### High-level flow (Provision → Validate → Ready)
1) **Infra allocates** GPU instances and bootstraps base OS + kubelet.
2) **Provisioning Orchestrator** runs orchestration steps (join cluster, apply configs, deploy validation agent, etc.).
3) **Node Validation Agent (DaemonSet)** executes pre/post-flight checks and produces validation results.
4) Orchestrator and/or validation agent **POST results to FRS**.
5) FRS updates node state to **Validating** and then **Ready** (or **Quarantined**) based on gating policy.
6) Control plane and fleet tooling consult FRS before adding a node to **serving pools**.

### Integration points
- Provisioning Orchestrator → FRS: state transitions, step telemetry, orchestration metadata
- Validation Agent → FRS: validation results + artifact pointers
- Fleet UI / Ops Tooling → FRS: node search, readiness summaries, failure reasons
- SLO Toolkit / Observability → FRS: event timestamps for SLI computation, exported metrics

---

## Readiness states and transitions
FRS implements the canonical state machine from ADR-017.

### States
- **Allocated**: Capacity exists and is assigned to a pool, but node has not started provisioning.
- **Provisioning**: Orchestrator is bringing the node to a baseline Kubernetes-joined configuration.
- **Validating**: Node is running required validation gates (hardware + config + runtime smoke tests).
- **Ready**: Node has passed required validations and is eligible to join serving pools.
- **Quarantined**: Node is blocked from serving due to repeated failures or quarantine-worthy signals; requires operator action.

### Authoritative signals
- **State writes:** Only control-plane service identities (provisioning orchestrator) may mutate readiness state.
- **Validation results:** Validation Agent writes results; FRS uses them to compute gating outcomes.
- **Heartbeat / liveness:** FRS uses control-plane writes + freshness windows (not agent heartbeats) as the authoritative readiness state. Heartbeats are treated as diagnostic signals, not state authority.

### Transition rules (summary)
- Allocated → Provisioning: orchestration started
- Provisioning → Validating: node joined cluster + baseline config applied + validation agent deployed
- Validating → Ready: all required checks passed
- Validating → Quarantined: quarantine-worthy check fails OR failure count exceeds threshold
- Any → Quarantined: manual operator action (restricted)

Invalid transitions are rejected (HTTP 409) with a reason.

---

## Data model
FRS stores (at minimum) the following entities.

### NodeReadiness (current state)
Fields:
- node_id (internal canonical ID)
- provider_instance_id (e.g., i-…)
- cluster_id
- pool_id (Dedicated pool or shared pool)
- region
- gpu_type (A100/H100/L40S/etc.)
- image_channel (candidate/stable)
- image_version (immutable build identifier)
- state (Allocated/Provisioning/Validating/Ready/Quarantined)
- state_reason (short operator-readable string)
- last_transition_at
- created_at
- updated_at

### ReadinessEvent (append-only transition log)
Fields:
- node_id
- from_state / to_state
- transition_at
- actor (service identity)
- request_id / trace_id
- metadata (small key/value tags; see cardinality constraints)

### ValidationRun and ValidationResult
ValidationRun fields:
- run_id
- node_id
- run_kind (preflight/postflight)
- started_at / completed_at
- overall_status (pass/fail)
- orchestrator_attempt (int)

ValidationResult fields:
- run_id
- check_name (e.g., gpu_health, driver_parity, kubelet_join, nccl_smoke, runtime_smoke, perf_canary)
- status (pass/fail/skip)
- duration_ms
- failure_category (see below)
- failure_code (stable string for dashboards)
- failure_summary (operator-readable)
- artifact_refs (pointers only)

### Failure categories (normalized)
- GPU_HARDWARE (ECC/Xid, unhealthy GPU)
- DRIVER_TOOLKIT (driver/CUDA/NCCL mismatch)
- K8S_JOIN (kubelet/CSR/CNI)
- NETWORK_FABRIC (bandwidth/latency/NCCL fabric)
- STORAGE (mounts/IOPS)
- RUNTIME_SMOKE (inference runtime failure)
- PERF_REGRESSION (perf canary below threshold)
- CONFIG_DRIFT (baseline config mismatch)
- UNKNOWN (fallback)

---

## Artifact storage
FRS stores pointers (URIs) to artifacts produced by orchestrator/agent.

### Artifact types
- validation logs (bundled)
- diagnostic snapshots (nvidia-smi, dmesg excerpts)
- NCCL test output
- runtime smoke test output
- perf canary results summary

### Storage policy
- Artifacts are stored in object storage (internal bucket). FRS stores:
  - uri
  - sha256
  - byte_size
  - created_at
  - retention_class

### Retention
- Default retention: 14 days for validation artifacts
- Extended retention (restricted): 30–90 days for incident-linked runs (ticket/incident ID required)

### Access control
- Artifact URIs are not world-readable; access requires internal auth + membership in approved groups.
- FRS returns URIs only to authorized identities; otherwise returns redacted pointers.

---

## API (internal)
All endpoints are internal-only behind the control-plane gateway.

### Authentication and authorization
- mTLS + service identity for machine-to-machine calls.
- User access via gateway-issued JWT with RBAC scopes.

Roles/scopes:
- frs.read: view readiness + validation details
- frs.write_state: mutate readiness state (restricted)
- frs.write_validation: submit validation runs/results
- frs.quarantine_override: manual quarantine/unquarantine (very restricted)

### Core endpoints
#### Get node readiness
GET /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}
Returns current readiness state and latest validation summary.

#### List nodes (filters)
GET /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes?pool_id=&region=&gpu_type=&state=&image_channel=
Supports pagination.

#### Readiness summary for UI
GET /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/summary?pool_id=&region=
Returns counts by state and top failure codes (no per-node identifiers in summary).

#### Record a state transition
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}/transition
Body (example):
{
  "to_state": "Validating",
  "reason": "kubelet joined; validation agent deployed",
  "actor": "provisioning-orchestrator",
  "request_id": "...",
  "metadata": {
    "pool_id": "ded-123",
    "region": "us-east-1",
    "gpu_type": "a100"
  }
}
Notes:
- Invalid transitions return 409 with expected_state.
- Metadata is subject to cardinality constraints (see Telemetry section).

#### Submit a validation run
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}/validation-runs
Creates a run_id.

#### Submit validation results
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/validation-runs/{run_id}/results
Accepts multiple results per run.

#### Quarantine/unquarantine (operator override)
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}/quarantine
POST /internal/fleet-readiness/v1/nodes/{node_id}/unquarantine
Restricted; emits audit log entries.

### Idempotency and retries
- Write endpoints support Idempotency-Key header.
- Orchestrator retries are expected; FRS de-dupes by (node_id, to_state, request_id) where possible.

---

## Readiness gating and serving pool admission
FRS is the source of truth for whether a node may join a serving pool.

Admission rule (current):
- Only nodes in **Ready** state may be added to serving pools.
- Nodes in **Validating** may remain schedulable for validation workloads only.
- Nodes in **Quarantined** are tainted and blocked from inference workloads.

If capacity is constrained, operators may temporarily bypass gating via feature flag, but this must be treated as an incident-level decision with explicit approval and rollback plan.

---

## Telemetry
FRS exports metrics and traces following observability standards (low-cardinality labels; no per-node IDs in metrics labels).

### Metrics (examples)
Service health:
- frs_http_requests_total{route,method,status}
- frs_http_request_duration_ms_bucket{route,method}
- frs_db_query_duration_ms_bucket{operation}

Readiness state:
- frs_nodes_in_state{state,region,pool_type,gpu_type}
- frs_state_transitions_total{from_state,to_state,region,pool_type,gpu_type}

Validation:
- frs_validation_checks_total{check_name,status,region,pool_type,gpu_type}
- frs_validation_check_duration_ms_bucket{check_name,region,pool_type,gpu_type}
- frs_failure_codes_total{failure_code,region,pool_type,gpu_type}

Time-to-ready:
- frs_time_to_ready_seconds_bucket{region,pool_type,gpu_type}

### Tracing
- Every write request attaches trace_id; FRS creates spans:
  - frs.transition.write
  - frs.validation_run.create
  - frs.validation_results.write
  - frs.summary.read
- Downstream calls (DB, object-store signer) are wrapped in child spans.

### Logs
- Structured JSON logs with fields: request_id, trace_id, actor, node_id (allowed in logs), pool_id, region, gpu_type, operation, outcome.
- Logs are the correct place for per-node identifiers (not metric labels).

### Cardinality constraints
- DO NOT include node_id, instance_id, or run_id as metric labels.
- Allowed labels are limited to: region, pool_type (dedicated/shared), gpu_type, image_channel, state, check_name, failure_code.

---

## Reliability: SLIs/SLOs (service-level)
FRS participates in the overall Provision-to-Ready SLOs and also maintains service health SLOs.

### Service SLO targets (initial)
- Availability: 99.9% monthly for read APIs; 99.5% for write APIs (writes depend on external actors)
- Latency:
  - p95 < 250ms for GET /summary
  - p95 < 400ms for GET /nodes (filtered)
  - p95 < 500ms for POST write endpoints (excluding client retries)

### Error budget policy
- If burn rate exceeds threshold, pause rollout expansion (additional regions/GPU types) until stabilized.

---

## Operational procedures
### Common operator workflows
1) Node stuck in Validating
- Check latest ValidationRun and failing checks.
- Confirm whether failure is flake vs deterministic.
- If deterministic and quarantine-worthy: quarantine node, reimage/retry per runbook.

2) High quarantine counts causing capacity shortfall
- Identify top failure codes via /summary and dashboards.
- If caused by a new image version/channel: stop promotion, consider rollback.
- If caused by NCCL/network flakes: tune timeouts/retries; consider lowering sensitivity temporarily with SRE approval.

3) Artifact access request
- Ensure user has approved group access.
- Provide dashboard links and the node details view; do not paste raw URIs in tickets unless necessary.

### Runbooks and references
- Provision-to-ready failures triage: confluence://eng-infra/runbooks/provision-to-ready-failures-triage-runbook
- SLOs and alerting: confluence://eng-sre/reliability-initiatives/provision-to-ready-slo-and-error-budget
- Dashboards: confluence://eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/provision-to-ready-dashboards

---

## Failure modes and mitigations
### DB unavailable / high latency
Impact: UI and orchestrator writes fail; readiness states may not progress.
Mitigations:
- Circuit-breaker on client side; retry with exponential backoff.
- Page Platform on-call if write error rate breaches threshold.

### Metric tag explosion
Impact: monitoring instability and alert noise.
Mitigations:
- Enforce label allowlist in code review.
- If detected in prod: hotfix to remove offending labels; backfill dashboards if needed.

### Incorrect state transitions
Impact: nodes admitted prematurely or blocked incorrectly.
Mitigations:
- Strict transition validation (409s on invalid transitions).
- Audit log for manual overrides.
- Post-deploy canary: ensure Ready requires required check set.

### Artifact access leakage
Impact: security incident.
Mitigations:
- Signer service with short-lived tokens.
- Redaction for unauthorized callers.
- Audit logs for artifact URI requests.

---

## Security and compliance
- RBAC enforced at gateway and service layer.
- Manual quarantine/unquarantine is an auditable action.
- Image channel/version changes are audited (where initiated through control-plane tooling).
- Least privilege: validation agent may only write validation results for its node_id; orchestrator may write transitions for nodes in its assigned pools.

---

## Configuration and deployment
- Deployed as a control-plane service in each major region.
- Backed by regional database (multi-AZ) with read replicas for summary endpoints.
- Feature flags:
  - frs.enable_quarantine (default: on)
  - frs.enforce_ready_gate_for_serving_pool (default: on in staged rollout regions)
  - frs.allow_manual_override (default: off; requires break-glass)

---

## Change management
- API changes must be backwards compatible for internal clients.
- New validation check names require:
  1) documented semantics and failure codes
  2) dashboard updates
  3) runbook updates if quarantine-worthy

---

## Appendix: Check naming conventions
Use snake_case and keep names stable across releases.
Examples:
- gpu_health
- driver_parity
- kubelet_join
- network_throughput

Failure codes should be stable strings suitable for dashboards, e.g.:
- GPU_XID_ERROR

## Appendix: Quick links (internal)
