title: Provision-to-Ready Dashboards (Catalog + Alert Links)
preview:
## Purpose
This page is the catalog of **Grafana dashboards** and **alert rules** used to operate the Provision-to-Ready automation for Dedicated capacity and shared GPU fleet bring-up.

Dashboards are designed to answer:
- How long does it take to go from **Allocated → Ready** (overall + by step)?
- Where are we failing (step + error class), and is it isolated to a **region** or **GPU type**?
- Are nodes being **Quarantined** at an elevated rate, and what’s driving it?
- Which validation checks are **flaky** (pass/fail oscillation) vs consistently failing?

## Quick links (Grafana)
> Note: Links assume the internal Grafana base URL. If you use a different environment, swap the host.

### 1) Executive / SLO view
- **Provision-to-Ready: SLO Overview**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-slo/provision-to-ready-slo
  - Best for: weekly reporting, error budget posture, rollout go/no-go
  - Primary panels:
    - Time-to-Ready (P50/P95) – overall + by region
    - Success rate (Ready without quarantine) – overall + by GPU type
    - Error budget burn (fast/slow burn)
    - Top failure step (last 24h / 7d)
  - Default filters: env=prod, pool_type=dedicated

### 2) Per-step latency breakdown
- **Provision-to-Ready: Step Latency Breakdown**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-steps/provision-to-ready-steps
  - Best for: isolating which stage is contributing to time-to-ready regressions
  - Primary panels:
    - Step duration heatmap (p50/p95) by step_name
    - “Longest step per node” distribution
    - Retry counts per step (by attempt)
    - Step timeout rate
  - Common filters:
    - region (e.g., us-east-1)
    - gpu_sku (e.g., a100-80gb)
    - image_channel (candidate/stable)
    - rollout_flag (enabled/disabled)

### 3) Failure heatmaps (region × GPU type)
- **Provision-to-Ready: Failure Heatmap**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-fail-heat/provision-to-ready-failure-heatmap
  - Best for: quickly spotting localized failures (network, image, fabric, capacity provider)
  - Primary panels:
    - Failure rate heatmap (region × gpu_sku)
    - Failures by step_name (stacked) per region
    - Top failure_reason (normalized) per gpu_sku
  - Notes:
    - This dashboard intentionally uses **coarse failure reasons** (see “Cardinality & tagging” below).

### 4) Quarantine operations
- **Fleet Readiness: Quarantine & Overrides**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-quarantine/quarantine-operations
  - Best for: capacity shortfall risk and operational triage
  - Primary panels:
    - Quarantined node count (current) by region/pool_id
    - Quarantine events over time (rate)
    - Quarantine triggers by validation_name
    - Manual override actions (unquarantine, force-ready) count
  - Operator notes:
    - If quarantine count is rising and pool capacity is impacted, follow: confluence://eng-infra/runbooks/provision-to-ready-failures-triage-runbook

### 5) Validation flake rate (signal quality)
- **Provision-to-Ready: Validation Flake Rate**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-flake/validation-flake-rate
  - Best for: reducing noisy gates and preventing false quarantines
  - Primary panels:
    - Flake rate by validation_name (24h / 7d)
    - Pass→Fail and Fail→Pass transition counts
    - Timeout fraction by validation_name
    - NCCL smoke test: latency + timeout breakdown
  - When to use:
    - After rollout changes to timeouts/retries
    - When on-call reports “Validating stuck” or frequent oscillation

### 6) Rollout guardrails (candidate image / staged rollout)
- **Provision-to-Ready: Rollout Guardrails (Image + Flags)**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-rollout/rollout-guardrails
  - Best for: staged rollout in a single region/GPU type and rapid rollback decisions
  - Primary panels:
    - Success rate by image_channel (candidate vs stable)
    - Time-to-ready p95 by image_version
    - Failure reasons after image change (24h window)
    - Auto-rollback triggers (alert state)

## Alert catalog (Grafana + routing)
Alert rules live in the observability pack and route via PagerDuty (SRE) and Jira (Infra) depending on severity.

### Paging (P1/P2) alerts
- **P2: Time-to-Ready p95 regression (prod / dedicated)**
  - Alert name: p2r_time_to_ready_p95_high
  - Dashboard panel: SLO Overview → Time-to-Ready p95
  - Trigger (default): p95 > 45m for 30m (region-scoped)
  - Route: PagerDuty (SRE: Fleet Readiness)

- **P1: Provision-to-Ready success rate below SLO**
  - Alert name: p2r_success_rate_low
  - Trigger (default): success_rate < 0.98 over 1h AND total_nodes_attempted >= 20
  - Route: PagerDuty (SRE) + Slack #eng-infra

- **P2: Quarantine spike**
  - Alert name: p2r_quarantine_rate_spike
  - Trigger (default): quarantine_events_rate > 3/hr for 1h (per pool_id)
  - Route: PagerDuty (Infra primary) during rollout windows; otherwise ticket

### Ticket / triage alerts
- **Validation flake rate elevated (non-paging)**
  - Alert name: p2r_validation_flake_high
  - Trigger (default): flake_rate > 2% over 6h for a single validation_name
  - Route: Jira (ENG backlog) + Slack #eng-sre (no page)

- **“Validating” stuck rate**
  - Alert name: p2r_validating_stuck
  - Trigger (default): nodes_in_state{state="VALIDATING"} age > 30m, count > 5
  - Route: Jira + Slack #eng-oncall

## Metric & dimension reference (for queries)
### Canonical event timestamps
Time-to-ready is computed from readiness events in the control plane:
- allocated_at: first time the node is known to the fleet allocator
- provisioning_started_at: orchestrator begins
- validating_started_at: validations begin
- ready_at: node marked READY (after all gates)
- quarantined_at: node moved to QUARANTINED (terminal until operator action)

### Key metrics (Prometheus)
These are the metrics most commonly used in dashboards/alerts:
- fleet_readiness_time_to_ready_seconds (histogram)
- fleet_readiness_step_duration_seconds (histogram)
- fleet_readiness_step_failures_total (counter)
- fleet_readiness_step_retries_total (counter)
- fleet_readiness_nodes_in_state (gauge)
- fleet_readiness_quarantine_events_total (counter)
- fleet_readiness_validation_result_total{result="pass|fail|timeout"} (counter)
- fleet_readiness_validation_flake_rate (gauge; derived)

### Standard dimensions (tags)
Allowed high-signal dimensions:
- env (prod/staging)
- region
- pool_type (dedicated/shared)
- gpu_sku
- image_channel (candidate/stable)
- image_version (limited; see note below)
- step_name
- validation_name

## Cardinality & tagging rules (required)
To keep metrics safe and dashboards usable:
- Do NOT tag metrics with per-node identifiers (node_id, instance_id, hostname).
- failure_reason must be **normalized** into a small enum-like set (e.g., DRIVER_MISMATCH, KUBELET_JOIN_FAILED, NCCL_TIMEOUT, GPU_HEALTH_XID, DISK_IOPS_LOW, NETWORK_THROUGHPUT_LOW).
- image_version tagging is allowed only for:
  - candidate rollouts
  - short retention windows (dashboards default to 24h)

If you see cardinality blow-ups, reference the mitigation playbook in the triage runbook and confirm that the “step tags” dashboards still load within 10s.

## How to use this catalog during on-call
1) Start with **SLO Overview** to confirm whether this is global or localized.
2) If localized, open **Failure Heatmap** and filter by affected region/gpu_sku.
3) Open **Step Latency Breakdown** and find the top regressed step_name.
4) If failures cluster around a validation, open **Validation Flake Rate** and check timeouts vs hard fails.
5) If nodes are quarantining, open **Quarantine & Overrides**, then follow the runbook for safe remediation order (drain → quarantine → reimage → retry).

## Access & permissions
- Grafana folder: “Fleet Readiness / Provision-to-Ready”
- Default read access: Platform + Infra + SRE
- To request access: file INT ticket and include business justification and least-privilege needs.

## Owners
- Dashboards & alert rules: Sergio Costa (Eng), Aisha Bello (SRE)
- Metric naming/standards: Allison Grant (Observability)
- Source services:
  - Fleet Readiness Service (control plane): Benji Okafor
  - Provisioning orchestrator (fleet automation): Gabe Wilson

## Changelog
- 2025-10-20: Added Rollout Guardrails dashboard; updated quarantine spike alert routing during staged rollouts.
- 2025-10-14: Removed per-node tags from step metrics; normalized failure_reason set; updated heatmap panels accordingly.
- 2025-10-07: Initial dashboard catalog created for sprint kickoff.
text:
## Purpose
This page is the catalog of **Grafana dashboards** and **alert rules** used to operate the Provision-to-Ready automation for Dedicated capacity and shared GPU fleet bring-up.

Dashboards are designed to answer:
- How long does it take to go from **Allocated → Ready** (overall + by step)?
- Where are we failing (step + error class), and is it isolated to a **region** or **GPU type**?
- Are nodes being **Quarantined** at an elevated rate, and what’s driving it?
- Which validation checks are **flaky** (pass/fail oscillation) vs consistently failing?

## Quick links (Grafana)
> Note: Links assume the internal Grafana base URL. If you use a different environment, swap the host.

### 1) Executive / SLO view
- **Provision-to-Ready: SLO Overview**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-slo/provision-to-ready-slo
  - Best for: weekly reporting, error budget posture, rollout go/no-go
  - Primary panels:
    - Time-to-Ready (P50/P95) – overall + by region
    - Success rate (Ready without quarantine) – overall + by GPU type
    - Error budget burn (fast/slow burn)
    - Top failure step (last 24h / 7d)
  - Default filters: env=prod, pool_type=dedicated

### 2) Per-step latency breakdown
- **Provision-to-Ready: Step Latency Breakdown**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-steps/provision-to-ready-steps
  - Best for: isolating which stage is contributing to time-to-ready regressions
  - Primary panels:
    - Step duration heatmap (p50/p95) by step_name
    - “Longest step per node” distribution
    - Retry counts per step (by attempt)
    - Step timeout rate
  - Common filters:
    - region (e.g., us-east-1)
    - gpu_sku (e.g., a100-80gb)
    - image_channel (candidate/stable)
    - rollout_flag (enabled/disabled)

### 3) Failure heatmaps (region × GPU type)
- **Provision-to-Ready: Failure Heatmap**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-fail-heat/provision-to-ready-failure-heatmap
  - Best for: quickly spotting localized failures (network, image, fabric, capacity provider)
  - Primary panels:
    - Failure rate heatmap (region × gpu_sku)
    - Failures by step_name (stacked) per region
    - Top failure_reason (normalized) per gpu_sku
  - Notes:
    - This dashboard intentionally uses **coarse failure reasons** (see “Cardinality & tagging” below).

### 4) Quarantine operations
- **Fleet Readiness: Quarantine & Overrides**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-quarantine/quarantine-operations
  - Best for: capacity shortfall risk and operational triage
  - Primary panels:
    - Quarantined node count (current) by region/pool_id
    - Quarantine events over time (rate)
    - Quarantine triggers by validation_name
    - Manual override actions (unquarantine, force-ready) count
  - Operator notes:
    - If quarantine count is rising and pool capacity is impacted, follow: confluence://eng-infra/runbooks/provision-to-ready-failures-triage-runbook

### 5) Validation flake rate (signal quality)
- **Provision-to-Ready: Validation Flake Rate**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-flake/validation-flake-rate
  - Best for: reducing noisy gates and preventing false quarantines
  - Primary panels:
    - Flake rate by validation_name (24h / 7d)
    - Pass→Fail and Fail→Pass transition counts
    - Timeout fraction by validation_name
    - NCCL smoke test: latency + timeout breakdown
  - When to use:
    - After rollout changes to timeouts/retries
    - When on-call reports “Validating stuck” or frequent oscillation

### 6) Rollout guardrails (candidate image / staged rollout)
- **Provision-to-Ready: Rollout Guardrails (Image + Flags)**
  - Link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/fleet-p2r-rollout/rollout-guardrails
  - Best for: staged rollout in a single region/GPU type and rapid rollback decisions
  - Primary panels:
    - Success rate by image_channel (candidate vs stable)
    - Time-to-ready p95 by image_version
    - Failure reasons after image change (24h window)
    - Auto-rollback triggers (alert state)

## Alert catalog (Grafana + routing)
Alert rules live in the observability pack and route via PagerDuty (SRE) and Jira (Infra) depending on severity.

### Paging (P1/P2) alerts
- **P2: Time-to-Ready p95 regression (prod / dedicated)**
  - Alert name: p2r_time_to_ready_p95_high
  - Dashboard panel: SLO Overview → Time-to-Ready p95
  - Trigger (default): p95 > 45m for 30m (region-scoped)
  - Route: PagerDuty (SRE: Fleet Readiness)

- **P1: Provision-to-Ready success rate below SLO**
  - Alert name: p2r_success_rate_low
  - Trigger (default): success_rate < 0.98 over 1h AND total_nodes_attempted >= 20
  - Route: PagerDuty (SRE) + Slack #eng-infra

- **P2: Quarantine spike**
  - Alert name: p2r_quarantine_rate_spike
  - Trigger (default): quarantine_events_rate > 3/hr for 1h (per pool_id)
  - Route: PagerDuty (Infra primary) during rollout windows; otherwise ticket

### Ticket / triage alerts
- **Validation flake rate elevated (non-paging)**
  - Alert name: p2r_validation_flake_high
  - Trigger (default): flake_rate > 2% over 6h for a single validation_name
  - Route: Jira (ENG backlog) + Slack #eng-sre (no page)

- **“Validating” stuck rate**
  - Alert name: p2r_validating_stuck
  - Trigger (default): nodes_in_state{state="VALIDATING"} age > 30m, count > 5
  - Route: Jira + Slack #eng-oncall

## Metric & dimension reference (for queries)
### Canonical event timestamps
Time-to-ready is computed from readiness events in the control plane:
- allocated_at: first time the node is known to the fleet allocator
- provisioning_started_at: orchestrator begins
- validating_started_at: validations begin
- ready_at: node marked READY (after all gates)
- quarantined_at: node moved to QUARANTINED (terminal until operator action)

### Key metrics (Prometheus)
These are the metrics most commonly used in dashboards/alerts:
- fleet_readiness_time_to_ready_seconds (histogram)
- fleet_readiness_step_duration_seconds (histogram)
- fleet_readiness_step_failures_total (counter)
- fleet_readiness_step_retries_total (counter)
- fleet_readiness_nodes_in_state (gauge)
- fleet_readiness_quarantine_events_total (counter)
- fleet_readiness_validation_result_total{result="pass|fail|timeout"} (counter)
- fleet_readiness_validation_flake_rate (gauge; derived)

### Standard dimensions (tags)
Allowed high-signal dimensions:
- env (prod/staging)
- region
- pool_type (dedicated/shared)
- gpu_sku
- image_channel (candidate/stable)
- image_version (limited; see note below)
- step_name
- validation_name

## Cardinality & tagging rules (required)
To keep metrics safe and dashboards usable:
- Do NOT tag metrics with per-node identifiers (node_id, instance_id, hostname).
- failure_reason must be **normalized** into a small enum-like set (e.g., DRIVER_MISMATCH, KUBELET_JOIN_FAILED, NCCL_TIMEOUT, GPU_HEALTH_XID, DISK_IOPS_LOW, NETWORK_THROUGHPUT_LOW).
- image_version tagging is allowed only for:
  - candidate rollouts
  - short retention windows (dashboards default to 24h)

If you see cardinality blow-ups, reference the mitigation playbook in the triage runbook and confirm that the “step tags” dashboards still load within 10s.

## How to use this catalog during on-call
1) Start with **SLO Overview** to confirm whether this is global or localized.
2) If localized, open **Failure Heatmap** and filter by affected region/gpu_sku.
3) Open **Step Latency Breakdown** and find the top regressed step_name.
4) If failures cluster around a validation, open **Validation Flake Rate** and check timeouts vs hard fails.
5) If nodes are quarantining, open **Quarantine & Overrides**, then follow the runbook for safe remediation order (drain → quarantine → reimage → retry).

## Access & permissions
- Grafana folder: “Fleet Readiness / Provision-to-Ready”
- Default read access: Platform + Infra + SRE
- To request access: file INT ticket and include business justification and least-privilege needs.

## Owners
- Dashboards & alert rules: Sergio Costa (Eng), Aisha Bello (SRE)
- Metric naming/standards: Allison Grant (Observability)
- Source services:
  - Fleet Readiness Service (control plane): Benji Okafor
  - Provisioning orchestrator (fleet automation): Gabe Wilson

## Changelog
- 2025-10-20: Added Rollout Guardrails dashboard; updated quarantine spike alert routing during staged rollouts.
- 2025-10-14: Removed per-node tags from step metrics; normalized failure_reason set; updated heatmap panels accordingly.
- 2025-10-07: Initial dashboard catalog created for sprint kickoff.

title: Capacity Policy Service (CPS) — Control Plane service for scaling policy evaluation
preview:
## Overview
Capacity Policy Service (CPS) is an internal control plane service responsible for storing, validating, and *deterministically evaluating* per-deployment scaling policies for **Redwood Dedicated**.

CPS exists to ensure consistent, auditable scaling semantics across:
- Console + API configuration (writes)
- Fleet autoscaler loops (reads/evaluations)
- Smart routing and downstream consumers that need a canonical “bounded” capacity state

**Primary responsibilities**
1. Persist per-deployment scaling policy configuration (min/max bounds, cooldowns, predictive mode settings).
2. Provide an evaluation API that resolves **effective policy** (customer config + platform defaults + feature flags) and returns a canonical, deterministic result.
3. Emit audit events for config changes and evaluations (who/when/what/why) to support enterprise change management.
4. Provide caching and rate-limiting primitives to keep evaluation latency low and protect storage.

**Non-responsibilities**
- CPS does not make scaling decisions (desired replicas) itself. It evaluates and clamps/scopes inputs from autoscaler and signal pipelines.
- CPS does not generate forecasts. It only consumes forecast metadata where needed for policy semantics (e.g., “predictive mode enabled”).

---

## Terminology
- **Deployment**: A Dedicated customer deployment (account_id + deployment_id + region).
- **Policy config**: The customer-configurable settings stored by CPS.
- **Platform defaults**: Redwood-defined defaults (per region, hardware class, or SKU) applied when policy fields are unset.
- **Effective policy**: The resolved policy used at runtime after defaults and feature-flag gating.
- **Requested desired**: Desired replicas computed by the autoscaler prior to clamping.
- **Bounded desired**: Desired replicas after enforcing min/max + safety invariants.

---

## Ownership and on-call
- **Owning team:** Platform Control Plane
- **DRI (engineering):** Ethan Park
- **Secondary DRIs:** Benji Okafor (API + RBAC), Jordan Lee (policy evaluation engine)
- **SRE partner:** Rafael Mendes

Support model:
- CPS is a Tier-1 dependency for Dedicated autoscaling. Platform on-call owns first response; SRE is engaged for sustained error budget burn or customer-impacting incidents.

---

## Architecture summary
CPS is deployed as a stateless service in each primary control plane region.

### Dependencies
- **Storage:** Postgres (control-plane cluster) for policy configs + change history.
- **Cache:** Redis for hot reads of effective policies and “last known good” results.
- **Eventing/audit:** Internal audit-log pipeline (Kafka → log archive + SIEM export) for policy change events and scaling evaluation events.
- **AuthZ:** Control-plane RBAC service.

### Data flow (high level)
1. Console/API updates policy → CPS validates → persists → emits **CapacityPolicyChanged** audit event.
2. Fleet autoscaler requests evaluation (or fetches effective policy) → CPS returns effective policy and (optionally) clamped result for a requested desired capacity.
3. CPS emits **ScalingPolicyEvaluated** audit event when evaluation is requested with a desired input (used for deterministic “why” trails).

---

## Public (internal) APIs
CPS is internal-only; customers interact via Console and the Dedicated management API, which calls CPS.

### API surface
CPS exposes:
- **REST** for control plane + Console backend.
- **gRPC** for high-QPS internal consumers (fleet autoscaler).

All APIs are idempotent and require request IDs.

### Resource identifiers
- account_id (string)
- deployment_id (string)
- region (string; e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-1)

Canonical resource key: `account_id/deployment_id/region`.

### Endpoints (REST)
**GET /v1/accounts/{account_id}/deployments/{deployment_id}/regions/{region}/scaling-policy**
- Returns stored policy config and resolved effective policy metadata.

**PUT /v1/accounts/{account_id}/deployments/{deployment_id}/regions/{region}/scaling-policy**
- Updates policy config.
- Requires RBAC permission: `dedicated.scaling_policy.write`.
- Emits CapacityPolicyChanged audit event.

**POST /v1/scaling-policy:evaluate**
- Input: deployment identity + evaluation context.
- Output: effective policy + bounded result (if requested_desired is provided) + reasons.
- Primary caller: fleet autoscaler.

**GET /v1/scaling-policy:effective**
- Batch variant (up to 200 deployments per request) returning effective policy only.
- Used by routing and periodic reconcilers.

### gRPC methods (canonical)
- `GetScalingPolicy(GetScalingPolicyRequest) returns (ScalingPolicyResponse)`
- `UpdateScalingPolicy(UpdateScalingPolicyRequest) returns (ScalingPolicyResponse)`
- `EvaluateScalingPolicy(EvaluateScalingPolicyRequest) returns (EvaluateScalingPolicyResponse)`
- `BatchGetEffectivePolicy(BatchGetEffectivePolicyRequest) returns (BatchGetEffectivePolicyResponse)`

---

## Policy schema (stored config)
The stored policy config is intentionally small and stable. The *evaluation response* may include additional derived fields.

### Stored fields
- `min_replicas` (int, optional): Hard floor for replicas.
- `max_replicas` (int, optional): Hard ceiling for replicas.
- `cooldown_up_seconds` (int, optional): Minimum time between scale-up actions.
- `cooldown_down_seconds` (int, optional): Minimum time between scale-down actions.
- `predictive_mode` (enum): `OFF | ADVISORY | ENFORCED` (see semantics below).
- `predictive_timezone` (string, optional): IANA TZ (e.g., America/Los_Angeles). Used only for interpreting customer schedules and for UI consistency.
- `notes` (string, optional): Customer-visible notes (limited length; sanitized; stored for change management).

### Validation rules (server-side)
- min/max:
  - If both set: `min_replicas <= max_replicas`.
  - `min_replicas >= 1` (Dedicated does not support scale-to-zero).
  - `max_replicas <= platform_absolute_max` (per SKU/region).
- cooldowns:
  - Range: 0–3600 seconds.
  - `cooldown_down_seconds >= cooldown_up_seconds` is recommended but not required; CPS returns a warning when violated.
- predictive:
  - Predictive mode requires feature flag enablement on the account (and sometimes per-deployment) to be effective.
  - If predictive mode is set but the flag is disabled, CPS treats it as OFF in *effective policy* and returns `effective_predictive_mode=OFF` with reason.

### Semantics of predictive_mode
- **OFF**: autoscaler uses reactive signals only.
- **ADVISORY**: predictions may bias scale-up/scale-down targets, but reactive saturation/queue triggers override prediction. CPS reports prediction availability in evaluation response; autoscaler is expected to continue operating normally if predictions are stale.
- **ENFORCED**: autoscaler is permitted to use predictions as a primary driver, but must still respect:
  - min/max bounds
  - cooldowns/anti-oscillation constraints
  - reactive “safety overrides” (e.g., queue depth sustained high) when prediction underestimates demand

Authoritative precedence and safety invariants are defined in the ADR listed in Related Pages.

---

## Evaluation API semantics
CPS evaluation is designed to be **deterministic and auditable**.

### Evaluate request (conceptual)
- deployment identity (account_id, deployment_id, region)
- `requested_desired_replicas` (int, optional)
- `signals` (optional metadata; not used for decisions in CPS, only for logging/reasoning)
  - `queue_depth` (float, optional)
  - `saturation` (float 0..1, optional)
  - `forecast_timestamp` (RFC3339, optional)
  - `forecast_horizon_seconds` (int, optional)
  - `forecast_staleness_seconds` (int, optional)
- `request_id` (string)
- `actor` (service identity; e.g., dedicated-autoscaler)

### Evaluate response (conceptual)
- `effective_min_replicas` (int)
- `effective_max_replicas` (int)
- `effective_cooldown_up_seconds` (int)
- `effective_cooldown_down_seconds` (int)
- `effective_predictive_mode` (enum)
- `bounded_desired_replicas` (int, optional)
- `reasons` (list of strings; stable reason codes)
- `warnings` (list of strings)
- `policy_version` (string; monotonic per deployment)

### Determinism requirements
For a given:
- policy_version
- platform defaults version
- feature-flag snapshot
- requested_desired_replicas

CPS must return the same bounded result and reason codes.

**Reason codes (examples)**
- `CLAMPED_TO_MIN`
- `CLAMPED_TO_MAX`
- `PREDICTIVE_DISABLED_BY_FLAG`
- `DEFAULT_MAX_APPLIED`
- `DEFAULT_COOLDOWN_APPLIED`
- `INVALID_INPUT_COERCED` (only for internal callers; Console should receive a 4xx instead)

---

## Storage model
### Tables (Postgres)
- `scaling_policy`
  - primary key: (account_id, deployment_id, region)
  - columns: min_replicas, max_replicas, cooldown_up_seconds, cooldown_down_seconds, predictive_mode, predictive_timezone, notes
  - metadata: created_at, updated_at, updated_by_actor, updated_by_user_id (nullable), policy_version
- `scaling_policy_change_history`
  - append-only record of changes (normalized diff + full snapshot)
  - used for internal investigations and compliance exports

### Consistency
- CPS provides **read-after-write** consistency for a single deployment within a region.
- Cross-region reads are eventually consistent; Console traffic is routed to the authoritative region for the deployment.

### Backward compatibility
When new fields are added:
- default behavior must match legacy behavior until explicitly enabled by feature flag and/or customer config
- schema migrations must be additive first (expand), then backfill, then contract (per standard migration policy)

---

## Caching strategy
CPS caches effective policy results to reduce load and tail latency.

### Cache keys
- `effective_policy:{account_id}:{deployment_id}:{region}:{policy_version}:{defaults_version}:{flag_snapshot_hash}`

### TTLs
- Effective policy cache TTL: 60 seconds (sliding)
- “Last known good” (LKG) effective policy: 15 minutes (used only when storage is degraded; must be explicitly marked as LKG in responses)

### Cache invalidation
- On policy update, CPS publishes an internal invalidation message and evicts keys by prefix (deployment-scoped).
- Default-version changes and flag changes are naturally reflected via the key components.

### Failure behavior
- If Redis is down: CPS falls back to Postgres reads (with rate limiting).
- If Postgres is impaired: CPS may serve LKG effective policy for up to 15 minutes, but **must not** accept writes.

---

## Audit logging
Auditability is a core requirement for enterprise Dedicated.

### Event types
1. **CapacityPolicyChanged**
   - Emitted on successful writes.
   - Fields (minimum): timestamp, account_id, deployment_id, region, actor_type (user/service), actor_id, request_id, previous_policy_hash, new_policy_hash, diff_summary, policy_version.

2. **ScalingPolicyEvaluated**
   - Emitted when Evaluate is called with a requested_desired.
   - Fields (minimum): timestamp, account_id, deployment_id, region, actor_service, request_id, policy_version, requested_desired, bounded_desired, reasons, effective_predictive_mode, forecast_metadata_present (bool).

### Retention
- Default retention: 90 days in hot search
- Archive retention: 400 days for enterprise Dedicated accounts (aligned with audit log product tier)

### Privacy and data handling
- Do not log raw customer prompts or model outputs.
- `notes` is customer-visible and may contain sensitive content; it is stored but must be redacted in broad internal search indices. Audit events should include a hash and truncated preview only.

---

## RBAC and authorization
- Read permission: `dedicated.scaling_policy.read`
- Write permission: `dedicated.scaling_policy.write`

Console writes require:
- Account Admin or Dedicated Operator role
- MFA enforced per enterprise policy (handled upstream by Console auth)

Service-to-service access:
- Autoscaler: read + evaluate
- Routing: read effective policy only (no writes)

All requests must include:
- request_id
- actor identity
- source service name

---

## Rate limiting and abuse protection
CPS protects itself and downstream dependencies using:
- Per-account write limits (default: 10 updates/minute/deployment; bursts allowed with token bucket)
- Global write circuit breaker during incidents
- Evaluate QPS shaping by caller (autoscaler is allowlisted with higher quota)

If a caller exceeds limits:
- Return 429 with `Retry-After`
- Emit audit event `ScalingPolicyRateLimited` (internal)

---

## Reliability: SLOs and expectations
CPS is a critical dependency for Dedicated autoscaling. The following SLOs apply per region.

### Service level objectives
- **Availability SLO:** 99.95% monthly for Get/Evaluate endpoints
- **Latency SLO:**
  - Evaluate: p99 < 50ms
  - GetEffective (batch): p99 < 150ms for 200 deployments
- **Durability:** Policy writes are committed to Postgres before acknowledging success.

### Error budget policy
- During feature rollout (predictive mode + bounds GA), CPS changes are gated behind feature flags and can be rolled back per account.
- If CPS error budget burn exceeds thresholds defined in the SRE doc (Related Pages), freeze non-urgent changes and prioritize mitigation.

### Degraded modes
- Cache degraded: allowed; expect higher latency but no functionality loss.
- Storage degraded: reads may serve LKG; writes disabled.

---

## Observability
CPS follows the scaling/capacity metric naming standard (Related Pages).

### Key metrics
- Request metrics:
  - `controlplane.cps.request.count` (labels: endpoint, status_code, caller)
  - `controlplane.cps.request.latency_ms` (histogram)
- Policy evaluation outcomes:
  - `controlplane.cps.evaluation.clamped_to_min.count`
  - `controlplane.cps.evaluation.clamped_to_max.count`
  - `controlplane.cps.evaluation.predictive_disabled_by_flag.count`
- Dependency health:
  - `controlplane.cps.redis.errors.count`
  - `controlplane.cps.postgres.errors.count`

### Logs
- Structured logs include request_id, deployment key, policy_version, reasons, and caller.
- Sensitive fields are hashed or omitted.

### Alerts (summary)
- p99 latency regression (Evaluate > 50ms for 10 minutes)
- 5xx rate > 0.5% for 5 minutes
- Postgres connection saturation
- Redis error spikes (non-paging unless correlated with latency/5xx)

---

## Operational guidelines
### Safe rollout checklist (CPS changes)
1. Validate backward compatibility (no behavior change without flag).
2. Add reason codes for any new evaluation behavior.
3. Ensure audit events include required fields and remain stable.
4. Load test Evaluate path (target: autoscaler peak QPS + 30%).
5. Canary in one region; monitor latency, 5xx, and clamping reason distribution.

### Common failure modes
- Misconfiguration: min > max (blocked at write time; Console shows validation error)
- Flag mismatch: customer sets predictive but feature flag disabled (effective mode OFF with reason)
- Storage incidents: inability to read policy (serve LKG, emit warnings; autoscaler should continue using last effective policy cached on its side if available)

---

## Appendix: Example evaluation (human readable)
Input:
- requested_desired_replicas: 40
- stored: min=10, max=32, predictive_mode=ADVISORY
- flags: predictive enabled

Output:
- effective_min_replicas: 10
- effective_max_replicas: 32
- bounded_desired_replicas: 32
- reasons: ["CLAMPED_TO_MAX"]

---

## Changelog
- 2025-06-20: Initial service doc created (bounds + predictive mode rollout planning).
- 2025-08-01: Added LKG behavior and explicit retention guidance for audit events.
- 2025-09-09: Updated SLO targets and cache key versioning to include flag snapshot hash.

4. Provide caching and rate-limiting primitives to keep evaluation latency low and protect storage.

**Non-responsibilities**
- CPS does not make scaling decisions (desired replicas) itself. It evaluates and clamps/scopes inputs from autoscaler and signal pipelines.
- CPS does not generate forecasts. It only consumes forecast metadata where needed for policy semantics (e.g., “predictive mode enabled”).

---

## Terminology
- **Deployment**: A Dedicated customer deployment (account_id + deployment_id + region).
- **Policy config**: The customer-configurable settings stored by CPS.
- **Platform defaults**: Redwood-defined defaults (per region, hardware class, or SKU) applied when policy fields are unset.
- **Effective policy**: The resolved policy used at runtime after defaults and feature-flag gating.
- **Requested desired**: Desired replicas computed by the autoscaler prior to clamping.
- **Bounded desired**: Desired replicas after enforcing min/max + safety invariants.

---

## Ownership and on-call
- **Owning team:** Platform Control Plane
- **DRI (engineering):** Ethan Park
- **Secondary DRIs:** Benji Okafor (API + RBAC), Jordan Lee (policy evaluation engine)
- **SRE partner:** Rafael Mendes

Support model:
- CPS is a Tier-1 dependency for Dedicated autoscaling. Platform on-call owns first response; SRE is engaged for sustained error budget burn or customer-impacting incidents.

---

## Architecture summary
CPS is deployed as a stateless service in each primary control plane region.

### Dependencies
- **Storage:** Postgres (control-plane cluster) for policy configs + change history.
- **Cache:** Redis for hot reads of effective policies and “last known good” results.
- **Eventing/audit:** Internal audit-log pipeline (Kafka → log archive + SIEM export) for policy change events and scaling evaluation events.
- **AuthZ:** Control-plane RBAC service.

### Data flow (high level)
1. Console/API updates policy → CPS validates → persists → emits **CapacityPolicyChanged** audit event.
2. Fleet autoscaler requests evaluation (or fetches effective policy) → CPS returns effective policy and (optionally) clamped result for a requested desired capacity.
3. CPS emits **ScalingPolicyEvaluated** audit event when evaluation is requested with a desired input (used for deterministic “why” trails).

---

## Public (internal) APIs
CPS is internal-only; customers interact via Console and the Dedicated management API, which calls CPS.

### API surface
CPS exposes:
- **REST** for control plane + Console backend.
- **gRPC** for high-QPS internal consumers (fleet autoscaler).

All APIs are idempotent and require request IDs.

### Resource identifiers
- account_id (string)
- deployment_id (string)
- region (string; e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-1)

Canonical resource key: `account_id/deployment_id/region`.

### Endpoints (REST)
**GET /v1/accounts/{account_id}/deployments/{deployment_id}/regions/{region}/scaling-policy**
- Returns stored policy config and resolved effective policy metadata.

**PUT /v1/accounts/{account_id}/deployments/{deployment_id}/regions/{region}/scaling-policy**
- Updates policy config.
- Requires RBAC permission: `dedicated.scaling_policy.write`.
- Emits CapacityPolicyChanged audit event.

**POST /v1/scaling-policy:evaluate**
- Input: deployment identity + evaluation context.
- Output: effective policy + bounded result (if requested_desired is provided) + reasons.
- Primary caller: fleet autoscaler.

**GET /v1/scaling-policy:effective**
- Batch variant (up to 200 deployments per request) returning effective policy only.
- Used by routing and periodic reconcilers.

### gRPC methods (canonical)
- `GetScalingPolicy(GetScalingPolicyRequest) returns (ScalingPolicyResponse)`
- `UpdateScalingPolicy(UpdateScalingPolicyRequest) returns (ScalingPolicyResponse)`
- `EvaluateScalingPolicy(EvaluateScalingPolicyRequest) returns (EvaluateScalingPolicyResponse)`
- `BatchGetEffectivePolicy(BatchGetEffectivePolicyRequest) returns (BatchGetEffectivePolicyResponse)`

---

## Policy schema (stored config)
The stored policy config is intentionally small and stable. The *evaluation response* may include additional derived fields.

### Stored fields
- `min_replicas` (int, optional): Hard floor for replicas.
- `max_replicas` (int, optional): Hard ceiling for replicas.
- `cooldown_up_seconds` (int, optional): Minimum time between scale-up actions.
- `cooldown_down_seconds` (int, optional): Minimum time between scale-down actions.
- `predictive_mode` (enum): `OFF | ADVISORY | ENFORCED` (see semantics below).
- `predictive_timezone` (string, optional): IANA TZ (e.g., America/Los_Angeles). Used only for interpreting customer schedules and for UI consistency.
- `notes` (string, optional): Customer-visible notes (limited length; sanitized; stored for change management).

### Validation rules (server-side)
- min/max:
  - If both set: `min_replicas <= max_replicas`.
  - `min_replicas >= 1` (Dedicated does not support scale-to-zero).
  - `max_replicas <= platform_absolute_max` (per SKU/region).
- cooldowns:
  - Range: 0–3600 seconds.
  - `cooldown_down_seconds >= cooldown_up_seconds` is recommended but not required; CPS returns a warning when violated.
- predictive:
  - Predictive mode requires feature flag enablement on the account (and sometimes per-deployment) to be effective.
  - If predictive mode is set but the flag is disabled, CPS treats it as OFF in *effective policy* and returns `effective_predictive_mode=OFF` with reason.

### Semantics of predictive_mode
- **OFF**: autoscaler uses reactive signals only.
- **ADVISORY**: predictions may bias scale-up/scale-down targets, but reactive saturation/queue triggers override prediction. CPS reports prediction availability in evaluation response; autoscaler is expected to continue operating normally if predictions are stale.
- **ENFORCED**: autoscaler is permitted to use predictions as a primary driver, but must still respect:
  - min/max bounds
  - cooldowns/anti-oscillation constraints
  - reactive “safety overrides” (e.g., queue depth sustained high) when prediction underestimates demand

Authoritative precedence and safety invariants are defined in the ADR listed in Related Pages.

---

## Evaluation API semantics
CPS evaluation is designed to be **deterministic and auditable**.

### Evaluate request (conceptual)
- deployment identity (account_id, deployment_id, region)
- `requested_desired_replicas` (int, optional)
- `signals` (optional metadata; not used for decisions in CPS, only for logging/reasoning)
  - `queue_depth` (float, optional)
  - `saturation` (float 0..1, optional)
  - `forecast_timestamp` (RFC3339, optional)
  - `forecast_horizon_seconds` (int, optional)
  - `forecast_staleness_seconds` (int, optional)
- `request_id` (string)
- `actor` (service identity; e.g., dedicated-autoscaler)

### Evaluate response (conceptual)
- `effective_min_replicas` (int)
- `effective_max_replicas` (int)
- `effective_cooldown_up_seconds` (int)
- `effective_cooldown_down_seconds` (int)
- `effective_predictive_mode` (enum)
- `bounded_desired_replicas` (int, optional)
- `reasons` (list of strings; stable reason codes)
- `warnings` (list of strings)
- `policy_version` (string; monotonic per deployment)

### Determinism requirements
For a given:
- policy_version
- platform defaults version
- feature-flag snapshot
- requested_desired_replicas

CPS must return the same bounded result and reason codes.

**Reason codes (examples)**
- `CLAMPED_TO_MIN`
- `CLAMPED_TO_MAX`
- `PREDICTIVE_DISABLED_BY_FLAG`
- `DEFAULT_MAX_APPLIED`
- `DEFAULT_COOLDOWN_APPLIED`
- `INVALID_INPUT_COERCED` (only for internal callers; Console should receive a 4xx instead)

---

## Storage model
### Tables (Postgres)
- `scaling_policy`
  - primary key: (account_id, deployment_id, region)
  - columns: min_replicas, max_replicas, cooldown_up_seconds, cooldown_down_seconds, predictive_mode, predictive_timezone, notes
  - metadata: created_at, updated_at, updated_by_actor, updated_by_user_id (nullable), policy_version
- `scaling_policy_change_history`
  - append-only record of changes (normalized diff + full snapshot)
  - used for internal investigations and compliance exports

### Consistency
- CPS provides **read-after-write** consistency for a single deployment within a region.
- Cross-region reads are eventually consistent; Console traffic is routed to the authoritative region for the deployment.

### Backward compatibility
When new fields are added:
- default behavior must match legacy behavior until explicitly enabled by feature flag and/or customer config
- schema migrations must be additive first (expand), then backfill, then contract (per standard migration policy)

---

## Caching strategy
CPS caches effective policy results to reduce load and tail latency.

### Cache keys
- `effective_policy:{account_id}:{deployment_id}:{region}:{policy_version}:{defaults_version}:{flag_snapshot_hash}`

### TTLs
- Effective policy cache TTL: 60 seconds (sliding)
- “Last known good” (LKG) effective policy: 15 minutes (used only when storage is degraded; must be explicitly marked as LKG in responses)

### Cache invalidation
- On policy update, CPS publishes an internal invalidation message and evicts keys by prefix (deployment-scoped).
- Default-version changes and flag changes are naturally reflected via the key components.

### Failure behavior
- If Redis is down: CPS falls back to Postgres reads (with rate limiting).
- If Postgres is impaired: CPS may serve LKG effective policy for up to 15 minutes, but **must not** accept writes.

---

## Audit logging
Auditability is a core requirement for enterprise Dedicated.

### Event types
1. **CapacityPolicyChanged**
   - Emitted on successful writes.
   - Fields (minimum): timestamp, account_id, deployment_id, region, actor_type (user/service), actor_id, request_id, previous_policy_hash, new_policy_hash, diff_summary, policy_version.

2. **ScalingPolicyEvaluated**
   - Emitted when Evaluate is called with a requested_desired.
   - Fields (minimum): timestamp, account_id, deployment_id, region, actor_service, request_id, policy_version, requested_desired, bounded_desired, reasons, effective_predictive_mode, forecast_metadata_present (bool).

### Retention
- Default retention: 90 days in hot search
- Archive retention: 400 days for enterprise Dedicated accounts (aligned with audit log product tier)

### Privacy and data handling
- Do not log raw customer prompts or model outputs.
- `notes` is customer-visible and may contain sensitive content; it is stored but must be redacted in broad internal search indices. Audit events should include a hash and truncated preview only.

---

## RBAC and authorization
- Read permission: `dedicated.scaling_policy.read`
- Write permission: `dedicated.scaling_policy.write`

Console writes require:
- Account Admin or Dedicated Operator role
- MFA enforced per enterprise policy (handled upstream by Console auth)

Service-to-service access:
- Autoscaler: read + evaluate
- Routing: read effective policy only (no writes)

All requests must include:
- request_id
- actor identity
- source service name

---

## Rate limiting and abuse protection
CPS protects itself and downstream dependencies using:
- Per-account write limits (default: 10 updates/minute/deployment; bursts allowed with token bucket)
- Global write circuit breaker during incidents
- Evaluate QPS shaping by caller (autoscaler is allowlisted with higher quota)

If a caller exceeds limits:
- Return 429 with `Retry-After`
- Emit audit event `ScalingPolicyRateLimited` (internal)

---

## Reliability: SLOs and expectations
CPS is a critical dependency for Dedicated autoscaling. The following SLOs apply per region.

### Service level objectives
- **Availability SLO:** 99.95% monthly for Get/Evaluate endpoints
- **Latency SLO:**
  - Evaluate: p99 < 50ms
  - GetEffective (batch): p99 < 150ms for 200 deployments
- **Durability:** Policy writes are committed to Postgres before acknowledging success.

### Error budget policy
- During feature rollout (predictive mode + bounds GA), CPS changes are gated behind feature flags and can be rolled back per account.
- If CPS error budget burn exceeds thresholds defined in the SRE doc (Related Pages), freeze non-urgent changes and prioritize mitigation.

### Degraded modes
- Cache degraded: allowed; expect higher latency but no functionality loss.
- Storage degraded: reads may serve LKG; writes disabled.

---

## Observability
CPS follows the scaling/capacity metric naming standard (Related Pages).

### Key metrics
- Request metrics:
  - `controlplane.cps.request.count` (labels: endpoint, status_code, caller)
  - `controlplane.cps.request.latency_ms` (histogram)
- Policy evaluation outcomes:
  - `controlplane.cps.evaluation.clamped_to_min.count`
  - `controlplane.cps.evaluation.clamped_to_max.count`
  - `controlplane.cps.evaluation.predictive_disabled_by_flag.count`
- Dependency health:
  - `controlplane.cps.redis.errors.count`
  - `controlplane.cps.postgres.errors.count`

### Logs
- Structured logs include request_id, deployment key, policy_version, reasons, and caller.
- Sensitive fields are hashed or omitted.

### Alerts (summary)
- p99 latency regression (Evaluate > 50ms for 10 minutes)
- 5xx rate > 0.5% for 5 minutes
- Postgres connection saturation
- Redis error spikes (non-paging unless correlated with latency/5xx)

---

## Operational guidelines
### Safe rollout checklist (CPS changes)
1. Validate backward compatibility (no behavior change without flag).
2. Add reason codes for any new evaluation behavior.
3. Ensure audit events include required fields and remain stable.
4. Load test Evaluate path (target: autoscaler peak QPS + 30%).
5. Canary in one region; monitor latency, 5xx, and clamping reason distribution.

### Common failure modes
- Misconfiguration: min > max (blocked at write time; Console shows validation error)
- Flag mismatch: customer sets predictive but feature flag disabled (effective mode OFF with reason)
- Storage incidents: inability to read policy (serve LKG, emit warnings; autoscaler should continue using last effective policy cached on its side if available)

---

## Appendix: Example evaluation (human readable)
Input:
- requested_desired_replicas: 40
- stored: min=10, max=32, predictive_mode=ADVISORY
- flags: predictive enabled

Output:
- effective_min_replicas: 10
- effective_max_replicas: 32
- bounded_desired_replicas: 32
- reasons: ["CLAMPED_TO_MAX"]

---

## Changelog
- 2025-06-20: Initial service doc created (bounds + predictive mode rollout planning).
- 2025-08-01: Added LKG behavior and explicit retention guidance for audit events.
- 2025-09-09: Updated SLO targets and cache key versioning to include flag snapshot hash.

title: Private Observability Minimum Pack (v1) — Grafana Dashboard Catalog
preview:
## Purpose
This page catalogs the Grafana dashboards shipped in **Redwood Private Observability Minimum Pack (v1)**. The goal is to provide a consistent set of *supported* dashboards for Private installs (VPC + on-prem) that cover the highest-impact failure modes for LLM inference.

This catalog is used by:
- Customer platform teams validating a Private install/upgrade
- Redwood Support/SRE during escalations
- Engineering teams owning instrumentation, dashboards, and alert links

## Where these dashboards live
- **Grafana folder (default):** `Redwood / Private / Minimum Pack`
- **Data source (default):** `Prometheus` (in-cluster)
- **Dashboard versioning:** Dashboards are shipped as versioned artifacts via `observability-pack` and wired into `redwood-helm-charts`.
- **Assumption:** Metrics names/labels follow the *Private metrics & cardinality standards*. Any dashboard relying on high-cardinality labels must use bounded aggregations.

## What “v1 minimum pack” means
Included dashboards must:
- Render with *no bespoke per-customer wiring* beyond standard Helm values
- Avoid unbounded metric label cardinality (no raw `tenant_id`, request payload fields, etc.)
- Provide at least one “what to check first” section in the dashboard description
- Be compatible with offline/air-gapped installs (no external panel plugins required)

Non-goals for v1:
- Customer-specific dashboards
- Deep tracing views (OTLP traces may be documented, but are not required for v1)

## Quickstart: What to validate after install/upgrade
1) Open **(RI-PVT-001) Private Overview** and confirm:
   - Requests are flowing (RPS > 0)
   - Error rate is within expected baseline
   - p95 latency is within expected baseline
2) Open **(RI-PVT-101) Serving Runtime — Golden Signals**:
   - Check queue depth/backpressure panels
   - Check GPU utilization and memory headroom
3) Open **(RI-PVT-201) Control Plane — Health**:
   - Confirm all control plane deployments are Ready
   - Confirm gateway/auth upstream errors are not elevated

---

## Dashboard catalog (v1)
**Legend**
- **Tier:** Core (always installed) | Optional (installed when component enabled)
- **Owner team:** Team responsible for dashboard upkeep and primary instrumentation

### Summary table
| ID | Dashboard name | Tier | Primary use | Primary signals | Owner team | Owner (DRI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RI-PVT-001 | Private Overview | Core | Exec summary for health + SLO posture | RPS, errors, p95/p99, saturation | SRE / Observability | Rafael Mendes |
| RI-PVT-101 | Serving Runtime — Golden Signals | Core | Runtime health and “is inference working?” | RPS, errors, latency, GPU, OOM/restarts | Serving Runtime / Telemetry | Omar El-Sayed |
| RI-PVT-102 | Serving Runtime — Queueing & Batching | Core | Diagnose backpressure and throughput regressions | queue depth, batch size, tokens/s | Serving Runtime | Omar El-Sayed |
| RI-PVT-103 | Serving Runtime — KV Cache & Context | Core | Diagnose context-length + cache issues | cache hit rate, KV mem, evictions | Serving Runtime | Omar El-Sayed |
| RI-PVT-111 | Serving Runtime — Per Route / Model Drilldown | Core | Identify hot routes and model-specific issues (bounded) | per-route aggregates, model groupings | Telemetry Platform | Nadia Rahman |
| RI-PVT-151 | GPU Fleet — Utilization & Memory | Core | Hardware saturation + scheduling issues | GPU util, mem, thermals (if available) | Infra / SRE | Sean Gallagher |
| RI-PVT-201 | Control Plane — Health | Core | Control plane readiness + dependency failures | deployment health, error rates | Platform / Control Plane | Ethan Park |
| RI-PVT-202 | API Gateway — Requests & Upstreams | Core | Edge errors, auth failures, upstream latency | 4xx/5xx, upstream timeouts | Platform / Edge | Ethan Park |
| RI-PVT-203 | Routing & Policy Engine | Core | Misroutes, fallback storms, policy misconfig | fallback rate, policy eval latency | Platform / Routing | Logan Wright |
| RI-PVT-221 | Model Registry & Artifact Sync | Optional | Model pull/sync failures affecting serving | sync errors, download latency | Platform / Registry Integrations | Quentin Harris |
| RI-PVT-241 | Installer & Upgrade Controller | Core | Upgrade safety + reconcile loops | reconcile errors, duration, retries | Private Deployments | Diego Santos |
| RI-PVT-301 | Prometheus — Scrape & TSDB Health | Core | Metrics pipeline health | scrape failures, TSDB size, WAL | Infra / Observability | Allison Grant |
| RI-PVT-311 | Grafana — Availability & Datasource | Core | Dashboard availability issues | Grafana errors, datasource health | Infra / Observability | Allison Grant |
| RI-PVT-401 | Log Pipeline — Collector Health | Optional | Log drops/backpressure | buffer fill, retry, drops | Telemetry Platform | Caleb Johnson |

---

## Dashboard details

### (RI-PVT-001) Private Overview
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Is the Private install healthy right now?
- Are we burning latency/availability SLOs?
- Is the issue data plane (runtime) vs control plane?

**Key panels**
- Request rate (RPS) and token throughput (tokens/sec)
- HTTP/gRPC error rate (4xx vs 5xx) with top-level breakdown
- Latency p50/p95/p99 (end-to-end)
- Saturation summary: GPU util, queue depth, pod restarts
- “Top active alerts” (if Alertmanager is connected)

**Drilldowns (links)**
- Runtime deep dive → **RI-PVT-101 / RI-PVT-102**
- Control plane deep dive → **RI-PVT-201 / RI-PVT-202**
- Metrics pipeline health → **RI-PVT-301**

**Owner team:** SRE / Observability

---

### (RI-PVT-101) Serving Runtime — Golden Signals
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Is inference serving successfully at expected latency?
- Is error rate elevated due to saturation (GPU/memory) or upstream/downstream errors?

**Key panels**
- Requests: RPS, concurrency (in-flight), active pods
- Errors: 5xx rate, timeout rate, cancellation rate
- Latency: p50/p95/p99; breakdown if available (queue vs compute)
- GPU: utilization, memory used, OOM events
- Pod health: restarts, CrashLoopBackOff counts

**Notes on label cardinality**
- Any route/model breakdowns shown here must use **bounded aggregations** (e.g., top-N by traffic; model family buckets) per Private standards.

**Owner team:** Serving Runtime / Telemetry

---

### (RI-PVT-102) Serving Runtime — Queueing & Batching
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are we backpressuring because of queue depth or batching constraints?
- Did a config change reduce batching efficiency?

**Key panels**
- Queue depth (current, max) and queue wait time percentiles
- Batch size distribution and “effective batch size” time series
- Tokens/sec (generated + processed), request/sec
- Rejects/429s due to capacity limits

**Common failure signatures**
- Rising queue wait + flat GPU util → scheduling/placement or bottleneck upstream of GPU
- Rising queue wait + high GPU util + rising p99 → saturation; consider scaling or batch tuning

**Owner team:** Serving Runtime

---

### (RI-PVT-103) Serving Runtime — KV Cache & Context
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are long contexts causing memory pressure and latency spikes?
- Is caching performing as expected (hit rate, eviction rate)?

**Key panels**
- KV cache memory allocated vs used; eviction rate
- Cache hit ratio (prefix/KV where applicable)
- Context length percentiles (bounded buckets)
- OOM kills correlated with context length spikes

**Owner team:** Serving Runtime

---

### (RI-PVT-111) Serving Runtime — Per Route / Model Drilldown
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Which routes or model families are driving load and/or errors?
- Did one route experience a regression after a rollout?

**Key panels**
- Top-N routes by RPS (bounded)
- Top-N routes by error rate (bounded)
- Latency percentiles by route group
- Model family / quantization profile aggregates (no raw per-tenant breakdown)

**Implementation note**
- This dashboard intentionally avoids raw `tenant_id` and uses hashed/bucketed identifiers only when explicitly enabled by config.

**Owner team:** Telemetry Platform

---

### (RI-PVT-151) GPU Fleet — Utilization & Memory
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are GPUs saturated or underutilized?
- Are there node-level hotspots driving tail latency?

**Key panels**
- GPU utilization heatmap by node (bounded to cluster size)
- GPU memory used and headroom
- GPU OOM / Xid errors (when exposed)
- Node CPU/memory pressure and eviction signals

**Dependencies**
- Requires NVIDIA DCGM exporter (or equivalent) enabled in the Private chart values.

**Owner team:** Infra / SRE

---

### (RI-PVT-201) Control Plane — Health
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are control plane services up and responding?
- Are we failing to reconcile config or apply policies?

**Key panels**
- Deployment readiness (desired vs ready replicas)
- Service error rate (by component)
- Internal request latency (p95/p99)
- Work queue / reconcile loop metrics (where applicable)

**Owner team:** Platform / Control Plane

---

### (RI-PVT-202) API Gateway — Requests & Upstreams
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are edge errors driven by auth, rate limiting, or upstream failures?
- Are we timing out to the runtime service?

**Key panels**
- Request rate and response code class (2xx/4xx/5xx)
- Auth failures (401/403) and rate limit responses (429)
- Upstream latency and upstream error rate
- TLS handshake / connection errors (if instrumented)

**Owner team:** Platform / Edge

---

### (RI-PVT-203) Routing & Policy Engine
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Is routing behaving as expected (including fallback policies)?
- Are policy evaluations slow or failing?

**Key panels**
- Policy evaluation latency and error rate
- Fallback events (rate, reasons)
- Capacity tier / region routing decisions (aggregated)
- “Fallback storms” detector panel (sustained elevated fallback rate)

**Owner team:** Platform / Routing

---

### (RI-PVT-221) Model Registry & Artifact Sync
**Tier:** Optional (enabled when registry integration is configured)

**Primary questions answered**
- Are model artifacts syncing/pulling successfully?
- Is model load failing due to missing blobs, auth, or storage pressure?

**Key panels**
- Sync job success rate / errors
- Artifact download duration and retry counts
- Storage utilization / free space (for artifact cache)

**Owner team:** Platform / Registry Integrations

---

### (RI-PVT-241) Installer & Upgrade Controller
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are installs/upgrades stuck, failing preflight, or retrying?
- Did a reconcile loop start thrashing after a config change?

**Key panels**
- Preflight checks: pass/fail counts by category (storage/network/resources)
- Upgrade duration histogram; success/failure rate
- Reconcile errors and retry backoff
- Helm release status signals (where available)

**Owner team:** Private Deployments

---

### (RI-PVT-301) Prometheus — Scrape & TSDB Health
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are dashboards empty because Prometheus scrapes are failing?
- Is Prometheus underprovisioned (CPU/mem/disk) for retention settings?

**Key panels**
- Target health: up/down by job
- Scrape duration and sample ingestion rate
- TSDB size, WAL growth, compaction duration
- Remote write health (if enabled)

**Owner team:** Infra / Observability

---

### (RI-PVT-311) Grafana — Availability & Datasource
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Is Grafana up and responding?
- Is the Prometheus datasource misconfigured/auth failing?

**Key panels**
- Grafana pod health and restarts
- HTTP 5xx rate from Grafana
- Datasource query error rate and latency (if instrumented)

**Owner team:** Infra / Observability

---

### (RI-PVT-401) Log Pipeline — Collector Health
**Tier:** Optional (enabled when log collection/forwarding is enabled)

**Primary questions answered**
- Are we dropping logs due to backpressure?
- Are exports failing (auth/network) to customer sinks?

**Key panels**
- Buffer fill %, retries, and dropped log counters
- Export throughput and failure rate by sink type
- CPU/memory usage of collector pods

**Security note**
- This dashboard should not surface sensitive log content. It focuses on pipeline health counters only.

**Owner team:** Telemetry Platform

---

## Naming conventions
- Dashboard IDs use the prefix `RI-PVT-` and a stable numeric range:
  - `001–099`: Overview
  - `101–199`: Serving runtime
  - `201–299`: Control plane
  - `301–399`: Observability infrastructure (Prometheus/Grafana)
  - `401–499`: Logging pipeline

## Support expectations (what is covered)
Redwood Support treats the above dashboards as part of the **supported minimum** for Private installs.

Support will:
- Validate that dashboards render with documented Helm/installer configuration
- Use these dashboards during incident triage
- File issues against owners when panels are broken due to metric/label changes

Support will not:
- Customize dashboards per customer beyond documented values and toggles
- Build customer-specific alerting strategies beyond v1 defaults (see SLO guidance)

## Change process
- Dashboard changes must be accompanied by:
  - A note in the release notes / upgrade guidance when panels or metric dependencies change
  - Backward-compatible metric transitions (or explicit migration instructions)
  - A quick validation in the private smoke-test harness (dashboards render + required queries return data)

## Appendix: Known gaps (tracked for future iterations)
- Tracing-first latency breakdowns (queue vs compute vs upstream) are limited in v1
- Limited per-tenant debugging by design (cardinality + privacy). Escalation path is to use customer-controlled logs/audit trails.
- Some GPU hardware error signals (Xid/thermal) may vary by driver/exporter version
text:
## Purpose
This page catalogs the Grafana dashboards shipped in **Redwood Private Observability Minimum Pack (v1)**. The goal is to provide a consistent set of *supported* dashboards for Private installs (VPC + on-prem) that cover the highest-impact failure modes for LLM inference.

This catalog is used by:
- Customer platform teams validating a Private install/upgrade
- Redwood Support/SRE during escalations
- Engineering teams owning instrumentation, dashboards, and alert links

## Where these dashboards live
- **Grafana folder (default):** `Redwood / Private / Minimum Pack`
- **Data source (default):** `Prometheus` (in-cluster)
- **Dashboard versioning:** Dashboards are shipped as versioned artifacts via `observability-pack` and wired into `redwood-helm-charts`.
- **Assumption:** Metrics names/labels follow the *Private metrics & cardinality standards*. Any dashboard relying on high-cardinality labels must use bounded aggregations.

## What “v1 minimum pack” means
Included dashboards must:
- Render with *no bespoke per-customer wiring* beyond standard Helm values
- Avoid unbounded metric label cardinality (no raw `tenant_id`, request payload fields, etc.)
- Provide at least one “what to check first” section in the dashboard description
- Be compatible with offline/air-gapped installs (no external panel plugins required)

Non-goals for v1:
- Customer-specific dashboards
- Deep tracing views (OTLP traces may be documented, but are not required for v1)

## Quickstart: What to validate after install/upgrade
1) Open **(RI-PVT-001) Private Overview** and confirm:
   - Requests are flowing (RPS > 0)
   - Error rate is within expected baseline
   - p95 latency is within expected baseline
2) Open **(RI-PVT-101) Serving Runtime — Golden Signals**:
   - Check queue depth/backpressure panels
   - Check GPU utilization and memory headroom
3) Open **(RI-PVT-201) Control Plane — Health**:
   - Confirm all control plane deployments are Ready
   - Confirm gateway/auth upstream errors are not elevated

---

## Dashboard catalog (v1)
**Legend**
- **Tier:** Core (always installed) | Optional (installed when component enabled)
- **Owner team:** Team responsible for dashboard upkeep and primary instrumentation

### Summary table
| ID | Dashboard name | Tier | Primary use | Primary signals | Owner team | Owner (DRI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RI-PVT-001 | Private Overview | Core | Exec summary for health + SLO posture | RPS, errors, p95/p99, saturation | SRE / Observability | Rafael Mendes |
| RI-PVT-101 | Serving Runtime — Golden Signals | Core | Runtime health and “is inference working?” | RPS, errors, latency, GPU, OOM/restarts | Serving Runtime / Telemetry | Omar El-Sayed |
| RI-PVT-102 | Serving Runtime — Queueing & Batching | Core | Diagnose backpressure and throughput regressions | queue depth, batch size, tokens/s | Serving Runtime | Omar El-Sayed |
| RI-PVT-103 | Serving Runtime — KV Cache & Context | Core | Diagnose context-length + cache issues | cache hit rate, KV mem, evictions | Serving Runtime | Omar El-Sayed |
| RI-PVT-111 | Serving Runtime — Per Route / Model Drilldown | Core | Identify hot routes and model-specific issues (bounded) | per-route aggregates, model groupings | Telemetry Platform | Nadia Rahman |
| RI-PVT-151 | GPU Fleet — Utilization & Memory | Core | Hardware saturation + scheduling issues | GPU util, mem, thermals (if available) | Infra / SRE | Sean Gallagher |
| RI-PVT-201 | Control Plane — Health | Core | Control plane readiness + dependency failures | deployment health, error rates | Platform / Control Plane | Ethan Park |
| RI-PVT-202 | API Gateway — Requests & Upstreams | Core | Edge errors, auth failures, upstream latency | 4xx/5xx, upstream timeouts | Platform / Edge | Ethan Park |
| RI-PVT-203 | Routing & Policy Engine | Core | Misroutes, fallback storms, policy misconfig | fallback rate, policy eval latency | Platform / Routing | Logan Wright |
| RI-PVT-221 | Model Registry & Artifact Sync | Optional | Model pull/sync failures affecting serving | sync errors, download latency | Platform / Registry Integrations | Quentin Harris |
| RI-PVT-241 | Installer & Upgrade Controller | Core | Upgrade safety + reconcile loops | reconcile errors, duration, retries | Private Deployments | Diego Santos |
| RI-PVT-301 | Prometheus — Scrape & TSDB Health | Core | Metrics pipeline health | scrape failures, TSDB size, WAL | Infra / Observability | Allison Grant |
| RI-PVT-311 | Grafana — Availability & Datasource | Core | Dashboard availability issues | Grafana errors, datasource health | Infra / Observability | Allison Grant |
| RI-PVT-401 | Log Pipeline — Collector Health | Optional | Log drops/backpressure | buffer fill, retry, drops | Telemetry Platform | Caleb Johnson |

---

## Dashboard details

### (RI-PVT-001) Private Overview
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Is the Private install healthy right now?
- Are we burning latency/availability SLOs?
- Is the issue data plane (runtime) vs control plane?

**Key panels**
- Request rate (RPS) and token throughput (tokens/sec)
- HTTP/gRPC error rate (4xx vs 5xx) with top-level breakdown
- Latency p50/p95/p99 (end-to-end)
- Saturation summary: GPU util, queue depth, pod restarts
- “Top active alerts” (if Alertmanager is connected)

**Drilldowns (links)**
- Runtime deep dive → **RI-PVT-101 / RI-PVT-102**
- Control plane deep dive → **RI-PVT-201 / RI-PVT-202**
- Metrics pipeline health → **RI-PVT-301**

**Owner team:** SRE / Observability

---

### (RI-PVT-101) Serving Runtime — Golden Signals
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Is inference serving successfully at expected latency?
- Is error rate elevated due to saturation (GPU/memory) or upstream/downstream errors?

**Key panels**
- Requests: RPS, concurrency (in-flight), active pods
- Errors: 5xx rate, timeout rate, cancellation rate
- Latency: p50/p95/p99; breakdown if available (queue vs compute)
- GPU: utilization, memory used, OOM events
- Pod health: restarts, CrashLoopBackOff counts

**Notes on label cardinality**
- Any route/model breakdowns shown here must use **bounded aggregations** (e.g., top-N by traffic; model family buckets) per Private standards.

**Owner team:** Serving Runtime / Telemetry

---

### (RI-PVT-102) Serving Runtime — Queueing & Batching
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are we backpressuring because of queue depth or batching constraints?
- Did a config change reduce batching efficiency?

**Key panels**
- Queue depth (current, max) and queue wait time percentiles
- Batch size distribution and “effective batch size” time series
- Tokens/sec (generated + processed), request/sec
- Rejects/429s due to capacity limits

**Common failure signatures**
- Rising queue wait + flat GPU util → scheduling/placement or bottleneck upstream of GPU
- Rising queue wait + high GPU util + rising p99 → saturation; consider scaling or batch tuning

**Owner team:** Serving Runtime

---

### (RI-PVT-103) Serving Runtime — KV Cache & Context
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are long contexts causing memory pressure and latency spikes?
- Is caching performing as expected (hit rate, eviction rate)?

**Key panels**
- KV cache memory allocated vs used; eviction rate
- Cache hit ratio (prefix/KV where applicable)
- Context length percentiles (bounded buckets)
- OOM kills correlated with context length spikes

**Owner team:** Serving Runtime

---

### (RI-PVT-111) Serving Runtime — Per Route / Model Drilldown
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Which routes or model families are driving load and/or errors?
- Did one route experience a regression after a rollout?

**Key panels**
- Top-N routes by RPS (bounded)
- Top-N routes by error rate (bounded)
- Latency percentiles by route group
- Model family / quantization profile aggregates (no raw per-tenant breakdown)

**Implementation note**
- This dashboard intentionally avoids raw `tenant_id` and uses hashed/bucketed identifiers only when explicitly enabled by config.

**Owner team:** Telemetry Platform

---

### (RI-PVT-151) GPU Fleet — Utilization & Memory
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are GPUs saturated or underutilized?
- Are there node-level hotspots driving tail latency?

**Key panels**
- GPU utilization heatmap by node (bounded to cluster size)
- GPU memory used and headroom
- GPU OOM / Xid errors (when exposed)
- Node CPU/memory pressure and eviction signals

**Dependencies**
- Requires NVIDIA DCGM exporter (or equivalent) enabled in the Private chart values.

**Owner team:** Infra / SRE

---

### (RI-PVT-201) Control Plane — Health
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are control plane services up and responding?
- Are we failing to reconcile config or apply policies?

**Key panels**
- Deployment readiness (desired vs ready replicas)
- Service error rate (by component)
- Internal request latency (p95/p99)
- Work queue / reconcile loop metrics (where applicable)

**Owner team:** Platform / Control Plane

---

### (RI-PVT-202) API Gateway — Requests & Upstreams
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are edge errors driven by auth, rate limiting, or upstream failures?
- Are we timing out to the runtime service?

**Key panels**
- Request rate and response code class (2xx/4xx/5xx)
- Auth failures (401/403) and rate limit responses (429)
- Upstream latency and upstream error rate
- TLS handshake / connection errors (if instrumented)

**Owner team:** Platform / Edge

---

### (RI-PVT-203) Routing & Policy Engine
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Is routing behaving as expected (including fallback policies)?
- Are policy evaluations slow or failing?

**Key panels**
- Policy evaluation latency and error rate
- Fallback events (rate, reasons)
- Capacity tier / region routing decisions (aggregated)
- “Fallback storms” detector panel (sustained elevated fallback rate)

**Owner team:** Platform / Routing

---

### (RI-PVT-221) Model Registry & Artifact Sync
**Tier:** Optional (enabled when registry integration is configured)

**Primary questions answered**
- Are model artifacts syncing/pulling successfully?
- Is model load failing due to missing blobs, auth, or storage pressure?

**Key panels**
- Sync job success rate / errors
- Artifact download duration and retry counts
- Storage utilization / free space (for artifact cache)

**Owner team:** Platform / Registry Integrations

---

### (RI-PVT-241) Installer & Upgrade Controller
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are installs/upgrades stuck, failing preflight, or retrying?
- Did a reconcile loop start thrashing after a config change?

**Key panels**
- Preflight checks: pass/fail counts by category (storage/network/resources)
- Upgrade duration histogram; success/failure rate
- Reconcile errors and retry backoff
- Helm release status signals (where available)

**Owner team:** Private Deployments

---

### (RI-PVT-301) Prometheus — Scrape & TSDB Health
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Are dashboards empty because Prometheus scrapes are failing?
- Is Prometheus underprovisioned (CPU/mem/disk) for retention settings?

**Key panels**
- Target health: up/down by job
- Scrape duration and sample ingestion rate
- TSDB size, WAL growth, compaction duration
- Remote write health (if enabled)

**Owner team:** Infra / Observability

---

### (RI-PVT-311) Grafana — Availability & Datasource
**Tier:** Core

**Primary questions answered**
- Is Grafana up and responding?
- Is the Prometheus datasource misconfigured/auth failing?

**Key panels**
- Grafana pod health and restarts
- HTTP 5xx rate from Grafana
- Datasource query error rate and latency (if instrumented)

**Owner team:** Infra / Observability

---

### (RI-PVT-401) Log Pipeline — Collector Health
**Tier:** Optional (enabled when log collection/forwarding is enabled)

**Primary questions answered**
- Are we dropping logs due to backpressure?
- Are exports failing (auth/network) to customer sinks?

**Key panels**
- Buffer fill %, retries, and dropped log counters
- Export throughput and failure rate by sink type
- CPU/memory usage of collector pods

**Security note**
- This dashboard should not surface sensitive log content. It focuses on pipeline health counters only.

**Owner team:** Telemetry Platform

---

## Naming conventions
- Dashboard IDs use the prefix `RI-PVT-` and a stable numeric range:
  - `001–099`: Overview
  - `101–199`: Serving runtime
  - `201–299`: Control plane
  - `301–399`: Observability infrastructure (Prometheus/Grafana)
  - `401–499`: Logging pipeline

## Support expectations (what is covered)
Redwood Support treats the above dashboards as part of the **supported minimum** for Private installs.

Support will:
- Validate that dashboards render with documented Helm/installer configuration
- Use these dashboards during incident triage
- File issues against owners when panels are broken due to metric/label changes

Support will not:
- Customize dashboards per customer beyond documented values and toggles
- Build customer-specific alerting strategies beyond v1 defaults (see SLO guidance)

## Change process
- Dashboard changes must be accompanied by:
  - A note in the release notes / upgrade guidance when panels or metric dependencies change
  - Backward-compatible metric transitions (or explicit migration instructions)
  - A quick validation in the private smoke-test harness (dashboards render + required queries return data)

## Appendix: Known gaps (tracked for future iterations)
- Tracing-first latency breakdowns (queue vs compute vs upstream) are limited in v1
- Limited per-tenant debugging by design (cardinality + privacy). Escalation path is to use customer-controlled logs/audit trails.
- Some GPU hardware error signals (Xid/thermal) may vary by driver/exporter version

title: Autoscaling Bounds Observability Dashboard Spec (Dedicated)
preview:
## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana dashboards required to operate **Dedicated autoscaling with customer-configurable min/max bounds** and optional **predictive scaling signals**.

The dashboards are designed to answer four recurring operational questions:
1) What did the autoscaler want to do vs what it actually did after applying bounds?
2) Are we saturating at the max bound (or failing to meet the min bound) and what is the customer impact?
3) Is predictive mode helping (earlier scale-up) or hurting (oscillation, overshoot, wrong forecasts)?
4) Can on-call and CS quickly drill down per customer/deployment/region to explain behavior with auditability?

## Audience
- SRE on-call (triage, incident response)
- Infra/Fleet (autoscaler behavior, capacity shortages)
- Platform/Control plane (policy evaluation, validation)
- Customer Success / TAM (customer-facing explanations and QBRs)

## Dashboard inventory
We will ship 3 dashboards (same folder/tagging) with shared variables for drilldown.

### 1) Dedicated Autoscaling: Overview (Bounds + Predictive)
Primary landing page. Summarizes bounded behavior, queueing, saturation, and forecast quality.

### 2) Dedicated Autoscaling: Scale Events & Decision Reasons
Timeline-focused view with scale events, bound hits, cool-downs, and decision reason codes.

### 3) Dedicated Autoscaling: Customer/Deployment Drilldown
Per deployment detail view intended for support escalations and post-incident analysis.

## Data sources
- Prometheus (metrics)
- Loki (structured logs for decision reasons, forecast staleness, capacity shortage state)
- Tempo (optional; trace exemplars for high queue depth / p95 latency; not required for GA)

## Global variables (Grafana templating)
All dashboards must support:
- $account_id (string; required)
- $deployment_id (string; optional; defaults to All)
- $region (string; optional; defaults to All)
- $model (string; optional; defaults to All)
- $capacity_tier (enum: dedicated)

Variable behavior:
- Prefer low-cardinality sources (control plane registry metric) over free-form label exploration.
- For shared views, default to filtering by $account_id to keep queries bounded.

## Metric conventions used in this spec
This spec uses Prometheus-style naming as implemented in the autoscaler/telemetry PRs.

Required labels:
- account_id (Dedicated account identifier; stable)
- deployment_id (Dedicated deployment identifier)
- region (e.g., us-east-1)

Optional labels (use carefully; see Cardinality section):
- model (pinned model identifier)
- reason (enumerated decision reason codes)
- bound (min|max)

Key metrics (expected to exist):
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_desired_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_current_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_min_bound_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_max_bound_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bound_hit_total{account_id,deployment_id,region,bound} (counter)
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total{account_id,deployment_id,region,direction,reason} (counter)
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_cooldown_active{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge 0/1)

Workload/saturation signals:
- redwood_dedicated_queue_depth{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_queue_wait_seconds_p95{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_request_rate_rps{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_gpu_utilization_ratio{account_id,deployment_id,region} (0..1)
- redwood_dedicated_kv_cache_hit_ratio{account_id,deployment_id,region} (0..1)
- redwood_dedicated_saturation_ratio{account_id,deployment_id,region} (0..1; composite autoscaler input)

Predictive mode signals:
- redwood_dedicated_predictive_mode_enabled{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge 0/1)
- redwood_dedicated_forecast_rps{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge)
- redwood_dedicated_forecast_age_seconds{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge)
- redwood_dedicated_forecast_error_ratio{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge; abs(actual-forecast)/max(actual,1))

Shortage / unmet min indicators:
- redwood_dedicated_min_bound_unmet{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge 0/1)
- redwood_dedicated_capacity_shortage_active{region,resource="gpu"} (gauge 0/1)
