## Dashboard 1: Dedicated Autoscaling: Overview (Bounds + Predictive)
### Layout (top to bottom)

#### Row A: Status summary
Panel A1 — "Predictive Mode Enabled" (stat)
- Visualization: Stat
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_predictive_mode_enabled{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"})
- Notes: If >1 deployment selected, stat shows max; include panel description clarifying.

Panel A2 — "Bound Hit Rate (last 30m)" (stat)
- Visualization: Stat (percentage)
- Query:
  - numerator: sum(increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bound_hit_total{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"}[30m]))
  - denominator: sum(increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"}[30m]))
  - expression: clamp_max(numerator / clamp_min(denominator, 1), 1)
- Thresholds (guide):
  - < 0.05: OK
  - 0.05–0.20: watch
  - > 0.20: investigate (frequent clamping or too-tight bounds)

Panel A3 — "Queue Wait p95" (stat)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_queue_wait_seconds_p95{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"})
- Thresholds (guide):
  - < 0.25s OK, 0.25–1s watch, >1s impact likely (align with SRE SLO doc)

#### Row B: Desired vs bounded vs current capacity
Panel B1 — "Replicas: desired vs bounded desired vs current" (time series)
- Queries (3 lines):
  1) desired: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_desired_replicas{...})
  2) bounded: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas{...})
  3) current: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_current_replicas{...})
- Overlay min/max (optional, dashed):
  - min: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_min_bound_replicas{...})
  - max: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_max_bound_replicas{...})
- Interpretation notes (in panel description):
  - If desired > max and bounded == max, we are max-clamped.
  - If current < bounded for sustained periods, suspect provisioning lag or regional shortage.

Panel B2 — "Bound clamp magnitude" (time series)
- Query: avg((redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_desired_replicas{...}) - (redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas{...}))
- Display: positive values indicate clamping (either max clamp on scale-up or min clamp on scale-down depending on sign; show both with "Transform: absolute" optional)

#### Row C: Saturation and queueing
Panel C1 — "Queue depth" (time series)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_queue_depth{...})
- Suggested annotation: scale events (from Dashboard 2 source) to correlate.

Panel C2 — "Saturation ratio and GPU utilization" (time series, dual axis)
- Queries:
  - saturation: max(redwood_dedicated_saturation_ratio{...})
  - gpu_util: max(redwood_dedicated_gpu_utilization_ratio{...})
- Thresholds (guide):
  - saturation > 0.85 sustained + queue growth indicates under-provisioning.

Panel C3 — "KV cache hit ratio" (time series)
- Query: avg(redwood_dedicated_kv_cache_hit_ratio{...})
- Note: a drop in cache hit ratio can increase effective demand without a request-rate change.

#### Row D: Predictive health and quality (only meaningful when predictive enabled)
Panel D1 — "Forecast vs actual request rate" (time series)
- Queries:
  - actual: sum(rate(redwood_dedicated_request_rate_rps{...}[5m]))
  - forecast: avg(redwood_dedicated_forecast_rps{...})

Panel D2 — "Forecast age (staleness)" (time series)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_forecast_age_seconds{...})
- Thresholds (guide):
  - < 120s OK
  - 120–300s watch (risk of stale forecast)
  - > 300s treat as effectively unavailable; verify fallback to reactive

Panel D3 — "Forecast error ratio" (time series)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_forecast_error_ratio{...})
- Suggested annotation: periods of oscillation (frequent scale events) to correlate.

## Dashboard 2: Dedicated Autoscaling: Scale Events & Decision Reasons
This dashboard is intended for engineers and on-call to explain *why* scaling changed.

### Panels

Panel E1 — "Scale events (up/down)" (time series, bar)
- Query: sum by (direction) (increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"}[5m]))
- Display: stacked bars by direction

Panel E2 — "Top decision reasons (last 1h)" (table)
- Query: topk(10, sum by (reason) (increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total{...}[1h])))
- Notes:
  - reason is an enum (e.g., "reactive_queue_depth", "reactive_saturation", "predictive_forecast", "cooldown_blocked", "max_bound_clamp", "min_bound_clamp", "shortage_unmet_min").

Panel E3 — "Bound hits over time (min vs max)" (time series)
- Query: sum by (bound) (increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bound_hit_total{...}[5m]))

Panel E4 — "Cooldown active" (time series)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_cooldown_active{...})
- Display: state timeline

Panel E5 — "Decision logs (Loki)" (logs)
- Data source: Loki
- Filter (example; adjust to real log labels):
  - {service="dedicated-autoscaler", account_id=~"$account_id", deployment_id=~"$deployment_id", region=~"$region"} |= "scaling_decision"
- Required log fields (documented in autoscaler implementation):
  - requested_desired_replicas
  - bounded_desired_replicas
  - current_replicas
  - min_bound_replicas / max_bound_replicas
  - reason
  - predictive_used (true/false)
  - forecast_age_seconds

## Dashboard 3: Dedicated Autoscaling: Customer/Deployment Drilldown
This dashboard is used by Support/TAM and incident commanders. It should be safe to share screenshots with customers (no internal-only IDs beyond the account/deployment they already know).

### Panels
Panel F1 — "Capacity and bounds (single deployment focus)" (time series)
- Same as Panel B1, but default time range 6h and requires $deployment_id selection (no All).

Panel F2 — "Customer impact signals" (time series)
- Queries:
  - queue_wait_p95: redwood_dedicated_queue_wait_seconds_p95{...}
  - error_rate (if available): redwood_dedicated_request_error_ratio{...}
  - p95_latency (if available): redwood_dedicated_request_latency_seconds_p95{...}

Panel F3 — "Max-bound saturation episodes" (state timeline)
- Expression: (redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas{...} >= redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_max_bound_replicas{...})
- Notes: This is an operator-friendly indicator to quickly validate "we hit your cap".

Panel F4 — "Min-bound unmet" (stat + logs)
- Stat query: max(redwood_dedicated_min_bound_unmet{...})
- Loki panel: shortage-related decision logs and provisioning failures.

Panel F5 — "Audit trail links" (text panel)
Include direct links (as plain URLs/Confluence links) to:
- Dedicated autoscaler troubleshooting runbook
- Capacity Policy Service (effective policy + audit events)
- Customer escalation playbook (CS)

## Annotations (shared across dashboards)
Enable the following annotations (Grafana annotations from Prometheus counters or Loki):
- Scale-up and scale-down events
- Bound-hit events
- Predictive disabled/fallback events (forecast stale/unavailable)
- Region shortage active (if available)

Recommended implementation:
- Use query-based annotations on increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total[1m]) > 0.

## Drilldown flows (required)
From any dashboard, provide quick drilldowns:
- Click a deployment_id -> open Dashboard 3 pre-filtered
- Click region -> open same dashboard filtered to region
- From max-bound saturation panel -> link to alerts (if firing) and the runbook section "Customer at max bound"

## Cardinality and performance requirements
We must keep dashboards usable for large enterprise accounts.

Rules:
- All panels must be valid with filters on account_id, deployment_id, region.
- Avoid grouping by request_path, user_id, prompt_id, or other high-cardinality fields.
- Use topk() with strict bounds for tables.
- Prefer avg/max over sum when comparing replicas (replicas are already per deployment).

Performance target:
- P95 panel query time < 2s for 6h range at 15s resolution when filtered to a single account_id.

## Access control and sharing
- Folder: "Dedicated / Autoscaling"
- Tags: dedicated, autoscaling, bounds, predictive
- Access: Engineering + SRE + Customer Success (read-only for CS)
- Customer sharing: screenshots allowed; direct Grafana access is not customer-facing.

## Validation checklist (before GA)
- Dashboard loads without errors for:
  - predictive disabled (reactive only)
  - predictive enabled with valid forecasts
  - forecast stale/unavailable (fallback path)
  - account at max bound (bound hits)
  - region shortage active (min unmet)
- Reason codes are consistent between metrics and logs.
- Panels do not require hidden knowledge (include descriptions with interpretation).
- Links to runbooks and SLO/alerts pages are correct.

## Ownership and change management
- Primary owner: Observability (Allison Grant, Sergio Costa)
- Domain reviewers: Fleet/Infra (Vanessa Ortiz), SRE (Rafael Mendes), Control Plane (Ethan Park)
- Any new label dimensions or metric renames must be reviewed against observability naming/label standards.

## Appendix A: Reason code glossary (initial)
- predictive_forecast: scale target influenced by forecast signal
- reactive_queue_depth: queue depth exceeded threshold
- reactive_saturation: saturation ratio exceeded threshold
- cooldown_blocked: scaling change blocked due to cooldown window
- max_bound_clamp: requested desired > max bound (clamped to max)
- min_bound_clamp: requested desired < min bound (clamped to min)
- shortage_unmet_min: unable to provision to min bound due to regional capacity shortage
- fallback_forecast_stale: forecast too old; predictive ignored
- fallback_forecast_missing: forecast unavailable; predictive ignored
text:
## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana dashboards required to operate **Dedicated autoscaling with customer-configurable min/max bounds** and optional **predictive scaling signals**.

The dashboards are designed to answer four recurring operational questions:
1) What did the autoscaler want to do vs what it actually did after applying bounds?
2) Are we saturating at the max bound (or failing to meet the min bound) and what is the customer impact?
3) Is predictive mode helping (earlier scale-up) or hurting (oscillation, overshoot, wrong forecasts)?
4) Can on-call and CS quickly drill down per customer/deployment/region to explain behavior with auditability?

## Audience
- SRE on-call (triage, incident response)
- Infra/Fleet (autoscaler behavior, capacity shortages)
- Platform/Control plane (policy evaluation, validation)
- Customer Success / TAM (customer-facing explanations and QBRs)

## Dashboard inventory
We will ship 3 dashboards (same folder/tagging) with shared variables for drilldown.

### 1) Dedicated Autoscaling: Overview (Bounds + Predictive)
Primary landing page. Summarizes bounded behavior, queueing, saturation, and forecast quality.

### 2) Dedicated Autoscaling: Scale Events & Decision Reasons
Timeline-focused view with scale events, bound hits, cool-downs, and decision reason codes.

### 3) Dedicated Autoscaling: Customer/Deployment Drilldown
Per deployment detail view intended for support escalations and post-incident analysis.

## Data sources
- Prometheus (metrics)
- Loki (structured logs for decision reasons, forecast staleness, capacity shortage state)
- Tempo (optional; trace exemplars for high queue depth / p95 latency; not required for GA)

## Global variables (Grafana templating)
All dashboards must support:
- $account_id (string; required)
- $deployment_id (string; optional; defaults to All)
- $region (string; optional; defaults to All)
- $model (string; optional; defaults to All)
- $capacity_tier (enum: dedicated)

Variable behavior:
- Prefer low-cardinality sources (control plane registry metric) over free-form label exploration.
- For shared views, default to filtering by $account_id to keep queries bounded.

## Metric conventions used in this spec
This spec uses Prometheus-style naming as implemented in the autoscaler/telemetry PRs.

Required labels:
- account_id (Dedicated account identifier; stable)
- deployment_id (Dedicated deployment identifier)
- region (e.g., us-east-1)

Optional labels (use carefully; see Cardinality section):
- model (pinned model identifier)
- reason (enumerated decision reason codes)
- bound (min|max)

Key metrics (expected to exist):
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_desired_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_current_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_min_bound_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_max_bound_replicas{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bound_hit_total{account_id,deployment_id,region,bound} (counter)
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total{account_id,deployment_id,region,direction,reason} (counter)
- redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_cooldown_active{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge 0/1)

Workload/saturation signals:
- redwood_dedicated_queue_depth{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_queue_wait_seconds_p95{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_request_rate_rps{account_id,deployment_id,region}
- redwood_dedicated_gpu_utilization_ratio{account_id,deployment_id,region} (0..1)
- redwood_dedicated_kv_cache_hit_ratio{account_id,deployment_id,region} (0..1)
- redwood_dedicated_saturation_ratio{account_id,deployment_id,region} (0..1; composite autoscaler input)

Predictive mode signals:
- redwood_dedicated_predictive_mode_enabled{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge 0/1)
- redwood_dedicated_forecast_rps{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge)
- redwood_dedicated_forecast_age_seconds{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge)
- redwood_dedicated_forecast_error_ratio{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge; abs(actual-forecast)/max(actual,1))

Shortage / unmet min indicators:
- redwood_dedicated_min_bound_unmet{account_id,deployment_id,region} (gauge 0/1)
- redwood_dedicated_capacity_shortage_active{region,resource="gpu"} (gauge 0/1)

## Dashboard 1: Dedicated Autoscaling: Overview (Bounds + Predictive)
### Layout (top to bottom)

#### Row A: Status summary
Panel A1 — "Predictive Mode Enabled" (stat)
- Visualization: Stat
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_predictive_mode_enabled{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"})
- Notes: If >1 deployment selected, stat shows max; include panel description clarifying.

Panel A2 — "Bound Hit Rate (last 30m)" (stat)
- Visualization: Stat (percentage)
- Query:
  - numerator: sum(increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bound_hit_total{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"}[30m]))
  - denominator: sum(increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"}[30m]))
  - expression: clamp_max(numerator / clamp_min(denominator, 1), 1)
- Thresholds (guide):
  - < 0.05: OK
  - 0.05–0.20: watch
  - > 0.20: investigate (frequent clamping or too-tight bounds)

Panel A3 — "Queue Wait p95" (stat)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_queue_wait_seconds_p95{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"})
- Thresholds (guide):
  - < 0.25s OK, 0.25–1s watch, >1s impact likely (align with SRE SLO doc)

#### Row B: Desired vs bounded vs current capacity
Panel B1 — "Replicas: desired vs bounded desired vs current" (time series)
- Queries (3 lines):
  1) desired: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_desired_replicas{...})
  2) bounded: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas{...})
  3) current: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_current_replicas{...})
- Overlay min/max (optional, dashed):
  - min: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_min_bound_replicas{...})
  - max: avg(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_max_bound_replicas{...})
- Interpretation notes (in panel description):
  - If desired > max and bounded == max, we are max-clamped.
  - If current < bounded for sustained periods, suspect provisioning lag or regional shortage.

Panel B2 — "Bound clamp magnitude" (time series)
- Query: avg((redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_desired_replicas{...}) - (redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas{...}))
- Display: positive values indicate clamping (either max clamp on scale-up or min clamp on scale-down depending on sign; show both with "Transform: absolute" optional)

#### Row C: Saturation and queueing
Panel C1 — "Queue depth" (time series)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_queue_depth{...})
- Suggested annotation: scale events (from Dashboard 2 source) to correlate.

Panel C2 — "Saturation ratio and GPU utilization" (time series, dual axis)
- Queries:
  - saturation: max(redwood_dedicated_saturation_ratio{...})
  - gpu_util: max(redwood_dedicated_gpu_utilization_ratio{...})
- Thresholds (guide):
  - saturation > 0.85 sustained + queue growth indicates under-provisioning.

Panel C3 — "KV cache hit ratio" (time series)
- Query: avg(redwood_dedicated_kv_cache_hit_ratio{...})
- Note: a drop in cache hit ratio can increase effective demand without a request-rate change.

#### Row D: Predictive health and quality (only meaningful when predictive enabled)
Panel D1 — "Forecast vs actual request rate" (time series)
- Queries:
  - actual: sum(rate(redwood_dedicated_request_rate_rps{...}[5m]))
  - forecast: avg(redwood_dedicated_forecast_rps{...})

Panel D2 — "Forecast age (staleness)" (time series)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_forecast_age_seconds{...})
- Thresholds (guide):
  - < 120s OK
  - 120–300s watch (risk of stale forecast)
  - > 300s treat as effectively unavailable; verify fallback to reactive

Panel D3 — "Forecast error ratio" (time series)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_forecast_error_ratio{...})
- Suggested annotation: periods of oscillation (frequent scale events) to correlate.

## Dashboard 2: Dedicated Autoscaling: Scale Events & Decision Reasons
This dashboard is intended for engineers and on-call to explain *why* scaling changed.

### Panels

Panel E1 — "Scale events (up/down)" (time series, bar)
- Query: sum by (direction) (increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total{account_id=~"$account_id",deployment_id=~"$deployment_id",region=~"$region"}[5m]))
- Display: stacked bars by direction

Panel E2 — "Top decision reasons (last 1h)" (table)
- Query: topk(10, sum by (reason) (increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total{...}[1h])))
- Notes:
  - reason is an enum (e.g., "reactive_queue_depth", "reactive_saturation", "predictive_forecast", "cooldown_blocked", "max_bound_clamp", "min_bound_clamp", "shortage_unmet_min").

Panel E3 — "Bound hits over time (min vs max)" (time series)
- Query: sum by (bound) (increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bound_hit_total{...}[5m]))

Panel E4 — "Cooldown active" (time series)
- Query: max(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_cooldown_active{...})
- Display: state timeline

Panel E5 — "Decision logs (Loki)" (logs)
- Data source: Loki
- Filter (example; adjust to real log labels):
  - {service="dedicated-autoscaler", account_id=~"$account_id", deployment_id=~"$deployment_id", region=~"$region"} |= "scaling_decision"
- Required log fields (documented in autoscaler implementation):
  - requested_desired_replicas
  - bounded_desired_replicas
  - current_replicas
  - min_bound_replicas / max_bound_replicas
  - reason
  - predictive_used (true/false)
  - forecast_age_seconds

## Dashboard 3: Dedicated Autoscaling: Customer/Deployment Drilldown
This dashboard is used by Support/TAM and incident commanders. It should be safe to share screenshots with customers (no internal-only IDs beyond the account/deployment they already know).

### Panels
Panel F1 — "Capacity and bounds (single deployment focus)" (time series)
- Same as Panel B1, but default time range 6h and requires $deployment_id selection (no All).

Panel F2 — "Customer impact signals" (time series)
- Queries:
  - queue_wait_p95: redwood_dedicated_queue_wait_seconds_p95{...}
  - error_rate (if available): redwood_dedicated_request_error_ratio{...}
  - p95_latency (if available): redwood_dedicated_request_latency_seconds_p95{...}

Panel F3 — "Max-bound saturation episodes" (state timeline)
- Expression: (redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas{...} >= redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_max_bound_replicas{...})
- Notes: This is an operator-friendly indicator to quickly validate "we hit your cap".

Panel F4 — "Min-bound unmet" (stat + logs)
- Stat query: max(redwood_dedicated_min_bound_unmet{...})
- Loki panel: shortage-related decision logs and provisioning failures.

Panel F5 — "Audit trail links" (text panel)
Include direct links (as plain URLs/Confluence links) to:
- Dedicated autoscaler troubleshooting runbook
- Capacity Policy Service (effective policy + audit events)
- Customer escalation playbook (CS)

## Annotations (shared across dashboards)
Enable the following annotations (Grafana annotations from Prometheus counters or Loki):
- Scale-up and scale-down events
- Bound-hit events
- Predictive disabled/fallback events (forecast stale/unavailable)
- Region shortage active (if available)

Recommended implementation:
- Use query-based annotations on increase(redwood_dedicated_autoscaler_scale_events_total[1m]) > 0.

## Drilldown flows (required)
From any dashboard, provide quick drilldowns:
- Click a deployment_id -> open Dashboard 3 pre-filtered
- Click region -> open same dashboard filtered to region
- From max-bound saturation panel -> link to alerts (if firing) and the runbook section "Customer at max bound"

## Cardinality and performance requirements
We must keep dashboards usable for large enterprise accounts.

Rules:
- All panels must be valid with filters on account_id, deployment_id, region.
- Avoid grouping by request_path, user_id, prompt_id, or other high-cardinality fields.
- Use topk() with strict bounds for tables.
- Prefer avg/max over sum when comparing replicas (replicas are already per deployment).

Performance target:
- P95 panel query time < 2s for 6h range at 15s resolution when filtered to a single account_id.

## Access control and sharing
- Folder: "Dedicated / Autoscaling"
- Tags: dedicated, autoscaling, bounds, predictive
- Access: Engineering + SRE + Customer Success (read-only for CS)
- Customer sharing: screenshots allowed; direct Grafana access is not customer-facing.

## Validation checklist (before GA)
- Dashboard loads without errors for:
  - predictive disabled (reactive only)
  - predictive enabled with valid forecasts
  - forecast stale/unavailable (fallback path)
  - account at max bound (bound hits)
  - region shortage active (min unmet)
- Reason codes are consistent between metrics and logs.
- Panels do not require hidden knowledge (include descriptions with interpretation).
- Links to runbooks and SLO/alerts pages are correct.

## Ownership and change management
- Primary owner: Observability (Allison Grant, Sergio Costa)
- Domain reviewers: Fleet/Infra (Vanessa Ortiz), SRE (Rafael Mendes), Control Plane (Ethan Park)
- Any new label dimensions or metric renames must be reviewed against observability naming/label standards.

## Appendix A: Reason code glossary (initial)
- predictive_forecast: scale target influenced by forecast signal
- reactive_queue_depth: queue depth exceeded threshold
- reactive_saturation: saturation ratio exceeded threshold
- cooldown_blocked: scaling change blocked due to cooldown window
- max_bound_clamp: requested desired > max bound (clamped to max)
- min_bound_clamp: requested desired < min bound (clamped to min)
- shortage_unmet_min: unable to provision to min bound due to regional capacity shortage
- fallback_forecast_stale: forecast too old; predictive ignored
- fallback_forecast_missing: forecast unavailable; predictive ignored

title: Burst Admission Controller (BAC) Service
preview:
## Summary
The Burst Admission Controller (BAC) is an internal, regional control-plane service that makes deterministic, low-latency admission decisions for Dedicated customers attempting to exceed their reserved baseline capacity (“bursting”) by borrowing from Redwood-managed burst pools.

BAC’s core responsibilities are:
- Enforce guardrails (tenant caps, max burst ratios, cooldown windows, per-pool saturation limits, global fleet safety caps).
- Apply fairness across tenants sharing a burst pool (weighted token-bucket / WFQ hybrid; see ADR for selection).
- Produce explainable denial reasons for customers and Support.
- Emit metering signals for billing and operational telemetry for SRE.

Non-goals:
- Scheduling/queuing long-running work (BAC returns immediate decisions; callers may implement client-side or gateway queuing/backoff).
- Cross-region bursting (out of scope for initial release).

## Service owners
- Primary owner team: Platform Control Plane
- Eng owner: Ethan Park
- Secondary owners: Benji Okafor (implementation), Jordan Lee (policy engine integration)
- SRE partner: Sean Gallagher
- Infra partner (pool capacity): Vanessa Ortiz

Escalation path:
1) #eng-platform on Slack (business hours)
2) #eng-sre (if customer impact, elevated latency, or widespread denials)
3) Incident Commander per on-call rotation

## Where BAC sits in the system
BAC is invoked on the request path used by Dedicated data plane routing when a tenant may exceed baseline capacity.

High-level flow (simplified):
1) Router/gateway computes whether a request would exceed the tenant’s baseline (based on current baseline utilization signals).
2) If baseline is sufficient, route normally (no BAC call).
3) If burst may be required, Router calls BAC Admit API with tenant + pool context and a request cost estimate.
4) BAC returns ADMIT or DENY with reason + retry guidance.
5) Router applies policy:
   - ADMIT: route to burst-capable capacity (shared burst pool segment).
   - DENY: apply backpressure (429/overload), or fall back to non-burst behavior per tenant policy (e.g., queue, degrade model variant, or reject).

Determinism requirement:
- BAC must be deterministic given the same (policy snapshot + telemetry snapshot + request fields). Router retries must not oscillate due to minor telemetry drift.

## Deployment model
- Deployed per region (e.g., us-east, us-west, eu-west).
- Stateless compute pods (Kubernetes), horizontally scalable.
- Uses regional Redis for short-lived state (token buckets / cooldown tracking) and regional telemetry feeds.
- Supports regional rollout gates by region + hardware class allow-list.

## Public (internal) APIs
BAC is internal-only. Primary callers: Smart Routing / gateway. Secondary callers: Console backend (read-only explain endpoints via support tooling), SRE tooling.

### 1) Admit API
HTTP: POST /v1/burst/admit
Auth: mTLS + service identity (router/gateway). No end-user auth.
Timeout budget: 50ms (callers should use 75ms overall including retries).

Request fields (JSON):
- request_id: string (required; idempotency and tracing)
- tenant_id: string (required)
- dedicated_deployment_id: string (required; maps to a Dedicated capacity slice)
- region: string (required)
- hardware_class: string (required; e.g., "h100", "l40s")
- pool_id: string (required; derived from region+hardware_class; validated server-side)
- priority_tier: string (optional; "standard" | "silver" | "gold"; default from tenant policy)
- estimated_cost_units: number (required; normalized cost estimate for fairness accounting; see “Cost Units”)
- baseline_in_use_units: number (optional; for debugging only; BAC does not trust this for policy enforcement)
- telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms: number (optional; caller-provided snapshot timestamp for determinism hints)

Response fields (JSON):
- decision: string ("ADMIT" | "DENY")
- reason_code: string ("OK" if ADMIT; otherwise one of denial codes)
- retry_after_ms: number (0 if not applicable)
- decision_ttl_ms: number (if ADMIT, how long the decision/lease is considered valid for metering correlation)
- policy_hash: string (hash of policy snapshot used)
- telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms: number (telemetry timestamp used by BAC)
- debug_ref: string (opaque; safe to share with Support to locate decision details)

Example (ADMIT):
{
  "decision": "ADMIT",
  "reason_code": "OK",
  "retry_after_ms": 0,
  "decision_ttl_ms": 2000,
  "policy_hash": "pol_8b1c6f7",
  "telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms": 1749550134123,
  "debug_ref": "bac_01J0V7B1TQ3Y9H1E"
}

Example (DENY):
{
  "decision": "DENY",
  "reason_code": "DENIED_POOL_SATURATED",
  "retry_after_ms": 250,
  "decision_ttl_ms": 0,
  "policy_hash": "pol_8b1c6f7",
  "telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms": 1749550134123,
  "debug_ref": "bac_01J0V7B2K8ZP5M9A"
}

### 2) Explain (Support Debug) API
HTTP: POST /v1/burst/explain
Auth: restricted (RBAC: support_debug:read), audited.
Purpose: explain why a specific request was admitted/denied.

Request:
- debug_ref: string (required)

Response (redacted, safe):
- decision, reason_code
- evaluated_guardrails: list as text (e.g., “tenant_cap: hit”, “global_cap: ok”)
- pool_saturation_pct: number (rounded)
- tenant_bucket_remaining_units: number (rounded)
- cooldown_remaining_ms: number
- policy_hash, telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms

Note: This endpoint must never return other tenants’ identifiers, raw per-tenant usage, or customer-sensitive configs.

### 3) Health/metrics
- GET /healthz (liveness)
- GET /readyz (readiness; fails if policy cache is empty beyond threshold)
- GET /metrics (Prometheus)

## Denial reason codes
BAC denial codes are stable, documented, and surfaced to callers for customer-visible reporting.

Primary codes:
- DENIED_BURST_DISABLED: tenant not enabled for burst.
- DENIED_REGION_OR_HW_NOT_ALLOWED: rollout gate blocked (region/hardware class).
- DENIED_TENANT_CAP: tenant exceeded per-tenant burst cap (hard limit).
- DENIED_MAX_BURST_RATIO: tenant exceeded max burst ratio relative to baseline.
- DENIED_COOLDOWN_ACTIVE: tenant in cooldown window after sustained bursting or cap hit.
- DENIED_POOL_SATURATED: pool has insufficient headroom (local saturation guardrail).
- DENIED_FLEET_SAFETY_CAP: global cap triggered (platform-wide protection).
- DENIED_PRIORITY_FAIRNESS: fairness algorithm rejected to prevent starvation/neighbor impact.

Operational/edge codes:
- ERROR_POLICY_UNAVAILABLE: policy engine/config service unavailable beyond allowed stale window.
- ERROR_TELEMETRY_STALE: telemetry too stale to make a safe decision.
- ERROR_INTERNAL: unexpected failure (should be rare; counts toward BAC error budget).

Callers should treat ERROR_* as retriable only if retry_after_ms > 0 and overall request budget allows; otherwise degrade gracefully.

## Core concepts
### Burst pools
A burst pool is a shared capacity pool segmented by:
- region
- hardware_class
Optionally segmented by compliance constraints (where applicable) and internal fleet partitioning.

BAC does not provision capacity; it consumes pool state from Fleet Telemetry and enforces the pool’s admission limits.

### Cost Units
Fairness and caps are enforced using “cost units” rather than raw request counts.
- Default definition: cost units ≈ estimated GPU-milliseconds for the request’s expected work.
- Router provides estimated_cost_units; BAC applies guardrails and token bucket accounting in these units.

We intentionally keep units abstract/stable for API evolution; the metering pipeline translates to billable burst GPU-seconds using authoritative runtime signals.

### Determinism & telemetry smoothing
To minimize oscillation and prevent unfair denials under telemetry lag:
- BAC uses a bounded-staleness telemetry snapshot per pool.
- If telemetry is newer than the max staleness threshold, BAC evaluates against that snapshot.
- If telemetry is stale but within the “soft stale” window, BAC applies conservative smoothing (reduces computed headroom) and may admit only higher-priority tiers.
- If telemetry exceeds hard stale threshold, BAC returns ERROR_TELEMETRY_STALE with retry guidance.

See: “Failure modes” and the telemetry lag mitigation hotfix notes in the fairness ADR.

## Dependencies
### Policy/config
- Control Plane Tenant Config Service (source of truth)
  - Reads tenant burst enablement and limits
  - Versioned config with validation
- Policy Engine
  - Evaluates guardrail rules (max burst ratio, cooldown policy, fleet safety rules)
  - Provides deterministic evaluation (policy_hash returned in responses)

### Fleet telemetry
- FleetPulse (fleet telemetry aggregator; internal)
  - Inputs: pool capacity, pool utilization, saturation, admission queue depth (if any), health signals
  - Outputs: per-pool snapshot with timestamps

### State store
- Redis (regional)
  - Per-tenant token bucket state (remaining units, refill time)
  - Cooldown state
  - Small, bounded cardinality by (tenant_id, pool_id)

### Observability
- Prometheus metrics via /metrics
- OpenTelemetry tracing (propagates request_id)
- Structured logs (redacted) to centralized log pipeline

### Billing/metering
- Metering Exporter (async)
  - Emits admission decisions with debug_ref, tenant_id, pool_id, decision_ttl
  - Downstream joins with runtime burst usage signals to prevent double-counting on retries

### Security/audit
- Audit logging service for:
  - Access to /v1/burst/explain
  - Admin overrides (if enabled in emergency mode)
- RBAC service for privileged endpoints

## Data model (logical)
BAC interacts with the following logical entities. (Storage varies by entity.)

1) TenantBurstPolicy (authoritative; Control Plane DB)
- tenant_id
- enabled: true/false
- max_burst_ratio: e.g., 1.5x baseline
- burst_cap_units_per_min: per-tenant cap in cost units
- cooldown_seconds: e.g., 300
- priority_tier: standard/silver/gold
- denial_mode: immediate_deny | allow_queue (future)
- allowlist_regions_hw: optional overrides

2) PoolStateSnapshot (authoritative; FleetPulse)
- pool_id
- total_capacity_units
- reserved_safety_margin_units
- current_in_use_units
- computed_headroom_units
- saturation_pct
- snapshot_ts_ms

3) TenantBucketState (ephemeral; Redis)
- key: (tenant_id, pool_id)
- remaining_units
- last_refill_ts_ms
- cooldown_until_ts_ms

4) DecisionRecord (ephemeral; logs/metrics; optional short-lived cache)
- debug_ref
- request_id
- decision + reason_code
- policy_hash
- telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms
- estimated_cost_units

Retention:
- Logs: per platform standard
- Explain lookups: best-effort (typically 24–72 hours, depending on log retention and debug index)

## Guardrail evaluation order (current)
BAC evaluates in a strict order to keep outcomes explainable and predictable:
1) Rollout gates (region/hardware class allow-list)
2) Tenant burst enabled check
3) Telemetry validity (hard stale cutoff)
4) Global fleet safety cap
5) Pool headroom check (local saturation)
6) Per-tenant hard caps (max ratio and cap units)
7) Cooldown enforcement
8) Fairness/anti-starvation enforcement (tier weights + smoothing)

Important: Even for high priority tiers, global fleet safety and hard stale telemetry gates are not bypassable.

## Performance and scaling targets
BAC is on the request path only when baseline is exceeded; it must be fast.

Targets:
- p50 latency: < 5ms
- p99 latency: < 20ms
- Availability: 99.95% (regional)
- Error rate (5xx + ERROR_INTERNAL): < 0.1%

Operational notes:
- BAC should degrade safely: when in doubt, deny with an explainable reason rather than admitting and risking fleet impact.
- BAC uses aggressive timeouts to policy/telemetry dependencies; beyond the allowed stale window it should return ERROR_POLICY_UNAVAILABLE or ERROR_TELEMETRY_STALE.

## Failure modes and expected behavior
1) Policy engine unavailable
- BAC uses last known-good policy snapshot for up to the configured stale window.
- After stale window: deny with ERROR_POLICY_UNAVAILABLE.

2) Fleet telemetry lag / stale snapshots
- Within soft stale window: conservative headroom, may deny more aggressively.
- Beyond hard cutoff: ERROR_TELEMETRY_STALE.

3) Redis unavailable
- BAC cannot enforce fairness/cooldowns reliably.
- Behavior: fail closed for burst admission (DENY with ERROR_INTERNAL), emit high-severity alert.

4) Pool overload (saturation)
- DENIED_POOL_SATURATED with retry_after_ms based on pool refill estimate.
- If sustained: global circuit breaker may engage (DENIED_FLEET_SAFETY_CAP).

5) Retry storms
- Callers must use idempotency (request_id) and follow retry_after_ms.
- Metering pipeline uses idempotency keys to prevent double-counting.

## Feature flags and emergency controls
BAC supports the following kill switches (controlled via Control Plane config, audited):
- burst_global_disable: disables all burst admissions (still returns reasons).
- burst_disable_by_region_hw: disables specific pools.
- burst_disable_by_tenant: tenant-level disable.
- fleet_safety_cap_override: emergency-only; requires SRE + executive approval; all uses audited.

## Metrics (selected)
BAC emits Prometheus metrics with controlled cardinality (tenant_id is NOT a label).

Counters:
- bac_admit_total{pool_id, priority_tier}
- bac_deny_total{pool_id, reason_code, priority_tier}
- bac_error_total{pool_id, error_type}

Gauges:
- bac_pool_headroom_units{pool_id}
- bac_pool_saturation_pct{pool_id}
- bac_telemetry_staleness_ms{pool_id}

Histograms:
- bac_request_latency_ms{endpoint}

Dashboards: see “Burst Capacity Dashboards” page.

## Operational runbooks
- Overload mitigation: adjust caps, engage circuit breaker, communicate with CS/Support
  - See: Burst Pool Overload Mitigation Runbook
- Denials higher than expected: validate tenant config, telemetry staleness, pool headroom
- Explain endpoint usage: how to interpret reason codes and guardrail evaluation

## Change management
- Any change to denial codes or guardrail semantics requires:
  - Review by Smart Routing (Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon)
  - Review by SRE (Sean Gallagher)
  - Update to customer-facing docs and Support playbook
- Any change affecting billing/metering correlation requires Finance/Billing sign-off.

## Appendix: Integration guidance for Router/Gateway
Recommended caller behavior:
- Only call BAC when baseline is exceeded or predicted to exceed within the request window.
- Use a stable request_id for retries.
- Respect retry_after_ms; jitter retries.
- Surface reason_code to customer-visible reporting where safe.
- For DENIED_POOL_SATURATED / DENIED_FLEET_SAFETY_CAP:
  - Prefer backpressure (429) and/or queue with bounded waiting.
  - Avoid uncontrolled retries.

For customer comms:
- “Burst is best-effort above baseline and subject to shared-pool availability and fleet safety; baseline SLAs remain protected.”
text:
## Summary
The Burst Admission Controller (BAC) is an internal, regional control-plane service that makes deterministic, low-latency admission decisions for Dedicated customers attempting to exceed their reserved baseline capacity (“bursting”) by borrowing from Redwood-managed burst pools.

BAC’s core responsibilities are:
- Enforce guardrails (tenant caps, max burst ratios, cooldown windows, per-pool saturation limits, global fleet safety caps).
- Apply fairness across tenants sharing a burst pool (weighted token-bucket / WFQ hybrid; see ADR for selection).
- Produce explainable denial reasons for customers and Support.
- Emit metering signals for billing and operational telemetry for SRE.

Non-goals:
- Scheduling/queuing long-running work (BAC returns immediate decisions; callers may implement client-side or gateway queuing/backoff).
- Cross-region bursting (out of scope for initial release).

## Service owners
- Primary owner team: Platform Control Plane
- Eng owner: Ethan Park
- Secondary owners: Benji Okafor (implementation), Jordan Lee (policy engine integration)
- SRE partner: Sean Gallagher
- Infra partner (pool capacity): Vanessa Ortiz

Escalation path:
1) #eng-platform on Slack (business hours)
2) #eng-sre (if customer impact, elevated latency, or widespread denials)
3) Incident Commander per on-call rotation

## Where BAC sits in the system
BAC is invoked on the request path used by Dedicated data plane routing when a tenant may exceed baseline capacity.

High-level flow (simplified):
1) Router/gateway computes whether a request would exceed the tenant’s baseline (based on current baseline utilization signals).
2) If baseline is sufficient, route normally (no BAC call).
3) If burst may be required, Router calls BAC Admit API with tenant + pool context and a request cost estimate.
4) BAC returns ADMIT or DENY with reason + retry guidance.
5) Router applies policy:
   - ADMIT: route to burst-capable capacity (shared burst pool segment).
   - DENY: apply backpressure (429/overload), or fall back to non-burst behavior per tenant policy (e.g., queue, degrade model variant, or reject).

Determinism requirement:
- BAC must be deterministic given the same (policy snapshot + telemetry snapshot + request fields). Router retries must not oscillate due to minor telemetry drift.

## Deployment model
- Deployed per region (e.g., us-east, us-west, eu-west).
- Stateless compute pods (Kubernetes), horizontally scalable.
- Uses regional Redis for short-lived state (token buckets / cooldown tracking) and regional telemetry feeds.
- Supports regional rollout gates by region + hardware class allow-list.

## Public (internal) APIs
BAC is internal-only. Primary callers: Smart Routing / gateway. Secondary callers: Console backend (read-only explain endpoints via support tooling), SRE tooling.

### 1) Admit API
HTTP: POST /v1/burst/admit
Auth: mTLS + service identity (router/gateway). No end-user auth.
Timeout budget: 50ms (callers should use 75ms overall including retries).

Request fields (JSON):
- request_id: string (required; idempotency and tracing)
- tenant_id: string (required)
- dedicated_deployment_id: string (required; maps to a Dedicated capacity slice)
- region: string (required)
- hardware_class: string (required; e.g., "h100", "l40s")
- pool_id: string (required; derived from region+hardware_class; validated server-side)
- priority_tier: string (optional; "standard" | "silver" | "gold"; default from tenant policy)
- estimated_cost_units: number (required; normalized cost estimate for fairness accounting; see “Cost Units”)
- baseline_in_use_units: number (optional; for debugging only; BAC does not trust this for policy enforcement)
- telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms: number (optional; caller-provided snapshot timestamp for determinism hints)

Response fields (JSON):
- decision: string ("ADMIT" | "DENY")
- reason_code: string ("OK" if ADMIT; otherwise one of denial codes)
- retry_after_ms: number (0 if not applicable)
- decision_ttl_ms: number (if ADMIT, how long the decision/lease is considered valid for metering correlation)
- policy_hash: string (hash of policy snapshot used)
- telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms: number (telemetry timestamp used by BAC)
- debug_ref: string (opaque; safe to share with Support to locate decision details)

Example (ADMIT):
{
  "decision": "ADMIT",
  "reason_code": "OK",
  "retry_after_ms": 0,
  "decision_ttl_ms": 2000,
  "policy_hash": "pol_8b1c6f7",
  "telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms": 1749550134123,
  "debug_ref": "bac_01J0V7B1TQ3Y9H1E"
}

Example (DENY):
{
  "decision": "DENY",
  "reason_code": "DENIED_POOL_SATURATED",
  "retry_after_ms": 250,
  "decision_ttl_ms": 0,
  "policy_hash": "pol_8b1c6f7",
  "telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms": 1749550134123,
  "debug_ref": "bac_01J0V7B2K8ZP5M9A"
}

### 2) Explain (Support Debug) API
HTTP: POST /v1/burst/explain
Auth: restricted (RBAC: support_debug:read), audited.
Purpose: explain why a specific request was admitted/denied.

Request:
- debug_ref: string (required)

Response (redacted, safe):
- decision, reason_code
- evaluated_guardrails: list as text (e.g., “tenant_cap: hit”, “global_cap: ok”)
- pool_saturation_pct: number (rounded)
- tenant_bucket_remaining_units: number (rounded)
- cooldown_remaining_ms: number
- policy_hash, telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms

Note: This endpoint must never return other tenants’ identifiers, raw per-tenant usage, or customer-sensitive configs.

### 3) Health/metrics
- GET /healthz (liveness)
- GET /readyz (readiness; fails if policy cache is empty beyond threshold)
- GET /metrics (Prometheus)

## Denial reason codes
BAC denial codes are stable, documented, and surfaced to callers for customer-visible reporting.

Primary codes:
- DENIED_BURST_DISABLED: tenant not enabled for burst.
- DENIED_REGION_OR_HW_NOT_ALLOWED: rollout gate blocked (region/hardware class).
- DENIED_TENANT_CAP: tenant exceeded per-tenant burst cap (hard limit).
- DENIED_MAX_BURST_RATIO: tenant exceeded max burst ratio relative to baseline.
- DENIED_COOLDOWN_ACTIVE: tenant in cooldown window after sustained bursting or cap hit.
- DENIED_POOL_SATURATED: pool has insufficient headroom (local saturation guardrail).
- DENIED_FLEET_SAFETY_CAP: global cap triggered (platform-wide protection).
- DENIED_PRIORITY_FAIRNESS: fairness algorithm rejected to prevent starvation/neighbor impact.

Operational/edge codes:
- ERROR_POLICY_UNAVAILABLE: policy engine/config service unavailable beyond allowed stale window.
- ERROR_TELEMETRY_STALE: telemetry too stale to make a safe decision.
- ERROR_INTERNAL: unexpected failure (should be rare; counts toward BAC error budget).

Callers should treat ERROR_* as retriable only if retry_after_ms > 0 and overall request budget allows; otherwise degrade gracefully.

## Core concepts
### Burst pools
A burst pool is a shared capacity pool segmented by:
- region
- hardware_class
Optionally segmented by compliance constraints (where applicable) and internal fleet partitioning.

BAC does not provision capacity; it consumes pool state from Fleet Telemetry and enforces the pool’s admission limits.

### Cost Units
Fairness and caps are enforced using “cost units” rather than raw request counts.
- Default definition: cost units ≈ estimated GPU-milliseconds for the request’s expected work.
- Router provides estimated_cost_units; BAC applies guardrails and token bucket accounting in these units.

We intentionally keep units abstract/stable for API evolution; the metering pipeline translates to billable burst GPU-seconds using authoritative runtime signals.

### Determinism & telemetry smoothing
To minimize oscillation and prevent unfair denials under telemetry lag:
- BAC uses a bounded-staleness telemetry snapshot per pool.
- If telemetry is newer than the max staleness threshold, BAC evaluates against that snapshot.
- If telemetry is stale but within the “soft stale” window, BAC applies conservative smoothing (reduces computed headroom) and may admit only higher-priority tiers.
- If telemetry exceeds hard stale threshold, BAC returns ERROR_TELEMETRY_STALE with retry guidance.

See: “Failure modes” and the telemetry lag mitigation hotfix notes in the fairness ADR.

## Dependencies
### Policy/config
- Control Plane Tenant Config Service (source of truth)
  - Reads tenant burst enablement and limits
  - Versioned config with validation
- Policy Engine
  - Evaluates guardrail rules (max burst ratio, cooldown policy, fleet safety rules)
  - Provides deterministic evaluation (policy_hash returned in responses)

### Fleet telemetry
- FleetPulse (fleet telemetry aggregator; internal)
  - Inputs: pool capacity, pool utilization, saturation, admission queue depth (if any), health signals
  - Outputs: per-pool snapshot with timestamps

### State store
- Redis (regional)
  - Per-tenant token bucket state (remaining units, refill time)
  - Cooldown state
  - Small, bounded cardinality by (tenant_id, pool_id)

### Observability
- Prometheus metrics via /metrics
- OpenTelemetry tracing (propagates request_id)
- Structured logs (redacted) to centralized log pipeline

### Billing/metering
- Metering Exporter (async)
  - Emits admission decisions with debug_ref, tenant_id, pool_id, decision_ttl
  - Downstream joins with runtime burst usage signals to prevent double-counting on retries

### Security/audit
- Audit logging service for:
  - Access to /v1/burst/explain
  - Admin overrides (if enabled in emergency mode)
- RBAC service for privileged endpoints

## Data model (logical)
BAC interacts with the following logical entities. (Storage varies by entity.)

1) TenantBurstPolicy (authoritative; Control Plane DB)
- tenant_id
- enabled: true/false
- max_burst_ratio: e.g., 1.5x baseline
- burst_cap_units_per_min: per-tenant cap in cost units
- cooldown_seconds: e.g., 300
- priority_tier: standard/silver/gold
- denial_mode: immediate_deny | allow_queue (future)
- allowlist_regions_hw: optional overrides

2) PoolStateSnapshot (authoritative; FleetPulse)
- pool_id
- total_capacity_units
- reserved_safety_margin_units
- current_in_use_units
- computed_headroom_units
- saturation_pct
- snapshot_ts_ms

3) TenantBucketState (ephemeral; Redis)
- key: (tenant_id, pool_id)
- remaining_units
- last_refill_ts_ms
- cooldown_until_ts_ms

4) DecisionRecord (ephemeral; logs/metrics; optional short-lived cache)
- debug_ref
- request_id
- decision + reason_code
- policy_hash
- telemetry_snapshot_ts_ms
- estimated_cost_units

Retention:
- Logs: per platform standard
- Explain lookups: best-effort (typically 24–72 hours, depending on log retention and debug index)

## Guardrail evaluation order (current)
BAC evaluates in a strict order to keep outcomes explainable and predictable:
1) Rollout gates (region/hardware class allow-list)
2) Tenant burst enabled check
3) Telemetry validity (hard stale cutoff)
4) Global fleet safety cap
5) Pool headroom check (local saturation)
6) Per-tenant hard caps (max ratio and cap units)
7) Cooldown enforcement
8) Fairness/anti-starvation enforcement (tier weights + smoothing)

Important: Even for high priority tiers, global fleet safety and hard stale telemetry gates are not bypassable.

## Performance and scaling targets
BAC is on the request path only when baseline is exceeded; it must be fast.

Targets:
- p50 latency: < 5ms
- p99 latency: < 20ms
- Availability: 99.95% (regional)
- Error rate (5xx + ERROR_INTERNAL): < 0.1%

Operational notes:
- BAC should degrade safely: when in doubt, deny with an explainable reason rather than admitting and risking fleet impact.
- BAC uses aggressive timeouts to policy/telemetry dependencies; beyond the allowed stale window it should return ERROR_POLICY_UNAVAILABLE or ERROR_TELEMETRY_STALE.

## Failure modes and expected behavior
1) Policy engine unavailable
- BAC uses last known-good policy snapshot for up to the configured stale window.
- After stale window: deny with ERROR_POLICY_UNAVAILABLE.

2) Fleet telemetry lag / stale snapshots
- Within soft stale window: conservative headroom, may deny more aggressively.
- Beyond hard cutoff: ERROR_TELEMETRY_STALE.

3) Redis unavailable
- BAC cannot enforce fairness/cooldowns reliably.
- Behavior: fail closed for burst admission (DENY with ERROR_INTERNAL), emit high-severity alert.

4) Pool overload (saturation)
- DENIED_POOL_SATURATED with retry_after_ms based on pool refill estimate.
- If sustained: global circuit breaker may engage (DENIED_FLEET_SAFETY_CAP).

5) Retry storms
- Callers must use idempotency (request_id) and follow retry_after_ms.
- Metering pipeline uses idempotency keys to prevent double-counting.

## Feature flags and emergency controls
BAC supports the following kill switches (controlled via Control Plane config, audited):
- burst_global_disable: disables all burst admissions (still returns reasons).
- burst_disable_by_region_hw: disables specific pools.
- burst_disable_by_tenant: tenant-level disable.
- fleet_safety_cap_override: emergency-only; requires SRE + executive approval; all uses audited.

## Metrics (selected)
BAC emits Prometheus metrics with controlled cardinality (tenant_id is NOT a label).

Counters:
- bac_admit_total{pool_id, priority_tier}
- bac_deny_total{pool_id, reason_code, priority_tier}
- bac_error_total{pool_id, error_type}

Gauges:
- bac_pool_headroom_units{pool_id}
- bac_pool_saturation_pct{pool_id}
- bac_telemetry_staleness_ms{pool_id}

Histograms:
- bac_request_latency_ms{endpoint}

Dashboards: see “Burst Capacity Dashboards” page.

## Operational runbooks
- Overload mitigation: adjust caps, engage circuit breaker, communicate with CS/Support
  - See: Burst Pool Overload Mitigation Runbook
- Denials higher than expected: validate tenant config, telemetry staleness, pool headroom
- Explain endpoint usage: how to interpret reason codes and guardrail evaluation

## Change management
- Any change to denial codes or guardrail semantics requires:
  - Review by Smart Routing (Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon)
  - Review by SRE (Sean Gallagher)
  - Update to customer-facing docs and Support playbook
- Any change affecting billing/metering correlation requires Finance/Billing sign-off.

## Appendix: Integration guidance for Router/Gateway
Recommended caller behavior:
- Only call BAC when baseline is exceeded or predicted to exceed within the request window.
- Use a stable request_id for retries.
- Respect retry_after_ms; jitter retries.
- Surface reason_code to customer-visible reporting where safe.
- For DENIED_POOL_SATURATED / DENIED_FLEET_SAFETY_CAP:
  - Prefer backpressure (429) and/or queue with bounded waiting.
  - Avoid uncontrolled retries.

For customer comms:
- “Burst is best-effort above baseline and subject to shared-pool availability and fleet safety; baseline SLAs remain protected.”

title: Multi-signal Priority Lattice and Decision Runbook
preview:
Summary:

This runbook defines a multi-signal prioritization lattice used by platform operators when multiple alerts and business signals fire simultaneously. The lattice converts heterogeneous signals (latency, token-errors, quality regressions, customer impact, payment deltas, and telemetry noise) into a ranked action queue with clear ownership and operator instructions. The goal is to: reduce time-to-mitigation for high-impact events, limit unnecessary escalations, and preserve customer SLAs.

When to use:
- Multi-region or multi-model incidents where more than two distinct alert streams are active concurrently.
- When the root cause is unclear and immediate mitigation decisions are required.
- During Brownouts (degraded SLOs in single region) or Multi-tenant contention events.

Key definitions:
- Priority score: a weighted integer (0-100) combining signals in the table below.
- Lattice tiers: Critical (80+), High (60-79), Medium (40-59), Low (0-39).
- Action bucket: the set of operator interventions available for each tier (see Actions section).

Signals and weightings (default canonical weights):
| Signal                    | Source (metric/event)                        | Weight |
|---------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|--------|
| Customer-facing errors    | 5xx/error-rate (per-tenant)                   | 30     |
| Latency tail              | p95 latency on route/model (region)           | 20     |
| Token surge               | tokens/s exceed baseline by > 3x              | 10     |
| Quality regression        | eval alert / regression-detector             | 15     |
| Payment/quotas impact     | quota-breach or billing alerts                | 10     |
| Capacity availability     | GPU pool utilization / preemption signal      | 8      |
| External dependency error | third-party model / storage error            | 7      |

Notes on weighting: weights are tuned by the Platform SRE guild; use the incident runbook config utility to override for customer-specific dedicated pools. The lattice assumes higher weight for direct customer error signals.

Priority lattice calculation (operator view):
1. Gather active signals for the time window t0 - t0-5m.
2. Normalize each signal to a 0-100 subscore (examples below).
3. Multiply subscores by canonical weights and sum to compute Priority score.
4. Map Priority score to Lattice tier.

Normalization examples (quick heuristics):
- Customer error-rate (per-tenant): subscore = min(100, (error_rate_pct / 2) * 100). (i.e., 2% errors -> 100)
- p95 latency: subscore = clamp(0, 100, (p95_ms - baseline_ms) / baseline_ms * 100). Baseline_ms pulled from route SLI table.
- Token surge: subscore = min(100, ((tokens/s / baseline_tokens) - 1) * 50).

Investigative triage checklist (first 10 minutes):
1. Identify the tenant(s) and region(s) affected. (Command: `rbctl incidents list --active --since 10m`)
2. Check topology map for impacted model variants and routing policies. (Dashboard: Platform -> Model Routing Map)
3. Correlate traces: run `jaeger query --service=runtime-serving --tag region=<r> --duration=10m` to find tail latencies and error traces.
4. Check GPU pool health: `kubectl get nodes -l gpu=true -o wide` and `prometheus query: node_gpu_utilization{pool="<pool>"}`.
5. Confirm whether a model-variant-fallback occurred in the last 15 minutes (`/api/audit/model-fallbacks` events).

Tier-to-action mapping (decision lattice):
- Critical (80+): immediate mitigation, page oncall, run emergency fallback policy, enable cross-region redirect, consider traffic draining, and notify Customer Success for top 10 tenants.
- High (60-79): prioritize mitigations that reduce customer-facing errors and latency, consider model swap to smaller variant, scale dedicated pool if available, open issue ticket.
- Medium (40-59): trigger targeted throttling or tenant soft-quota, schedule controlled rollbacks or config changes during 30-minute window.
- Low (0-39): monitor and collect telemetry, treat as operational noise, add to 24h correlation board if recurring.

Operator action recipes (examples):
1. Emergency model fallback (Critical):
   - Action: Route traffic to verified fallback model in same family with lower latency profile.
   - CLI: `rbctl route set --tenant <T> --route <R> --model <fallback-model:v2> --immediate`
   - Safety checks: ensure fallback model has pass/fail evaluation for tenant prompt-set (dashboard shows green).
2. Soft-quota for noisy tenant (High/Medium):
   - Action: Apply transient throttle to tenant tokens/s with 5-minute backoff window.
   - CLI: `rbctl quota patch --tenant <T> --tokens-per-sec 0.25 --ttl 300s --reason "auto-throttle"`
3. Cross-region redirect (Critical):
   - Action: Re-route eligible requests to healthy region with matching model variant.
   - Steps: Update routing policy: `rbctl routing update --policy <policy-id> --add-fallback-region <region>` then validate traffic shift histogram.

Dashboard and alert cookbook (SLO, alert thresholds and playbook hooks):
- SLOs used by lattice:
  - p95 inference latency (hosted API): 150ms (SLO target 99.95%)
  - customer-visible error rate: 0.5% (SLO target 99.9% success)
  - model-quality-regression alert: > 2 failing prompts in regression-suite within 1 hour

- Representative alert thresholds (examples):
  - platform.inference.errors.rate{region,tenant} > 0.5% over 2m -> generates "tenant-error-spike" (weight 30)
  - platform.inference.p95{route,region} > 250ms over 5m -> generates "p95-tail-exceeded" (weight 20)
  - eval.regression.alert{model} severity=high -> generates "quality-regression" (weight 15)

Alert enrichment fields to surface on the incident page (required):
- tenant id(s), region, affected model variants, recent model-fallback events, top 5 trace ids, GPU pool id, estimated customer impact (tenants impacted, percent of traffic).

Escalation matrix and contacts:
- First response: platform-operator oncall (primary) -- paging policy 1 (15m SLA for acknowledgement).
- If Critical and not acknowledged in 15m: page Platform SRE lead and Eng Platform Director.
- For regressions involving model output quality: CC Applied ML lead and Solutions Engineer owning tenant.

Command cheat-sheet (useful quick commands):
- List active lattice events: `rbctl lattice events list --since 10m`
- Compute priority score locally: `python tools/compute_lattice_score.py --events events.json`
- Force-enable fallback for tenant: `rbctl route set --tenant <T> --force-fallback true`

Recovery and rollback guidance:
- When a mitigation reduces Priority score into a lower tier, hold mitigation for 30 minutes and monitor telemetry for regression before rolling back to original configs.
- For model swaps: run synthetic prompt-set validation (CLI: `python tools/synth_validate.py --tenant <T> --model <candidate>`) and ensure pass rate >= 95% before permanent rollout.

Post-incident actions (within 48 hours):
1. Create incident ticket with lattice event id and attach the prioritization calculation output.
2. Run root-cause correlation workbook and populate the Postmortem template fields: timeline, actions taken, data used in decision, why the lattice produced the priority it did.
3. If lattice weights produced an obviously incorrect decision, submit a lattice-tuning PR to `platform-sre/lattice-config` and include regression tests.

Maintenance and tuning guidance:
- Quarterly review cadence: Platform SRE guild runs a lattice calibration session each quarter to replay top 10 multi-signal incidents and adjust canonical weights.
- Canary: changes to lattice config must be applied to a non-prod environment for 7 days and evaluated on synthetic multi-signal scenarios.

Ownership and contact list:
- Lattice config repo: platform-sre/lattice-config (owner: Priya Natarajan)
- Oncall rotation: eng-platform -> platform-operator
- Escalation leads: Platform SRE (pager), Applied ML oncall for quality regressions

Appendix A — Example priority calculation (short):
- Event: Tenant T1 in us-west triggers: 1. tenant errors 1.2% (subscore=60), 2. p95 latency 320ms baseline 120ms (subscore ~167 -> clamp 100), 3. token surge 2.1x (subscore ~55).
- Weighted sum = 60*30 + 100*20 + 55*10 = 1800 + 2000 + 550 = 4350, normalized (divide by max possible 100*(sum weights=100)) = 43.5 -> Priority score 44 -> Lattice tier = Medium.

Appendix B — Lattice testing checklist (for changes):
- Add unit tests for normalization functions.
- Add 5 synthetic multi-signal scenarios to CI.
- Run A/B simulation comparing old vs new config for 72h on production-snapshot traces.

Revision history:
- 2024-11-04 Priya Natarajan: Created initial runbook and canonical weights.
- 2025-03-15 Liam Chen: Updated action recipes for cross-region redirect automations.
- 2025-06-18 Marina Alvarez: Added synthetic validation CLI checks and post-incident actions.

2. Normalize each signal to a 0-100 subscore (examples below).
3. Multiply subscores by canonical weights and sum to compute Priority score.
4. Map Priority score to Lattice tier.

Normalization examples (quick heuristics):
- Customer error-rate (per-tenant): subscore = min(100, (error_rate_pct / 2) * 100). (i.e., 2% errors -> 100)
- p95 latency: subscore = clamp(0, 100, (p95_ms - baseline_ms) / baseline_ms * 100). Baseline_ms pulled from route SLI table.
- Token surge: subscore = min(100, ((tokens/s / baseline_tokens) - 1) * 50).

Investigative triage checklist (first 10 minutes):
1. Identify the tenant(s) and region(s) affected. (Command: `rbctl incidents list --active --since 10m`)
2. Check topology map for impacted model variants and routing policies. (Dashboard: Platform -> Model Routing Map)
3. Correlate traces: run `jaeger query --service=runtime-serving --tag region=<r> --duration=10m` to find tail latencies and error traces.
4. Check GPU pool health: `kubectl get nodes -l gpu=true -o wide` and `prometheus query: node_gpu_utilization{pool="<pool>"}`.
5. Confirm whether a model-variant-fallback occurred in the last 15 minutes (`/api/audit/model-fallbacks` events).

Tier-to-action mapping (decision lattice):
- Critical (80+): immediate mitigation, page oncall, run emergency fallback policy, enable cross-region redirect, consider traffic draining, and notify Customer Success for top 10 tenants.
- High (60-79): prioritize mitigations that reduce customer-facing errors and latency, consider model swap to smaller variant, scale dedicated pool if available, open issue ticket.
- Medium (40-59): trigger targeted throttling or tenant soft-quota, schedule controlled rollbacks or config changes during 30-minute window.
- Low (0-39): monitor and collect telemetry, treat as operational noise, add to 24h correlation board if recurring.

Operator action recipes (examples):
1. Emergency model fallback (Critical):
   - Action: Route traffic to verified fallback model in same family with lower latency profile.
   - CLI: `rbctl route set --tenant <T> --route <R> --model <fallback-model:v2> --immediate`
   - Safety checks: ensure fallback model has pass/fail evaluation for tenant prompt-set (dashboard shows green).
2. Soft-quota for noisy tenant (High/Medium):
   - Action: Apply transient throttle to tenant tokens/s with 5-minute backoff window.
   - CLI: `rbctl quota patch --tenant <T> --tokens-per-sec 0.25 --ttl 300s --reason "auto-throttle"`
3. Cross-region redirect (Critical):
   - Action: Re-route eligible requests to healthy region with matching model variant.
   - Steps: Update routing policy: `rbctl routing update --policy <policy-id> --add-fallback-region <region>` then validate traffic shift histogram.

Dashboard and alert cookbook (SLO, alert thresholds and playbook hooks):
- SLOs used by lattice:
  - p95 inference latency (hosted API): 150ms (SLO target 99.95%)
  - customer-visible error rate: 0.5% (SLO target 99.9% success)
  - model-quality-regression alert: > 2 failing prompts in regression-suite within 1 hour

- Representative alert thresholds (examples):
  - platform.inference.errors.rate{region,tenant} > 0.5% over 2m -> generates "tenant-error-spike" (weight 30)
  - platform.inference.p95{route,region} > 250ms over 5m -> generates "p95-tail-exceeded" (weight 20)
  - eval.regression.alert{model} severity=high -> generates "quality-regression" (weight 15)

Alert enrichment fields to surface on the incident page (required):
- tenant id(s), region, affected model variants, recent model-fallback events, top 5 trace ids, GPU pool id, estimated customer impact (tenants impacted, percent of traffic).

Escalation matrix and contacts:
- First response: platform-operator oncall (primary) -- paging policy 1 (15m SLA for acknowledgement).
- If Critical and not acknowledged in 15m: page Platform SRE lead and Eng Platform Director.
- For regressions involving model output quality: CC Applied ML lead and Solutions Engineer owning tenant.

Command cheat-sheet (useful quick commands):
- List active lattice events: `rbctl lattice events list --since 10m`
- Compute priority score locally: `python tools/compute_lattice_score.py --events events.json`
- Force-enable fallback for tenant: `rbctl route set --tenant <T> --force-fallback true`

Recovery and rollback guidance:
- When a mitigation reduces Priority score into a lower tier, hold mitigation for 30 minutes and monitor telemetry for regression before rolling back to original configs.
- For model swaps: run synthetic prompt-set validation (CLI: `python tools/synth_validate.py --tenant <T> --model <candidate>`) and ensure pass rate >= 95% before permanent rollout.

Post-incident actions (within 48 hours):
1. Create incident ticket with lattice event id and attach the prioritization calculation output.
2. Run root-cause correlation workbook and populate the Postmortem template fields: timeline, actions taken, data used in decision, why the lattice produced the priority it did.
3. If lattice weights produced an obviously incorrect decision, submit a lattice-tuning PR to `platform-sre/lattice-config` and include regression tests.

Maintenance and tuning guidance:
- Quarterly review cadence: Platform SRE guild runs a lattice calibration session each quarter to replay top 10 multi-signal incidents and adjust canonical weights.
- Canary: changes to lattice config must be applied to a non-prod environment for 7 days and evaluated on synthetic multi-signal scenarios.

Ownership and contact list:
- Lattice config repo: platform-sre/lattice-config (owner: Priya Natarajan)
- Oncall rotation: eng-platform -> platform-operator
- Escalation leads: Platform SRE (pager), Applied ML oncall for quality regressions

Appendix A — Example priority calculation (short):
- Event: Tenant T1 in us-west triggers: 1. tenant errors 1.2% (subscore=60), 2. p95 latency 320ms baseline 120ms (subscore ~167 -> clamp 100), 3. token surge 2.1x (subscore ~55).
- Weighted sum = 60*30 + 100*20 + 55*10 = 1800 + 2000 + 550 = 4350, normalized (divide by max possible 100*(sum weights=100)) = 43.5 -> Priority score 44 -> Lattice tier = Medium.

Appendix B — Lattice testing checklist (for changes):
- Add unit tests for normalization functions.
- Add 5 synthetic multi-signal scenarios to CI.
- Run A/B simulation comparing old vs new config for 72h on production-snapshot traces.

Revision history:
- 2024-11-04 Priya Natarajan: Created initial runbook and canonical weights.
- 2025-03-15 Liam Chen: Updated action recipes for cross-region redirect automations.
- 2025-06-18 Marina Alvarez: Added synthetic validation CLI checks and post-incident actions.

title: Operator signal fatigue and automation adoption retrospective
preview:
Summary
-------
This retrospective reviews a 5-month program (Oct 2024 — Feb 2025) to reduce operator signal fatigue across the platform operator surface. The initiative combined (1) signal compaction rules, (2) lightweight automation runbooks, and (3) a phased alert rationalization. Goals: reduce actionable alert noise by 60%, shorten average triage time by 35%, and increase operator satisfaction (measured via pulse survey) by 20%.

Context and motivation
----------------------
As our multitenant LLM inference platform scaled in 2024, the operator team reported increasing cognitive load: frequent low-value alerts, repeated manual mitigations for common prefixes/KV-cache issues, and friction in following complex manual runbooks. This created slow incident response and inconsistent mitigations. The program was funded as a cross-functional engineering + platform initiative to: 
- Prioritize high-fidelity signals over high-volume signals.
- Replace repetitive manual steps with validated automation.
- Provide clear ownerable playbooks and SLO-linked alert thresholds.

Timeline and milestones
-----------------------
1. Oct 2024 — Nov 2024: Signal inventory and heatmap (what fires, frequency, mean time to ack).
2. Dec 2024: Prototype compaction rules + operator automation sandbox.
3. Jan 2025: Canary rollout of reduced-alert profile for non-critical regions.
4. Feb 2025: Org-wide rollout, operator training, and pulse survey.

Observed metrics (pre and post)
------------------------------
| Metric | Baseline (Sep 2024) | Post-rollout (Mar 2025) | Delta |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Alerts/day (global operator queue) | 1,320 | 410 | -69% |
| Unique actionable alerts/day | 320 | 125 | -61% |
| Median time-to-ack (minutes) | 14.2 | 9.1 | -36% |
| Operator pulse (satisfaction index 0-100) | 48 | 62 | +29% |
| Repeat manual mitigations/week | 45 | 9 | -80% |

Key observations and root causes
--------------------------------
- Signal duplication: multiple downstream services emitted distinct alerts for the same underlying KV-prefix cache degradation, creating correlated noise.
- Low signal fidelity: several alerts lacked deterministic detection criteria (thresholds derived heuristically), resulting in frequent false positives for low-impact transient conditions.
- Manual choreography with fragile scripts: runbooks required manual sequence of steps across services that often failed if timing varied.
- Operator escalation ambiguity: ownership of noisy alerts was unclear leading to duplicated work and inconsistent mitigation choices.

Actions taken (technical and process)
-------------------------------------
1) Signal compaction layer (implementation)
- Implemented a compaction service upstream of the global operator queue. The compactor groups correlated alerts within a 120s window by fingerprint (tenant, model_id, prefix_shard, error_class) and emits a single consolidated signal with aggregated context.
- Compaction policy examples: 
  - KV-prefix-prefetch: combine "prefetch-miss" + "prefetch-latency" into a single "kv-prefetch-degradation" alert.
  - Thermal/host-level: coalesce host-level metrics into a single host-health signal if > 3 related alerts fire.

2) Fidelity-first alert thresholds
- Converted noisy heuristic rules into two-tier thresholds:
  - warning: 75th percentile for 5m window (no paging)
  - page: 95th percentile for sustained 10m window (page on-call)
- Added cardinality caps to prevent tenant noise blasts from paging (quotas per-minute).

3) Automation runbooks and playbooks
- Built vendor-agnostic automation tasks for common mitigations (cache drain, adapter restart, dynamic prefetch boost). Each task is idempotent and runs with a dry-run first in canary.
- Exposed automation as CLI + API for operators and automated-runbook triggers. Example CLI: 
  # Dry run mitigation for prefix shard 17
  rbox runbook execute --dry-run --name prefix-prefetch-boost --shard 17 --reason "auto-diagnose"

4) Ownership model and alert routing
- Adopted a three-layer owner model: "signal owner" (service that detects), "operator owner" (primary oncall rotation), "stakeholder owner" (team responsible for long-term fixes).
- Updated routing tables so compaction service attaches ‘primary_op_owner’ tag and forwards to the correct queue.

5) Training and operator playbook grooming
- Ran 4 hands-on workshops for operators covering the new compaction semantics, automation safety, and rollback patterns.
- Rewrote 18 runbooks to the new template (see Runbook change list below).

Runbook change list
--------------------
- prefix-prefetch-boost (v2) — now idempotent, supports dry-run and automated rollback.
- host-thermal-evacuate (v1) — combined step-runner and graceful preempt hook.
- tiered-fallback-route (v1) — updated to rely on compaction signals.
(Complete list and diffs are in the Runbook repo: /git/platform/runbooks/retcon-2025)

Quantitative outcomes and SLO implications
-----------------------------------------
- Noise reduction enabled faster triage: median time-to-resolve for actionable incidents fell from 50m to 32m for grouped incidents.
- Fewer pages preserved on-call budgets and allowed oncall to focus on higher-severity outages.
- SLOs: we tightened the service-level alert SLI to ensure compaction doesn’t mask true customer-impacting errors. Compaction only reduces paging for alerts where the underlying user-visible error rate was < 0.1% for the affected tenant/model.

Implementation details (configs and examples)
---------------------------------------------
- Compaction config (YAML excerpt):
  compaction_window_seconds: 120
  fingerprint_keys: [tenant_id, model_version, prefix_shard, error_class]
  aggregation_fields: [count, affected_tenants, top-3-error-messages]
  paging_thresholds: 
    warning_pctile: 75
    page_pctile: 95
  cardinality_cap_per_minute: 5

- Alert routing CLI example:
  # view routing for signal 'kv-prefetch-degradation'
  rbox signal route get --signal kv-prefetch-degradation --region us-west-2

- Automation safety pattern (required for all tasks):
  1) dry-run -> 2) operator confirmation (unless runbook flagged 'auto-approved') -> 3) execute -> 4) post-check and auto-rollback on failure.

What didn't work / surprises
---------------------------
- Overcompaction risk: initial compaction window (300s) suppressed distinct sequential incidents; reduced window to 120s and added fingerprinting to avoid masking.
- Tooling adoption lag: several operator teams preferred shell scripts; we added a thin compatibility shim to wrap legacy scripts safely.
- Measurement artifacts: early reductions in alerts/day were partly influenced by a concurrent throttling experiment — we gated attribution to verify real operator impact.

Open action items and follow-ups (owners and due dates)
------------------------------------------------------
- [ACTIONS-101] Migrate remaining 8 legacy runbooks to new template (owner: Mateo Alvarez, due: 2025-07-01)
- [ACTIONS-102] Add canned dashboards to show compaction attribution per-tenant (owner: Priya Nair, due: 2025-06-30)
- [ACTIONS-103] Harden compaction fingerprinting logic (owner: Jonah Reed, due: 2025-08-15)
- [ACTIONS-104] Run a 6-month regression check to ensure no customer-impact masking (owner: Eng-SRE, due: 2025-10-01)

Lessons learned and recommendations
----------------------------------
1. Measurement before mitigation: invest in signal heatmaps and owner-driven threshold experiments before wide rollouts.
2. Default to high-fidelity, low-volume signals for paging; funnel lower-fidelity conditions into operator queues or dashboards.
3. Make automation visible: operators must be able to dry-run easily and inspect previous auto-mitigations.
4. Rapid iteration windows: short canary windows with good attribution guard against regressions.
5. Social adoption matters: change management (workshops, compatibility shims) was crucial to adoption.

Appendix: operator pulse and survey instrument (short form)
--------------------------------------------------------
- NPS-style question: "How confident do you feel handling platform incidents today?" (0-10)
- Workload questions: "How often did you receive duplicate alerts this week?" (Never / Rare / Weekly / Daily)
- Automation trust gauge: "Would you opt into automatic mitigations for non-critical signals?" (Yes / No / Depends)

Contacts and owners
-------------------
- Document owner: Lena Thompson (Platform Observability) — lena@redwood.ai
- Program leads: Mateo Alvarez (operator flow), Priya Nair (compaction service), Jonah Reed (automation)
- Oncall reference: see /confluence/eng-platform/team-wiki/platform-oncall-quick-reference-shell-helpers-and-scripts-2026.json

Related artifacts and repositories
----------------------------------
- Runbooks repo: git@internal:redwood/platform/runbooks.git (branch: compaction-rollout-2025)
- Compaction service: /git/platform/observability/compactor (docker image: compactor:2025.02.1)
- Dashboards: grafana/org/platform/compaction-attribution

Acknowledgements
------------------
Thanks to all operators who participated in workshops and the engineering teams who contributed instrumentation and automation libraries.
